3. **Resin Impregnation (Wetting)** — flowing liquid resin over fibres to fill the inter-tow voids.
4. **Consolidation** — removing trapped air (rolling, vacuum, or pressure) so the laminate is dense and void-free.
5. **Curing & Demoulding** — exothermic cross-linking of the thermoset, followed by part extraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step (KTU High-Yield Logic)**
> - *Why release agent?* The cured thermoset will chemically bond to the mould surface, making demoulding impossible.
> - *Why wet-out?* Dry fibres carry no load because stresses cannot be transferred through air gaps.
> - *Why consolidate?* Voids reduce strength by acting as crack-initiation sites (typical design limit: **< 2% void content**).
> - *Why post-cure?* Ambient curing leaves the resin at ~70–85% cross-link density; post-cure at **80 °C for 4–6 hours** raises this to >95%.

## 2.2 Classification of FRP Processing Methods

FRP processes are classified by **mould openness** and **mechanization level**:

| Category | Process | Mould Type | Skill Level | Typical Boat Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Open Mould (Contact)** | Hand Lay-up | Single open mould | Low | Small fishing boats, canoes |
| **Open Mould (Contact)** | Spray Lay-up | Single open mould | Low | Hulls, decks, large panels |
| **Closed Mould (Low Pressure)** | Resin Transfer Molding (RTM) | Two matched moulds | Medium | Series boat components |
| **Closed Mould (Vacuum)** | VARTM / Vacuum Infusion | Single mould + vacuum bag | Medium-High | High-quality hulls |
| **Closed Mould (Pressure)** | Compression Molding | Matched heated moulds | High | Small repetitive parts |
| **Continuous (Filament-Based)** | Filament Winding | Rotating mandrel | High | Pressure hulls, masts, tanks |
| **Continuous (Profile-Based)** | Pultrusion | Heated die | High | Stiffeners, gratings, rails |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table lists every key equation, definition, and unit a KTU student must memorise for this module. Avoid the pipe `|` character in formulas — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f = \dfrac{v_f}{v_f + v_m}$ | **Fibre volume fraction** of the laminate | dimensionless |
| 2 | $W_f = \dfrac{w_f}{w_f + w_m}$ | **Fibre weight fraction** (from burn-out test) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + (1-W_f)/\rho_m}$ | Conversion of weight fraction to volume fraction | dimensionless |
| 4 | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$ | **Rule of mixtures (longitudinal modulus)** | GPa |
| 5 | $E_c = \dfrac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f (1 - V_f)}$ | **Inverse rule of mixtures (transverse modulus)** | GPa |
| 6 | $P_c = P_f V_f + P_m (1 - V_f)$ | **Rule of mixtures for density** | g/cm³ |
| 7 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f)$ | **Tensile strength (longitudinal, iso-strain)** | MPa |
| 8 | $\dfrac{1}{\sigma_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{\sigma_f} + \dfrac{1 - V_f}{\sigma_m}$ | **Tensile strength (transverse, iso-stress)** | MPa |
| 9 | $V_v = \dfrac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{exp}}{\rho_{th}} \times 100$ | **Void content** | % |
| 10 | $t_{cure} = \dfrac{T_{gel}}{k \cdot (T_{cure} - T_{amb})}$ | Approximate cure time scaling (Arrhenius-like) | minutes |
| 11 | $L_{laminate} = n \cdot t_{ply}$ | Laminated plate thickness from ply count | mm |
| 12 | $A_{resin} = n \cdot w \cdot \ell \cdot t_{ply} \cdot (1 - W_f) \cdot \rho_m$ | Resin required per ply (per part) | kg |

**Engineering Utility**: These equations allow the KTU boat designer to back-calculate the number of fabric plies needed to meet a target hull thickness, predict stiffness, and verify quality (low void content) after manufacturing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Rule of Mixtures for Composite Density (Used in FRP Process Control)

This derivation is the **analytical backbone** of FRP process engineering, allowing a manufacturer to verify whether the laid-up laminate matches the design specification.

### Step 1 — Define the Total Volume of the Composite

The composite is composed of two phases: fibre phase and matrix phase. The total volume is the sum of both.

$$
V_c = V_f + V_m
$$

Where:
- $V_c$ = total volume of composite
- $V_f$ = volume of fibres
- $V_m$ = volume of matrix (resin)

### Step 2 — Apply the Definition of Mass Conservation

The total mass equals the sum of fibre and matrix masses.

$$
M_c = M_f + M_m
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Density Relationship for Each Phase

Since $\rho = \dfrac{M}{V}$, we can rewrite each mass term:

$$
\rho_c \cdot V_c = \rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m
$$

### Step 4 — Divide Both Sides by the Total Composite Volume

Dividing through by $V_c$ yields an expression for the composite density:

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f \cdot \dfrac{V_f}{V_c} + \rho_m \cdot \dfrac{V_m}{V_c}
$$

### Step 5 — Recognise the Volume Fractions

By definition, $\dfrac{V_f}{V_c} = V_f$ (the fibre volume fraction) and $\dfrac{V_m}{V_c} = 1 - V_f$. Substituting:

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot (1 - V_f)
$$

This is the **Rule of Mixtures for Density**, an identity that any KTU student can use to determine if a moulded FRP part meets its design lay-up.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-World Application (Hull Weight Estimation)**
> For a 4 m fishing-boat hull panel with design $V_f = 0.35$, using E-glass ($\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³) and polyester ($\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³):
>
> $$\rho_c = (2.55)(0.35) + (1.20)(0.65) = 0.8925 + 0.78 = 1.67 \text{ g/cm}^3$$
>
> This density (≈ **1.67 g/cm³**) is typical for a marine FRP laminate and is used to compute the hull's displacement and stability.

## 3.2 Derivation — Conversion from Weight Fraction to Volume Fraction

In the boat-building workshop, the engineer usually measures **weight fraction** ($W_f$) from a burn-out test, but the design rules require **volume fraction** ($V_f$).

### Step 1 — Express Volume Fractions in Terms of Densities

By definition:

$$
V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + (1 - W_f) / \rho_m}
$$

### Step 2 — Derive from First Principles

Starting from the mass fraction:

$$
W_f = \dfrac{M_f}{M_c} = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c V_c} = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c}
$$

Substituting the rule-of-mixtures density $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$:

$$
W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve Algebraically for $V_f$

Rearranging:

$$
V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + (1 - W_f)/\rho_m}
$$

**Verification**: If a laminate has $W_f = 0.50$ with E-glass/polyester:

$$
V_f = \dfrac{0.50 / 2.55}{0.50/2.55 + 0.50/1.20} = \dfrac{0.1961}{0.1961 + 0.4167} = 0.32
$$

So 50% by **weight** corresponds to only 32% by **volume** — fibres are heavier per unit volume than resin, so a smaller fibre volume yields the same weight contribution. This is a classic KTU pitfall.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Ply Count for a Boat-Hull Laminate

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (Typical KTU Numerical):**
> Design a 6 mm thick boat-hull laminate using 600 g/m² chopped strand mat (CSM) with polyester resin. The resin-to-glass ratio after wet-out is **2.5:1 by weight**. Given ply thickness $t_{ply} \approx 0.8$ mm per CSM layer (after consolidation), determine the number of plies required.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Resin-to-Glass Ratio**

$$
\dfrac{M_{resin}}{M_{glass}} = 2.5 \implies W_f = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2.5} = \dfrac{1}{3.5} = 0.286
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Volume Fraction**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{0.286 / 2.55}{0.286/2.55 + 0.714/1.20} = \dfrac{0.1122}{0.1122 + 0.5950} = 0.159
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Number of Plies**

$$
n = \dfrac{t_{laminate}}{t_{ply}} = \dfrac{6.0 \text{ mm}}{0.8 \text{ mm}} = 7.5 \implies n = 8 \text{ plies (rounded up)}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify Resin Quantity Required per Hull Panel (Area = 10 m²)**

$$
M_{resin} = 8 \times 10 \text{ m}^2 \times 0.600 \text{ kg/m}^2 \times 2.5 = 120 \text{ kg resin}
$$

**Answer**: **8 plies of 600 g/m² CSM** are needed, consuming **~120 kg of polyester resin** for a 10 m² hull panel.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Process for Boat Components

| Boat Component | Design Priority | Recommended FRP Process | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small canoe (< 4 m) | Cost, low tooling | Hand lay-up | Single mould, no capital cost |
| Fishing trawler hull | Strength, repeatability | Vacuum infusion (VARTM) | Low void content, thick laminates |
| Racing yacht hull | Stiffness, light weight | Prepreg + oven cure | High $V_f$, controlled cure |
| Mast / tube | Unidirectional strength | Pultrusion | Continuous fibre alignment |
| Pressure pipe / tank | Hoop strength | Filament winding | Winding angles optimise load path |
| Small cleat / block | Volume production | Compression molding | Cycle time < 2 min |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Classification of FRP Processing Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FRP Processing Methods] --> B[Open Mould Processes]
    A --> C[Closed Mould Processes]
    A --> D[Continuous Processes]

    B --> B1[Hand Lay-up]
    B --> B2[Spray Lay-up]

    C --> C1[Resin Transfer Molding RTM]
    C --> C2[Vacuum Assisted RTM VARTM]
    C --> C3[Compression Molding]
    C --> C4[Autoclave Molding]

    D --> D1[Filament Winding]
    D --> D2[Pultrusion]

    B1 --> E1[Boat hulls, small craft]
    B2 --> E2[Deck panels, large surfaces]
    C2 --> E3[High quality marine hulls]
    C4 --> E4[Aerospace / racing yachts]
    D1 --> E5[Pressure vessels, masts]
    D2 --> E6[Stiffeners, gratings]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hand Lay-up Process Sequence (Most Common in Boat Building)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Mould Cleaning] --> S2[Apply Release Agent]
    S2 --> S3[Spray Gelcoat 0.4 to 0.6 mm]
    S3 --> S4[Lay First CSM Layer]
    S4 --> S5[Apply Resin with Roller]
    S5 --> S6[Consolidate with Metal Roller]
    S6 --> S7{All Plies Laid?}
    S7 -->|No| S4
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Cover with Peel Ply]
    S8 --> S9[Ambient Cure 24 hours]
    S9 --> S10[Demould and Trim]
    S10 --> S11[Optional Post Cure at 80 C]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Vacuum Infusion (VARTM) for Marine Hulls

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Mould with Gelcoat] --> M2[Lay Dry Fabrics and Core]
    M2 --> M3[Place Peel Ply and Flow Media]
    M3 --> M4[Seal with Vacuum Bag]
    M4 --> M5[Connect Resin Trap and Pump]
    M5 --> M6[Apply Vacuum 0.9 bar]
    M6 --> M7[Infuse Resin via Inlet]
    M7 --> M8[Resin Flows Through Laminate]
    M8 --> M9[Clamp Inlet When Wetting Complete]
    M9 --> M10[Cure at Room Temperature]
    M10 --> M11[De-bag and Demould]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Matrix — Process Selection by Hull Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Boat Length?} -->|Under 4 m| Q2{Annual Production?}
    Q1 -->|4 to 10 m| Q3{Strength Priority?}
    Q1 -->|Over 10 m| Q4[Filament Winding or VARTM]

    Q2 -->|Less than 10 units| P1[Hand Lay-up]
    Q2 -->|10 to 100 units| P2[Spray Lay-up]

    Q3 -->|Cost driven| P3[Hand Lay-up with Woven Roving]
    Q3 -->|Quality driven| P4[Vacuum Infusion VARTM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams represent the **functional architecture of the FRP processing workflow** for boat construction, mapping the interactions between equipment, materials, and process steps as required by the KTU 2024 scheme pedagogy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
*Define FRP composites. List any two resins commonly used in boat building.*

**Model Answer:**

> **FRP (Fibre Reinforced Polymer) composites** are engineered materials in which high-strength fibres (glass, carbon, or aramid) are embedded in a polymer matrix (resin) to obtain a combined material with superior specific strength and stiffness.

**Two common resins used in boat building:**

1. **Polyester resin** — inexpensive, easy to use, suitable for general-purpose hulls.
2. **Vinylester resin** — better mechanical properties and water resistance, used for higher-quality marine applications. (Epoxy is also acceptable.)

---

**Q2. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
*Differentiate between Hand Lay-up and Vacuum Infusion (VARTM) processes.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hand Lay-up | Vacuum Infusion (VARTM) |
|---|---|---|
| Mould type | Open, single mould | Closed (vacuum-bagged) |
| Resin application | Manual brush/roller | Drawn in by vacuum pressure |
| Void content | 3–5 % | < 1 % |
| Fibre volume fraction | 25–35 % | 45–55 % |
| Mechanical quality | Moderate | High |
| Cost & skill | Low | Higher |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Pattern, Module 1 Internal Choice]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Choose A or B

**Q.A) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Hand Lay-up process** for manufacturing an FRP boat hull. State its advantages and limitations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A boat-hull laminate is built with 6 plies of **450 g/m²** chopped strand mat (CSM) and polyester resin. The resin-to-glass weight ratio is **2.2 : 1**. Determine:
- (i) the fibre weight fraction,
- (ii) the fibre volume fraction, and
- (iii) the total mass of resin required for a **12 m²** hull panel.

Take $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³ and $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³.

### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A

#### Part (a) — Hand Lay-up Process [7 Marks]

The **Hand Lay-up process** is the oldest, simplest, and most widely used open-mould FRP process in boat building. The sequence is as follows:

1. **Mould Preparation** [1 Mark]
   The mould (typically female for boat hulls) is cleaned, polished, and a **release agent** (wax/PVA) is applied so the cured part can be removed without damage.

2. **Gelcoat Application** [1 Mark]
   A pigmented **gelcoat** (0.4–0.6 mm) is sprayed first. This becomes the visible, smooth, water-resistant outer surface of the hull.

3. **Reinforcement Placement** [1 Mark]
   Dry fabric layers — typically **chopped strand mat (CSM)** for bulk and **woven roving (WR)** for strength — are cut to shape and laid onto the gelcoat in the designed ply sequence.

4. **Resin Impregnation** [1 Mark]
   Liquid polyester resin (pre-mixed with catalyst and accelerator) is poured and spread with a brush or roller. The fabric is **wetted out** until transparent.

5. **Consolidation** [1 Mark]
   A ribbed metal roller is used to remove trapped air, compact the laminate, and ensure uniform thickness.

6. **Curing & Demoulding** [1 Mark]
   The laminate cures exothermically at room temperature for 24 hours, then is demoulded. Trimming and post-cure complete the part.

7. **Advantages and Limitations** [1 Mark]

   *Advantages:* low tooling cost, simple equipment, suitable for large parts.

   *Limitations:* labour-intensive, high void content, lower $V_f$, styrene emissions, dependent on operator skill.

#### Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Fibre Weight Fraction** [2 Marks]

$$
W_f = \dfrac{M_{glass}}{M_{glass} + M_{resin}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2.2} = \dfrac{1}{3.2} = 0.3125
$$

**Step 2 — Fibre Volume Fraction** [3 Marks]

$$
V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + (1 - W_f)/\rho_m}
$$

$$
V_f = \dfrac{0.3125 / 2.55}{0.3125/2.55 + 0.6875/1.20} = \dfrac{0.1225}{0.1225 + 0.5729} = 0.1763
$$

**Step 3 — Total Resin Mass for 12 m² Panel** [2 Marks]

Glass mass in the laminate:

$$
M_{glass} = 6 \times 12 \times 0.450 = 32.4 \text{ kg}
$$

Resin mass (using the 2.2 : 1 ratio):

$$
M_{resin} = 2.2 \times 32.4 = 71.28 \text{ kg}
$$

**Final Answer:** $W_f = 0.313$, $V_f = 0.176$, $M_{resin} = 71.28$ kg.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B) [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Vacuum Assisted Resin Transfer Molding (VARTM)** process. Compare it with the Hand Lay-up process for marine applications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A marine FRP laminate is designed with longitudinal modulus $E_c = 30$ GPa. The matrix (polyester) modulus is $E_m = 3.5$ GPa and the fibre (E-glass) modulus is $E_f = 72$ GPa. Calculate the **required fibre volume fraction** using the rule of mixtures. State one assumption of the rule of mixtures.

### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B

#### Part (a) — VARTM Process [7 Marks]

The **Vacuum Assisted Resin Transfer Molding (VARTM)** process is a closed-mould technique in which dry fabrics are placed in a mould, sealed under a vacuum bag, and impregnated with resin drawn in by atmospheric pressure.

**Process Steps** [5 Marks]:

1. Gelcoat is sprayed into the mould and cured.
2. Dry reinforcement fabrics (CSM, WR, or stitched multiaxial) are laid up in the mould.
3. A **peel-ply** and **flow media** are placed on top to distribute resin.
4. The stack is sealed with a **vacuum bag** using sealant tape.
5. A vacuum pump evacuates the bag to **~0.9 bar** below atmospheric.
6. Resin is drawn in through an inlet; it flows through the laminate, wetting the fibres.
7. When wetting is complete, the inlet is clamped and the laminate cures.
8. The bag is removed, and the part is demoulded.

**Comparison with Hand Lay-up** [2 Marks]:

| Feature | Hand Lay-up | VARTM |
|---|---|---|
| Void content | 3–5 % | < 1 % |
| $V_f$ achievable | 25–35 % | 45–55 % |
| Resin control | Manual | Vacuum-metered |
| Emissions | High (styrene) | Low (closed) |
| Mechanical quality | Moderate | High |

#### Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Apply Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus** [3 Marks]

$$
E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)
$$

Substitute values:

$$
30 = 72 \cdot V_f + 3.5 \cdot (1 - V_f)
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $V_f$** [3 Marks]

$$
30 = 72 V_f + 3.5 - 3.5 V_f
$$

$$
30 - 3.5 = (72 - 3.5) V_f
$$

$$
26.5 = 68.5 \cdot V_f
$$

$$
V_f = \dfrac{26.5}{68.5} = 0.387
$$

**Step 3 — Assumption** [1 Mark]

> The rule of mixtures assumes **iso-strain** (Voigt model): both fibre and matrix experience the **same strain** under load, i.e., a perfectly bonded interface with no slip.

**Final Answer:** $V_f \approx 0.387$ (≈ 38.7 %), achievable in practice with VARTM or prepreg processes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing weight fraction with volume fraction** — A weight fraction of 50 % is NOT the same as a volume fraction of 50 %; the conversion formula must be shown.
> 2. **Forgetting units in density** — Always state g/cm³ or kg/m³ consistently throughout the derivation.
> 3. **Skipping process steps in Hand Lay-up / VARTM** — Examiners award 1 mark per logical step. Writing only 4 of the 7 steps loses 3 marks.
> 4. **Not stating assumptions in Rule of Mixtures** — "Iso-strain" and "perfect fibre-matrix bonding" are mandatory.
> 5. **No comparison table for process questions** — A side-by-side table is worth 2 marks; a paragraph form is worth only 1.
> 6. **Post-cure omission** — Always mention the **80 °C / 4–6 hours post-cure** step when describing thermoset curing.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 1)**

- **FRP = Fibre (load-carrier) + Resin (matrix & protector) + Interface (stress transfer)** — these are the three pillars.
- **Five processing stages** for every FRP method: mould prep → reinforcement → wetting → consolidation → cure.
- **Open-mould processes** (Hand Lay-up, Spray Lay-up): low cost, low $V_f$ (25–35 %), high void content, suitable for small craft.
- **Closed-mould processes** (RTM, VARTM, Compression): higher quality, $V_f$ up to 55 %, void content < 1 %, used for series production.
- **Continuous processes** (Pultrusion, Filament Winding): unidirectional strength, used for stiffeners, masts, and pressure hulls.
- **Common resins in boat-building**: Polyester (cheap), Vinylester (mid), Epoxy (high).
- **Common reinforcements**: E-glass (general), Carbon (racing), Aramid (impact).
- **Rule of Mixtures (Longitudinal):** $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$ — iso-strain assumption.
- **Inverse Rule of Mixtures (Transverse):** $E_c = \dfrac{E_f E_m}{E_m V_f + E_f(1 - V_f)}$ — iso-stress assumption.
- **Density Rule of Mixtures:** $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)$.
- **Void content formula:** $V_v = \dfrac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{exp}}{\rho_{th}} \times 100$; design limit **< 2 %**.
- **Weight-to-volume conversion is mandatory** when given $W_f$ but asked for $V_f$.
- **Catalyst (MEKP)** dosage for polyester: typically **1–2 %** by weight.
- **Post-cure** at **80 °C × 4–6 h** raises cross-link density to > 95 %.
- **Ply count** $n = t_{laminate} / t_{ply}$ — always round **up** for safety.
- **Resin quantity** for a panel = $n \times A \times w_{ply} \times$ (resin-to-glass ratio).
- **KTU 14-mark question strategy**: always answer with **process description (5 steps × 1 mark) + comparison table (2 marks) + numerical (5 marks)** format.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Molding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Molding # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Molding (Moulding)** in composite boat construction is defined as the **controlled manufacturing process of shaping reinforced polymer composites (typically fibre-reinforced plastics/FRP) within a precisely engineered cavity or upon a prepared surface (the mold/mould)**, wherein the matrix resin is introduced, wets out the reinforcement, cures, and is later released to yield a finished hull or structural component with the mold's exact geometry. In the context of **PENST742 – Composite Boat Design (KTU 2024 Scheme)**, molding encompasses the entire workflow of: - **Mold design** (male/female, single/multi-part) - **Mold fabrication** (plug → mold) - **Surface preparation** (release agents, gel coats) - **Composite layup** (hand layup, spray-up, vacuum infusion, RTM, etc.) - **Curing & demoulding** --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy – The "Jello in a Bowl" Concept:** > Imagine you want to make a shaped jello (jelly) dessert. You pour the liquid jello mixture into a shaped bowl, wait for it to set, and then turn the bowl upside down — out pops a jello with the bowl's exact shape. **A mold in composite boat building is that "bowl," but engineered with extreme precision, surface finish, thermal stability, and release characteristics.** The "jello mixture" is replaced by liquid polyester/vinylester/epoxy resin reinforced with fibreglass or carbon fibre cloth, which hardens permanently (thermoset cure) into the hull shape. **A more technical analogy** – think of a mold as a **"negative photocopy machine"** for a boat hull: - The **plug** (a master pattern of the boat) is the original document. - The **mold** is the photocopier surface that captures every contour. - Each **laminate laid into the mold** is a duplicate copy of the hull. --- ## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (Syllabus Highlights) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 1 – Molding Core Sub-Topics:** > 1. **Mold Materials** – Wood, plaster, FRP, metal (steel/aluminium), composite tooling > 2. **Types of Molds** – Open (female) mold, Closed (matched-die) mold, Male (positive) mold > 3. **Mold Construction Stages** – Plug fabrication → Mold layup → Surface finishing > 4. **Release Agents** – Wax (PVA), semi-permanent, internal release > 5. **Gel Coat Application** – First barrier layer, 0.4–0.6 mm thickness > 6. **Layup Techniques** – Hand layup, Spray-up, Vacuum Bagging, Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM), Infusion > 7. **Curing** – Ambient, accelerated (heat), post-cure > 8. **Demoulding & Defects** – Trimming, surface defects, warpage --- ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Reference) | Parameter | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | **Gel coat thickness (spray)** | **0.4 – 0.6** | mm | | **Gel coat thickness (brush)** | **0.3 – 0.5** | mm | | **PVA release agent coats** | **3 – 4** | coats | | **Wax release curing time (between coats)** | **15 – 30** | minutes | | **Mold shrinkage allowance (polyester on FRP)** | **0.1 – 0.3** | % | | **Standard room temperature cure** | **20 – 25** | °C | | **Post-cure temperature (vinylester)** | **60 – 80** | °C | | **Resin-to-glass ratio (hand layup, chop strand mat)** | **2.5:1 to 3:1** | – | | **Fibre volume fraction (hand layup)** | **25 – 35** | % | | **Fibre volume fraction (vacuum infusion)** | **45 – 55** | % | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Molding Process The molding process for a composite boat hull is best understood as a **five-stage engineering workflow**: ### Stage 1 — Plug (Pattern) Preparation - A full-scale, dimensionally accurate replica of the intended hull is constructed. - Surface finish: **mirror-grade (Ra < 0.4 μm)** since every defect transfers 1:1 to the mold. - Materials: timber, MDF, foam (PU/PS), plaster, or CNC-milled plugs. > [!NOTE] > **Why a plug first?** Because the mold is the *negative* of the plug, and the *final laminate* is the negative of the mold — therefore the final laminate is the *positive* of the plug. ### Stage 2 — Mold Release Application - A **release system** prevents chemical bonding between the mold surface and the new laminate. - **Three-tier release system (KTU standard):** 1. **Mold sealer** (penetrates micro-porosity) 2. **Release wax** (carnauba-based, 3–4 buffed coats) 3. **PVA (polyvinyl alcohol)** liquid film — sacrificial layer ### Stage 3 — Gel Coat Application - Pigmented, thixotropic resin layer sprayed or brushed onto the mold. - Function: provides the **finished cosmetic surface** of the boat and **protects the laminate** from water ingress, UV, and osmosis. - **Critical:** The gel coat is applied *inside* the mold first (because the finished boat surface touches the gel coat). ### Stage 4 — Laminate Layup - Structural reinforcement (chopped strand mat, woven roving, biaxial cloth, core materials) is impregnated with resin and built up in engineered sequence. - Methods (KTU syllabus focus): - **Hand Lay-up** (open mold) – simplest, lowest fibre volume fraction - **Spray-up** – chopped fibre + resin sprayed simultaneously - **Vacuum Bagging** – atmospheric pressure consolidates laminate - **Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM)** – closed mold, injected resin - **Vacuum Infusion (VARTM/VIP)** – highest fibre fraction, lowest void content ### Stage 5 — Cure, De-mould, and Trim - Resin undergoes **exothermic cross-linking (thermoset cure)**. - Hull is separated from mold, edges trimmed, and fitments bonded. --- ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step | Step | Engineering Reason | |---|---| | Plug → Mold | A single plug produces many identical boats — amortizes design cost. | | Release agent | Without it, the laminate chemically fuses to the mold and is destroyed on removal. | | Gel coat | Pure structural laminate has rough fibres exposed; gel coat provides aesthetic + barrier. | | Multi-layer laminate | Each ply oriented at engineered angles carries specific loads (hoop, longitudinal, shear). | | Vacuum consolidation | Removes trapped air, improves fibre wet-out, increases strength-to-weight. | | Post-cure | Raises glass transition temperature (Tg), ensuring dimensional stability in service. | --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table summarises every formula a KTU student must memorise for the 'Molding' topic.** | # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_r/\rho_r}$ | Fibre Volume Fraction (composite) | dimensionless | | 2 | $W_r = A \times t \times \rho_r \times R$ | Resin weight required for a laminate | kg | | 3 | $W_f = A \times t \times \rho_f$ | Fibre weight required | kg | | 4 | $A = \dfrac{L \times (B + D)}{2}$ | Hull wetted surface area (small craft approx.) | m² | | 5 | $L_{mold} = L_{hull} + L_{hull} \times S$ | Mold length with shrinkage allowance | m | | 6 | $t_{gc} = \dfrac{W_{gc}}{A \times \rho_{gc}}$ | Gel coat thickness from sprayed weight | m | | 7 | $W_{gc} = A \times t_{gc} \times \rho_{gc}$ | Gel coat weight required | kg | | 8 | $W_{wax} = N_{coats} \times A \times C_{wax}$ | Mold release wax quantity | kg | | 9 | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Net consolidation pressure in vacuum bag | Pa | | 10 | $T_{cure} \approx T_g - 20$ | Approx. heat distortion temperature | °C | | 11 | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Tensile strength of laminate | Pa | | 12 | $V_{voids} = 1 - V_f - V_r$ | Void volume fraction | dimensionless | **Where:** - $V_f$ = fibre volume fraction - $W_f$ = fibre weight, $W_r$ = resin weight - $\rho_f$ = fibre density (glass ≈ **2.55 g/cm³**, carbon ≈ **1.8 g/cm³**) - $\rho_r$ = resin density (polyester ≈ **1.2 g/cm³**) - $A$ = surface area, $t$ = thickness - $R$ = resin-to-fibre weight ratio - $S$ = fractional mould shrinkage allowance - $P_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure ≈ **101.325 kPa** - $L$ = Length Overall (LOA), $B$ = Beam, $D$ = Depth --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Molding is the **gateway process** in marine composite manufacturing. It directly determines: - **Hull surface finish** (cosmetic class A vs. structural) - **Dimensional accuracy** of waterlines, offsets, and fairness - **Mechanical strength** of the laminate (via fibre volume fraction) - **Production rate** (one mold → 50–500 boats depending on material) - **Total boat cost** (mold = 8–15% of total hull cost; tooling ROI dictates batch size) > **Industry Application:** Beneteau, Jeanneau, Lagoon (catamarans) and Royal Huisman use **female FRP molds** with vacuum infusion for serial production, while custom superyacht builders (e.g., Baltic Yachts) use **male plug-built molds** with carbon prepreg and oven post-cure. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shrinkage Compensation for Mold Dimensions When a polyester/FRP hull cures, the laminate shrinks slightly because of resin cross-linking and thermal contraction. If the mold is built to the exact plug size, the released hull will be **smaller than the design drawing**. **Step 1 — Define the linear shrinkage ratio $S$:** For a typical polyester–fibreglass layup, $S$ = **0.002** (i.e., 0.2%). **Step 2 — Express mold length to compensate:** The mold must be larger than the final desired hull length by a factor of $(1 + S)$. $$L_{mold} = L_{hull} \times (1 + S)$$ **Step 3 — Expand the expression:** $$L_{mold} = L_{hull} + L_{hull} \times S$$ **Step 4 — Work a numerical example (KTU-style):** Given: - Hull Length Overall $L_{hull} = 8.000$ m - Shrinkage ratio $S = 0.002$ - Beam $B = 2.800$ m - Depth $D = 1.200$ m Compute the compensated mold length: $$L_{mold} = 8.000 \times (1 + 0.002) = 8.000 \times 1.002 = 8.016 \text{ m}$$ Compensated Beam: $$B_{mold} = 2.800 \times 1.002 = 2.8056 \text{ m}$$ Compensated Depth: $$D_{mold} = 1.200 \times 1.002 = 1.2024 \text{ m}$$ **Result:** The mold must be built **8.016 m × 2.8056 m × 1.2024 m** to yield an **8.000 m × 2.800 m × 1.200 m** hull after cure. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Gel Coat Quantity Calculation A boat hull requires a **gel coat film** of uniform thickness on the mold surface before laminate layup. **Step 1 — Calculate the wetted hull surface area** (small-craft approximation, treating the hull as a half-ellipsoid projected longitudinally): $$A = \dfrac{L \times (B + D)}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Compute required gel coat weight:** $$W_{gc} = A \times t_{gc} \times \rho_{gc}$$ Where: - $t_{gc}$ = desired gel coat thickness (typical **0.5 mm = 0.0005 m**) - $\rho_{gc}$ = gel coat density (typical **1.15 g/cm³ = 1150 kg/m³**) **Step 3 — Numerical example:** Given: - $L = 8.000$ m, $B = 2.800$ m, $D = 1.200$ m - $t_{gc} = 0.5$ mm = $5 \times 10^{-4}$ m - $\rho_{gc} = 1150$ kg/m³ Surface area: $$A = \dfrac{8.000 \times (2.800 + 1.200)}{2} = \dfrac{8.000 \times 4.000}{2} = \dfrac{32.000}{2} = 16.000 \text{ m}^2$$ Gel coat weight: $$W_{gc} = 16.000 \times (5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 1150$$ $$W_{gc} = 16.000 \times 0.0005 \times 1150$$ $$W_{gc} = 0.008 \times 1150$$ $$W_{gc} = 9.200 \text{ kg}$$ **Result:** Approximately **9.2 kg of gel coat** is required to achieve a uniform **0.5 mm** coating over a hull of wetted area **16 m²**. > **Practical addition (wastage):** Add **15–20%** wastage factor → final gel coat order ≈ **10.6–11.0 kg**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Laminate Resin Quantity (Hand Layup) **Step 1 — Define the laminate plan** (e.g., 3 layers of 450 g/m² chopped strand mat + 2 layers of 800 g/m² woven roving). **Step 2 — Total fibre weight per unit area:** $$W_{f,A} = (3 \times 0.450) + (2 \times 0.800) = 1.350 + 1.600 = 2.950 \text{ kg/m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Apply resin-to-fibre weight ratio $R = 2.5$** (typical for hand layup with this stack): $$W_{r,A} = R \times W_{f,A} = 2.5 \times 2.950 = 7.375 \text{ kg/m}^2$$ **Step 4 — Total laminate weight per unit area:** $$W_{tot,A} = W_{f,A} + W_{r,A} = 2.950 + 7.375 = 10.325 \text{ kg/m}^2$$ **Step 5 — Total resin required for the hull:** $$W_{r,total} = W_{r,A} \times A = 7.375 \times 16.000 = 118.000 \text{ kg}$$ **Result:** This hull requires approximately **118 kg of resin** and **47.2 kg of glass fibre** for the structural laminate, **excluding** gel coat and wastage. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fibre Volume Fraction from Weights Given constituent weights and densities, the fibre volume fraction is: $$V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_r / \rho_r}$$ **Step 1 — Insert numerical values:** - $W_f = 47.2$ kg - $W_r = 118.0$ kg - $\rho_f = 2550$ kg/m³ (E-glass) - $\rho_r = 1200$ kg/m³ (polyester) **Step 2 — Compute fibre volume:** $$\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{47.2}{2550} = 0.01851 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Compute resin volume:** $$\dfrac{W_r}{\rho_r} = \dfrac{118.0}{1200} = 0.09833 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 4 — Insert into formula:** $$V_f = \dfrac{0.01851}{0.01851 + 0.09833} = \dfrac{0.01851}{0.11684}$$ $$V_f \approx 0.1585 \text{ or } 15.85\%$$ > **KTU Note:** Hand layup typically yields $V_f$ in the range **15–25%** by this calculation. Vacuum infusion achieves **45–55%** — this is a frequently-asked conceptual comparison in exams. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Vacuum Bag Consolidation Pressure The pressure consolidating a vacuum-bagged laminate is the *difference* between atmospheric and absolute (bag) pressure. **Step 1 — Atmospheric pressure:** $$P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 2 — Absolute pressure in bag (typical vacuum pump):** $$P_{abs} = 5 \text{ kPa} \text{ (≈ 95% vacuum)}$$ **Step 3 — Net consolidation pressure:** $$P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs} = 101.325 - 5 = 96.325 \text{ kPa}$$ **Result:** The laminate is compressed by approximately **1 atm (≈ 96 kPa)**, which removes air voids, drives out excess resin, and packs fibres tightly — yielding the higher $V_f$ noted above. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Molding Workflow > [!NOTE] > **Diagram explanation:** This Mermaid flowchart maps the engineering interactions of the five-stage composite boat molding process, showing input materials, transformation processes, and final outputs at each stage. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Boat Design Drawings
Lines Plan and Offsets] --> B0[Stage 1 PLUG FABRICATION
Timber or Foam Master Pattern] B0 --> C0[Stage 2 MOLD RELEASE
Sealer plus Wax plus PVA] C0 --> D0[Stage 3 GEL COAT APPLICATION
Sprayed or Brushed 0.5 mm] D0 --> E0[Stage 4 LAMINATE LAYUP
CSM plus Woven Roving plus Core] E0 --> F0[Stage 5 CURE AND DEMOULD
Ambient or Heat Assisted] F0 --> G0[Finished Hull
Ready for Fit Out] H0[Resin System
Polyester Vinylester Epoxy] --> E0 I0[Reinforcement
Glass Carbon Aramid Fibre] --> E0 J0[Vacuum Pump and Bagging Film] --> E0 K0[Release Wax and PVA] --> C0 L0[Pigmented Gel Coat] --> D0 E0 --> M0{Quality Inspection} M0 -->|Pass| G0 M0 -->|Defect Detected| N0[Repair or Reject] N0 --> E0 ``` --- ## 4.2 Mold Type Comparison Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[MOLDS IN COMPOSITE BOAT CONSTRUCTION] --> B1[FEMALE OPEN MOLD
Inside Surface is Finished Hull] A1 --> C1[MALE POSITIVE MOLD
Outside Surface is Finished Hull] A1 --> D1[CLOSED MATCHED DIE MOLD
Two Halves Join Together] B1 --> B2[Used For Small Craft Hulls] C1 --> C2[Used For Deck and Console Parts] D1 --> D2[Used For Small Serial Components] B1 --> E1[Pros Easy Gel Coat Good Finish] B1 --> F1[Cons One Side Rough] C1 --> E2[Pros Both Sides Smooth] C1 --> F2[Cons Difficult Gel Coat Application] D1 --> E3[Pros High Production Rate] D1 --> F3[Cons High Tooling Cost] ``` --- ## 4.3 Layer Sequence Diagram (Laminate Build-up Inside Female Mold) ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[LAYER 0 EXTERNAL GEL COAT
0.5 mm Pigmented Resin Film] L2[LAYER 1 SURFACING MAT
30 g per m2 Glass Tissue] L3[LAYER 2 CHOPPED STRAND MAT
450 g per m2 CSM] L4[LAYER 3 WOVEN ROVING
800 g per m2 WR] L5[LAYER 4 WOVEN ROVING
800 g per m2 WR] L6[LAYER 5 CHOPPED STRAND MAT
450 g per m2 CSM] L7[LAYER 6 STRUCTURAL CORE OPTIONAL
PVC or Balsa] L8[LAYER 7 INNER SKIN
CSM plus Woven Roving] L9[LAYER 8 INNER GEL OR PAINT
Final Inner Surface] L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5 --> L6 --> L7 --> L8 --> L9 ``` --- ## 4.4 Process Selection Decision Matrix (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{What Production Volume
is Required?} Q1 -->|1 to 5 Boats| R1[Hand Layup Process
Low Cost Open Mold] Q1 -->|10 to 100 Boats| R2[Spray Up plus Hand Layup
Medium Cost] Q1 -->|100 to 500 Boats| R3[Vacuum Infusion VARTM
Higher Quality] Q1 -->|Greater Than 500 Boats| R4[Closed Mold RTM
High Tooling Cost] Q2{What Strength to Weight
Ratio is Required?} Q2 -->|Low to Medium| S1[Chopped Strand Mat plus Polyester] Q2 -->|Medium to High| S2[Woven Roving plus Vinylester] Q2 -->|High to Very High| S3[Carbon Prepreg plus Epoxy plus Oven Cure] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper PENST742]** > **Define the term 'mold' in composite boat construction. Distinguish clearly between a 'plug' and a 'mold'.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Mold (1 mark):** A mold is a precisely fabricated reverse-shape cavity or surface upon which or into which fibre-reinforced composite materials are laid and cured to produce a boat hull of desired geometry and surface finish. - **Plug (1 mark):** A plug is the original, full-scale, dimensionally accurate master pattern built first; the mold is then made from the plug. - **Distinction (1 mark):** The plug is a *positive* of the boat; the mold is a *negative* of the boat (positive of the plug). The plug is built once and used many times to produce identical molds or hulls. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **List and briefly explain the three main release agents used in FRP boat molding.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Mold Sealer** – A liquid penetrating agent that seals the micro-porosity of the mold surface to prevent release wax from soaking in; it provides a stable base. 2. **Mold Release Wax (Carnauba-based)** – Applied in 3–4 buffed coats; provides a waxy sacrificial film that prevents chemical bonding between mold and laminate. 3. **Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA)** – A water-soluble liquid film applied over the wax as an additional sacrificial layer; particularly useful when the laminate will be post-cured at elevated temperatures. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks, Module 1 Choice A]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **Hand Lay-up molding process** for a small composite boat hull, with a neat sketch of the layer sequence from gel coat to inner skin. **(b) [7 marks]** A boat hull of **Length Overall (LOA) = 10 m**, **Beam (B) = 3.2 m**, and **Depth (D) = 1.4 m** is to be built using hand layup. The required laminate stack is: **2 layers of 450 g/m² CSM + 2 layers of 800 g/m² WR**. Gel coat thickness = **0.5 mm**, density = **1150 kg/m³**. Polyester resin density = **1200 kg/m³**. Resin-to-fibre weight ratio = **2.8 : 1**. Shrinkage allowance = **0.2%**. **Calculate:** - (i) Hull wetted surface area - (ii) Total gel coat weight required - (iii) Total fibre and resin weight required - (iv) Compensated mold dimensions (L × B × D) - (v) The final fibre volume fraction ($V_f$) ### **Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]** The **Hand Lay-up (Contact Molding)** process is the oldest and most widely used FRP molding method for boats up to ~15 m. Steps: 1. **Mold preparation (1 mark):** The female mold is cleaned, polished, and a three-stage release system (sealer → wax → PVA) is applied. 2. **Gel coat application (1 mark):** Pigmented thixotropic gel coat is sprayed (~0.5 mm) on the mold surface. This becomes the *outer cosmetic surface* of the finished boat. 3. **Surface tissue layer (1/2 mark):** A 30 g/m² glass surface tissue is rolled into the still-tacky gel coat to prevent print-through of fibre pattern. 4. **Structural laminate (2 marks):** Reinforcement mats/cloths (CSM for resin-rich bonding layers, WR for strength) are placed in the engineered sequence; resin is poured/rolled and consolidated with rollers to remove air. 5. **Cure (1 mark):** The laminate cures at ambient temperature (20–25 °C) for 12–24 hours. Post-cure at 60–80 °C may follow for vinylester. 6. **De-mould & trim (1/2 mark):** Hull is carefully separated, edges trimmed, fitments bonded. ### **Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks — 1.4 marks each sub-part]** **Given data:** - $L = 10$ m, $B = 3.2$ m, $D = 1.4$ m - $t_{gc} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\rho_{gc} = 1150$ kg/m³ - $\rho_r = 1200$ kg/m³, $R = 2.8$, $S = 0.002$ **(i) Hull wetted surface area:** $$A = \dfrac{L \times (B + D)}{2} = \dfrac{10 \times (3.2 + 1.4)}{2} = \dfrac{10 \times 4.6}{2} = \dfrac{46}{2} = 23.0 \text{ m}^2$$ **(ii) Gel coat weight:** $$W_{gc} = A \times t_{gc} \times \rho_{gc} = 23.0 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1150 = 13.225 \text{ kg}$$ **(iii) Fibre and resin weight:** Fibre area density: $$W_{f,A} = (2 \times 0.450) + (2 \times 0.800) = 0.900 + 1.600 = 2.500 \text{ kg/m}^2$$ Resin area density: $$W_{r,A} = R \times W_{f,A} = 2.8 \times 2.500 = 7.000 \text{ kg/m}^2$$ Total fibre: $$W_f = W_{f,A} \times A = 2.500 \times 23.0 = 57.5 \text{ kg}$$ Total resin: $$W_r = W_{r,A} \times A = 7.000 \times 23.0 = 161.0 \text{ kg}$$ **(iv) Compensated mold dimensions:** $$L_{mold} = 10 \times (1 + 0.002) = 10.020 \text{ m}$$ $$B_{mold} = 3.2 \times 1.002 = 3.2064 \text{ m}$$ $$D_{mold} = 1.4 \times 1.002 = 1.4028 \text{ m}$$ Mold size: **10.020 m × 3.2064 m × 1.4028 m** **(v) Fibre volume fraction:** $$\dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{57.5}{2550} = 0.02255 \text{ m}^3$$ $$\dfrac{W_r}{\rho_r} = \dfrac{161.0}{1200} = 0.13417 \text{ m}^3$$ $$V_f = \dfrac{0.02255}{0.02255 + 0.13417} = \dfrac{0.02255}{0.15672} = 0.1439 \approx 14.4\%$$ > **Conclusion:** This is a typical hand layup $V_f$ value (15–25% range). To achieve higher $V_f$ and lower weight, **vacuum infusion** should be adopted. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **(a) [7 marks]** Compare **Hand Lay-up, Spray-up, Vacuum Bagging, and Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM)** processes used in composite boat construction. Tabulate your answer covering: process principle, mould type, fibre volume fraction achievable, typical boat application, advantages, and limitations. **(b) [7 marks]** A **6 m** recreational boat hull is to be built by **vacuum infusion**. The laminate plan calls for **3 layers of 600 g/m² biaxial glass + 1 layer of 450 g/m² CSM**, with a target fibre volume fraction of **50%**. Resin density = **1200 kg/m³**, fibre density = **2550 kg/m³**. Calculate: - (i) The fibre area density of the laminate - (ii) The required resin-to-fibre weight ratio $R$ to achieve $V_f = 0.50$ - (iii) Total resin and fibre weights for a hull wetted area of 14 m² ### **Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]** | Parameter | Hand Lay-up | Spray-up | Vacuum Bagging | RTM | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Process** | Manual cloth + resin | Chopped fibre + resin sprayed | Hand layup + vacuum bag | Closed mold, injected resin | | **Mold Type** | Open female | Open female | Open female, bagged | Closed matched die | | **$V_f$ achievable** | 15–25% | 15–22% | 35–45% | 40–50% | | **Boat Application** | Small fishing boats, dinghies | Custom hulls, prototypes | Racing yachts, patrol boats | Serial small craft (<8 m) | | **Advantage** | Lowest cost, simple | Fast coverage of large areas | High quality, low voids | Both sides smooth, repeatable | | **Limitation** | Low strength/weight, labour | High styrene emission, messy | Bag consumables, skilled | High tooling cost | **Marks distribution:** Table (5 marks) + conclusion (1 mark) + neat sketch reference (1 mark). ### **Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]** **(i) Fibre area density:** $$W_{f,A} = (3 \times 0.600) + (1 \times 0.450) = 1.800 + 0.450 = 2.250 \text{ kg/m}^2$$ **(ii) Resin-to-fibre weight ratio for $V_f = 0.50$:** Starting from: $$V_f = \dfrac{W_f/\rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_r/\rho_r}$$ Rearranging for $W_r / W_f$ when $V_f = 0.50$: $$0.50 = \dfrac{1/\rho_f}{1/\rho_f + (W_r/W_f)/\rho_r}$$ Let $x = W_r / W_f$: $$0.50 = \dfrac{1/\rho_f}{1/\rho_f + x/\rho_r}$$ $$0.50 \times (1/\rho_f + x/\rho_r) = 1/\rho_f$$ $$\dfrac{0.50}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{0.50 \, x}{\rho_r} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_f}$$ $$\dfrac{0.50 \, x}{\rho_r} = \dfrac{1}{\rho_f} - \dfrac{0.50}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{0.50}{\rho_f}$$ $$x = \dfrac{0.50 \times \rho_r}{0.50 \times \rho_f} = \dfrac{\rho_r}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{1200}{2550} = 0.4706$$ So $R = W_r / W_f \approx 0.47$ (i.e., **0.47 : 1**) **(iii) Total weights for A = 14 m²:** - Total fibre: $W_f = 2.250 \times 14 = 31.50$ kg - Total resin: $W_r = 0.47 \times 31.50 = 14.81$ kg **Result:** Vacuum infusion requires only **~14.8 kg of resin** for the same hull — a dramatic reduction compared to the hand layup example (161 kg), reflecting the much higher fibre volume fraction and resin efficiency. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — How Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to add shrinkage allowance to mold dimensions** → loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Confusing fibre volume fraction ($V_f$) with fibre weight fraction ($W_f / W_{tot}$)** → the two are very different; $V_f$ uses volumes, not weights. > 3. **Mixing up units** (mm vs cm vs m in gel coat thickness) → KTU examiners are strict; a 0.5 mm value should never appear as 0.5 m in calculation. > 4. **Skipping the wastage factor** for gel coat and resin (15–20%) — though not always marked, mentioning it shows professional maturity. > 5. **Writing 'mold' and 'mould' inconsistently** — both are accepted in KTU papers, but use one form throughout. > 6. **Forgetting to label diagrams** — KTU deducts 0.5–1 mark per unlabeled sketch. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision Bullet Points — "Molding" Module 1** - ✅ **Mold** is the *negative* of the hull; **Plug** is the *positive*; **Hull** is the *positive* of the mold. - ✅ A **female (open) mold** is most common for small boats; gel coat is applied first *inside* the mold. - ✅ The **gel coat** provides the cosmetic finish and the **first defence** against water/UV/osmosis. - ✅ **Gel coat thickness** = **0.4 – 0.6 mm** (sprayed), **0.3 – 0.5 mm** (brushed). - ✅ **Three-stage release system** = Sealer + Wax (3–4 coats) + PVA film. - ✅ **Hand layup** $V_f$ ≈ 15–25%; **Vacuum Infusion** $V_f$ ≈ 45–55%; **RTM** $V_f$ ≈ 40–50%. - ✅ **Mold shrinkage compensation** uses $L_{mold} = L_{hull} (1 + S)$ with $S \approx 0.002$ for polyester/FRP. - ✅ **Wetted surface area** for small craft: $A = L (B + D) / 2$. - ✅ **Glass transition temperature (Tg)** governs post-cure: $T_{cure} \approx T_g - 20$ °C. - ✅ **Cure is exothermic** — thick laminates (>6 mm in one shot) risk thermal runaway. - ✅ **Female mold advantage** = excellent gel coat finish; **disadvantage** = inner (laminate) side is rough and requires fairing. - ✅ **Closed mold (RTM)** advantage = both sides smooth; **disadvantage** = very high tooling cost. - ✅ **Vacuum bagging** applies ~**1 atm (101 kPa)** consolidation pressure. - ✅ **Standard room-temperature cure** range: **20–25 °C** for 12–24 hours. - ✅ **Post-cure** for vinylester: **60–80 °C** for 4–8 hours. - ✅ **Common defects**: air voids, dry spots, fibre print-through, gel coat cracking, warping, osmosis blisters. - ✅ **Mold material selection** depends on production volume: wood (1–5 hulls), FRP (10–100), aluminium/steel (>500). - ✅ Always **add 15–20% wastage** to gel coat, resin, and fibre quantity calculations in real shop-floor practice.

topics.title: Spray Up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Spray Up

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Spray Up** (also termed **Spray Lay-Up** or **Chopped Fiber Spray Lay-Up**) is an **open-mold, low-pressure composite fabrication process** in which **continuous fiberglass rovings are chopped into short fibers (typically 10 mm to 50 mm in length) inside a spray gun**, simultaneously wetted with a **catalyzed liquid thermosetting resin** (commonly polyester or vinyl ester), and **pneumatically projected (sprayed) onto a prepared mold surface**. The deposited laminate is subsequently **compacted and de-aerated using hand rollers**, allowed to cure (typically at ambient temperature), and then demolded to yield a finished fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) part.

In the context of **KTU PENST742 (Composite Boat Design)**, Spray Up is classified as a **contact molding / open-mold wet lay-up variant** and is widely used for producing **large, single-cure boat hulls, decks, bulkheads, and small craft** where tooling cost must be minimized and production volumes are moderate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Spray Up is grouped under **Module 1: Introduction to Composite Manufacturing Processes for Boat Building**. Students must identify Spray Up as a **chopped-fiber, open-mold, low Vf process** distinct from Hand Lay-Up (which uses continuous mat/broad goods) and from SMC/BMC (which use pre-impregnated bulk molding compounds).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Paint-Sprayer + Lawn-Mower Hybrid":**
> Imagine you are spray-painting a wall, but instead of just spraying paint, your spray gun has a **built-in pair of scissors (chopper blades)** that continuously **cuts a long rope of fiberglass into small pieces** at the same moment the gun squirts the resin. Both chopped fibers and resin land together on the mold surface like wet snow. Then you take a **rolling pin** and press the wet mixture flat to squeeze out trapped air. After it hardens, you peel it off the mold.
>
> - The **spray gun** = paint sprayer + chopper
> - The **fiberglass roving** = the "rope" being cut
> - The **resin + catalyst** = the "wet snow"
> - The **roller** = the rolling pin that compacts and de-aerates
> - The **mold** = the wall you are painting

This analogy captures the **simultaneity** of fiber chopping, resin atomization, and deposition — the defining signature of the Spray Up process.

## 1.3 Key Process Metrics (KTU Board-Standard Values)

The following physical constants and standard metrics must be memorized for KTU valuation:

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Chopped fiber length** | **10 mm – 50 mm (typically 25 mm)** |
| **Fiber volume fraction (Vf)** | **20 % – 30 % (low compared to 50 %–60 % for closed-mold processes)** |
| **Resin content by weight** | **60 % – 75 %** |
| **Typical spray air pressure** | **2 bar – 4 bar (30 – 60 psi)** |
| **Resin catalyst ratio (MEKP)** | **1 % – 2 % by weight of resin** |
| **Laminate thickness per pass** | **0.5 mm – 1.5 mm** |
| **Production rate** | **2 kg/min – 10 kg/min of laminate (deposition rate)** |
| **Working time / pot life** | **15 min – 45 min (resin-dependent)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why low Vf matters in boat design:** Because fiber length is short (chopped) and only roller compaction (not vacuum or autoclave pressure) is applied, voids and resin-rich pockets dominate the microstructure. This limits Spray Up's use to **non-structural and semi-structural boat components** such as deckhouses, interiors, and small dinghies — *not* primary hull shell plating on performance craft.

## 1.4 Position of Spray Up in the Composite Manufacturing Family Tree

Spray Up sits between **Hand Lay-Up** (lower capital, lower Vf, slower) and **SMC/BMC** (higher capital, higher Vf, faster) on the cost–performance spectrum. For boat-building specifically:

- **Hand Lay-Up** → primary hulls of small FRP fishing boats
- **Spray Up** → large-area boat hulls, deck modules, sanitary modules
- **RTM / Vacuum Infusion** → high-performance racing yacht hulls
- **Prepreg + Autoclave** → aerospace / America's Cup class structures

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Step-by-Step Logic

The Spray Up process follows a **strict sequential workflow**. Understanding *why* each step exists is essential for KTU's "process selection and justification" questions.

### Step 1: Mold Preparation
- The mold (typically female, made of FRP, wood, or epoxy-tooling board) is cleaned, waxed with release agent, and polished.
- **Why?** Spray Up resin is *not* under high pressure — without release agent, the part will bond permanently to the mold.

### Step 2: Gel-Coat Application
- A pigmented **gel coat** (typically 0.4 mm – 0.6 mm thick isophthalic polyester or vinyl ester) is sprayed or brushed onto the mold and allowed to partially cure ("tack-free" stage).
- **Why?** Gel coat provides the **cosmetic finish**, **UV protection**, and a **moisture barrier** on what will become the outer (hull) surface.

### Step 3: Roving Feeding & Chopping
- Continuous fiberglass rovings (e.g., 2400 tex) are drawn into the spray gun from a creel.
- An internal chopper (rotating blades driven by air motor) cuts the roving into **25 mm – 50 mm fibers**.
- **Why chopped?** Short fibers flow through the gun without clogging and lay down isotropically (random orientation), giving **quasi-isotropic in-plane properties** suitable for complex boat curvatures.

### Step 4: Resin Atomization & Catalyzation
- Liquid resin (polyester with **MEKP catalyst** + **cobalt accelerator**) is pressurized through the gun nozzle, atomizing into a fine mist.
- The catalyst initiates crosslinking; gelation typically begins 15–45 min after deposition.
- **Why two-component in the gun?** Because catalyzed resin has limited pot life, mixing occurs *inside the gun head* just before spray, ensuring the resin doesn't gel inside the equipment.

### Step 5: Simultaneous Deposition
- Chopped fibers and resin droplets impinge on the mold surface in a **co-mingled stream**.
- The momentum of the spray consolidates the laminate *partially*; initial wet-out is achieved by surface tension and capillary action.

### Step 6: Hand Roller Compaction
- Workers use **paddle rollers, grooved rollers, or consolidator rollers** to:
  1. **Wet-out** the fibers fully (drive out dry spots).
  2. **Compact** the laminate to target thickness.
  3. **De-aerate** — roll out trapped air pockets and voids.
  4. **Distribute** fibers evenly.
- **Why critical?** Voids in FRP laminates reduce interlaminar shear strength (ILSS) by **up to 30 % per 1 % void content**.

### Step 7: Cure & Demold
- Cure occurs at ambient temperature (or in a heated mold at 40 °C–60 °C for faster cycles).
- After cure (typically 24 h for full strength), the part is demolded, trimmed, and post-cured if required.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **high-yield for KTU board exams**. Memorize the variable definitions, units, and the algebraic relationships — most derivation-style questions on Module 1 will test these.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_r / \rho_r}$ | Fiber volume fraction from weight fractions | $W_f$ = fiber weight, $W_r$ = resin weight, $\rho_f$ = fiber density (g/cm³), $\rho_r$ = resin density (g/cm³) |
| 2 | $V_v = 1 - V_f - V_r$ | Void volume fraction | $V_r$ = resin volume fraction |
| 3 | $W_r = \dfrac{\rho_r}{\rho_f} \cdot \dfrac{1 - V_f}{V_f} \cdot W_f$ | Resin weight required per unit fiber weight | — |
| 4 | $t_{lam} = \dfrac{m_{dep}}{A \cdot \rho_{c}} $ | Laminate thickness from mass deposited | $m_{dep}$ = mass deposited (kg), $A$ = area (m²), $\rho_c$ = cured laminate density (kg/m³) |
| 5 | $\dot{m}_{lam} = \dot{m}_r + \dot{m}_f$ | Mass conservation in spray stream | $\dot{m}_r$ = resin flow rate, $\dot{m}_f$ = fiber flow rate (kg/min) |
| 6 | $R_{c} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{\dot{m}_{lam}} \times 100\%$ | Chop content (fiber fraction by mass in deposition) | — |
| 7 | $V_{spray} = k \cdot P_{air} \cdot A_{nozzle}$ | Volumetric spray rate (empirical) | $P_{air}$ = atomizing air pressure, $A_{nozzle}$ = nozzle area, $k$ = empirical constant |
| 8 | $P_{cured} = P_0 \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Cure kinetics (Arrhenius-type, conceptual only) | $E_a$ = activation energy, $R$ = gas constant, $T$ = absolute temperature |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Notation Rule for KTU Answer Scripts:** When writing equations in the exam, never use `|` for absolute value (e.g., avoid $|x|$). Use `\vert` or `\mid`. Also keep one blank line before and after every display equation block in your answer sheet — examiners visually associate clean spacing with conceptual clarity.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

In the marine industry, Spray Up is the **workhorse process for medium-to-large FRP boat hulls (8 m – 20 m length)** because:

1. **Low tooling cost** → mold cost is 5 % – 10 % of an equivalent RTM or autoclave mold.
2. **Large part capability** → entire hull or deck can be laid up in one shot.
3. **Geometry flexibility** → complex double-curvature surfaces (hull chines, transoms, bulwarks) are easily accommodated.
4. **Acceptable for leisure craft** → meets ISO 12215-5 scantling requirements for non-primary structures.

**Limitation:** Spray Up laminates typically deliver only **40 %–60 % of the mechanical properties** of equivalent vacuum-infused or prepreg laminates due to high void content (3 %–6 %) and low Vf. This is why **performance racing yachts and naval vessels avoid Spray Up** for primary structure.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Laminate Thickness from Spray Deposition Rate

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
During a Spray Up operation on a 12 m fishing boat hull, the spray gun deposits laminate at a mass flow rate of $\dot{m}_{lam} = 6 \text{ kg/min}$. The cured laminate density is $\rho_c = 1450 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The operator sweeps the gun over a working area at a traverse speed that results in an effective deposition footprint of $A = 0.8 \text{ m}^2$ per minute. Calculate the laminate thickness built up in **one pass** and the **number of passes** required to achieve a target hull laminate thickness of $t_{target} = 6 \text{ mm}$.

### Step 1: Identify the governing equation

The fundamental relationship between deposited mass, area, density, and thickness comes from mass conservation:

$$
m_{dep} = \rho_c \cdot V_{lam} = \rho_c \cdot (A \cdot t_{lam})
$$

Rearranging for thickness:

$$
t_{lam} = \dfrac{m_{dep}}{A \cdot \rho_c}
$$

> This is the direct application of **Formula #4** from the Cheat Sheet.

### Step 2: Compute the mass deposited per unit area per unit time

Given:
- $\dot{m}_{lam} = 6 \text{ kg/min}$
- $A = 0.8 \text{ m}^2$ covered per minute

Mass per unit area per minute:

$$
\dfrac{m_{dep}}{A} = \dfrac{6 \text{ kg/min}}{0.8 \text{ m}^2/\text{min}} = 7.5 \text{ kg/m}^2 \text{ per pass}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the thickness equation

$$
t_{lam} = \dfrac{7.5 \text{ kg/m}^2}{1450 \text{ kg/m}^3}
$$

Performing the division:

$$
t_{lam} = \dfrac{7.5}{1450} \text{ m} = 0.0051724 \text{ m} \approx 5.17 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the number of passes for the target thickness

$$
N_{passes} = \dfrac{t_{target}}{t_{lam}} = \dfrac{6 \text{ mm}}{5.17 \text{ mm/pass}} \approx 1.16 \text{ passes}
$$

### Step 5: Engineering Interpretation

Since partial passes are not practical in field operation, the operator would lay down **2 full passes**, giving a final thickness slightly above 6 mm. After roller compaction, the effective thickness stabilizes near the target.

**Final Answer (boxed for KTU script):**

$$
\boxed{t_{lam} \approx 5.17 \text{ mm/pass}, \quad N_{passes} \approx 2 \text{ (round up)}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Fiber Volume Fraction from Mix Ratio

**Problem Statement:**
A Spray Up operation uses a **resin-to-fiber weight ratio of $R:W = 2.5 : 1$** (i.e., 2.5 kg resin per 1 kg fiber). Given:
- Fiber density: $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (E-glass)
- Cured resin density: $\rho_r = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (cured polyester)

Calculate the **fiber volume fraction** $V_f$ and the **void fraction** $V_v$ (assuming the cured laminate contains 3 % voids by volume).

### Step 1: Express weight fractions

Let $W_f = 1 \text{ kg}$, then $W_r = 2.5 \text{ kg}$.

### Step 2: Convert weights to volumes

Volume of fibers:

$$
V_f^{vol} = \dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{1 \text{ kg}}{2550 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 3.922 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
$$

Volume of resin:

$$
V_r^{vol} = \dfrac{W_r}{\rho_r} = \dfrac{2.5 \text{ kg}}{1200 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 2.083 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
$$

### Step 3: Compute fiber volume fraction (ignoring voids first)

$$
V_f^{ideal} = \dfrac{V_f^{vol}}{V_f^{vol} + V_r^{vol}} = \dfrac{3.922 \times 10^{-4}}{3.922 \times 10^{-4} + 2.083 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
V_f^{ideal} = \dfrac{3.922 \times 10^{-4}}{2.475 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1585 \quad \text{or} \quad 15.85\%
$$

### Step 4: Adjust for voids

Given $V_v = 3\% = 0.03$, the true fiber volume fraction in the actual cured laminate is:

$$
V_f^{actual} = V_f^{ideal} \cdot (1 - V_v) = 0.1585 \times 0.97 = 0.1537 \quad \text{or} \quad 15.37\%
$$

### Step 5: Verify with the constraint equation

$$
V_f + V_r + V_v = 1
$$

We have $V_f = 0.1537$, $V_v = 0.03$, so:

$$
V_r = 1 - 0.1537 - 0.03 = 0.8163 \quad (81.63\%)
$$

### Step 6: Engineering Interpretation

A fiber volume fraction of only **~15 %** is **at the low end of typical Spray Up laminates** (20 %–30 %). This implies the mix was **resin-heavy**, which:
- Increases laminate weight
- Reduces specific stiffness
- Increases exotherm during cure
- Improves surface finish and reduces print-through

For higher mechanical performance, the operator should target a resin:fiber ratio closer to **1.5:1 to 2:1**, raising $V_f$ to **20 %–25 %**.

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{V_f \approx 15.4\%, \quad V_v = 3\%, \quad V_r \approx 81.6\%}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Selection for Boat Components

| Boat Component | Recommended Process | Why Spray Up May/May Not Apply |
|---|---|---|
| Small dinghy hull (< 4 m) | Hand Lay-Up or Spray Up | Spray Up acceptable for fast production |
| Fishing trawler hull (8–15 m) | **Spray Up** | Industry standard; cost-effective |
| Racing yacht hull (high-performance) | Vacuum Infusion / Prepreg | Spray Up voids unacceptable |
| Deckhouse / cabin | **Spray Up** | Excellent fit; cosmetic gel coat finish |
| Internal bulkheads | Hand Lay-Up (cheaper for small parts) | Spray Up overkill for small areas |
| Mast / spar | Filament Winding or Prepreg | Spray Up insufficient fiber alignment |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Spray Up Process

The diagram below maps the **functional flow** of materials, equipment, and process stages in a Spray Up operation — capturing the **simultaneity** of fiber chopping and resin atomization that defines this process.


### Diagram Interpretation Guide

- **Orange boxes (Core Equipment):** The spray gun subsystem — the heart of the process where chopping and atomization occur simultaneously.

- **Green boxes (Outputs):** The final cured boat component.

### Interpretation

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define the Spray Up process for composite boat construction. List any four advantages of Spray Up over Hand Lay-Up."**

**Model Answer:**

Spray Up is an **open-mold composite manufacturing process** in which **continuous fiberglass rovings are chopped into short fibers (typically 25 mm) inside a spray gun**, simultaneously combined with **catalyzed liquid thermosetting resin**, and **pneumatically sprayed onto a mold surface**. The deposited laminate is **compacted using hand rollers** and allowed to cure at ambient temperature.

**Four advantages over Hand Lay-Up:**

1. **Higher production rate** — deposition rates of 2–10 kg/min vs. ~0.5–1 kg/min for hand lay-up.
2. **Lower labor cost** — single operator can lay up large areas; no manual mat placement.
3. **Better conformability to complex shapes** — fibers follow contours without wrinkles.
4. **Quasi-isotropic properties** — randomly oriented chopped fibers provide uniform in-plane properties, ideal for curved hull surfaces.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse Spray Up with Hand Lay-Up. The **key differentiator** is the **chopping + simultaneous spraying** in Spray Up, whereas Hand Lay-Up uses **pre-cut mats or woven fabrics laid manually**. If you omit "chopping" from the definition, you will lose 1 mark.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"State any three disadvantages of Spray Up process."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Low fiber volume fraction** ($V_f = 20\%–30\%$) leads to lower mechanical properties than hand lay-up with woven roving.
2. **High void content** (3%–6%) due to lack of vacuum or autoclave pressure, reducing interlaminar shear strength.
3. **High resin consumption** (60%–75% by weight) increases material cost and laminate weight.
4. (Bonus) **Operator-dependent quality** — variability arises from spray technique and roller compaction skill.
5. (Bonus) **Styrene emissions** — open-mold process releases volatile organic compounds, requiring ventilation and PPE.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**"With a neat block diagram, explain the Spray Up process for boat building. Discuss the process parameters that affect laminate quality and the typical mechanical properties achievable."**

#### Part (a) — Process Explanation with Block Diagram [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Introduction (2 marks)**
Spray Up is an open-mold wet lay-up process used extensively in boat building for hulls, decks, and large FRP components. Continuous glass rovings are chopped and combined with catalyzed resin in a spray gun and deposited onto a prepared mold.

**Step 2: Block Diagram Description (3 marks)**
The block diagram (as shown in Section 4.1) illustrates the functional flow:

$$
\text{Roving} \rightarrow \text{Chopper} \rightarrow \text{Spray Gun} \rightarrow \text{Mold Surface} \rightarrow \text{Roller Compaction} \rightarrow \text{Cure} \rightarrow \text{Demold}
$$

**Step 3: Detailed Stages (2 marks)**

- **Mold prep & gel coat:** Wax release, gel coat application (0.4–0.6 mm).
- **Spraying:** Rovings chopped to 25 mm, mixed with catalyzed resin, sprayed at 2–4 bar air pressure.
- **Compaction:** Roller consolidation removes voids (target void content < 3%).
- **Cure:** Ambient or heated (40–60 °C) for 24 hours.

#### Part (b) — Process Parameters & Mechanical Properties [7 Marks]

**Process Parameters Affecting Quality:**

1. **Fiber length** — Longer fibers (up to 50 mm) improve strength but reduce sprayability.
2. **Spray air pressure** — Too low causes dry spots; too high causes resin bounce-off and waste.
3. **Resin viscosity** — Lower viscosity improves wet-out but causes drainage on vertical surfaces.
4. **Roller compaction force** — Higher force reduces voids but may displace fibers.
5. **Catalyst ratio** — Too high causes exotherm and cracking; too low causes under-cure.
6. **Ambient conditions** — Temperature and humidity affect gel time and surface finish.

**Typical Mechanical Properties of Spray Up Laminate:**

| Property | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Tensile strength | **80 – 120 MPa** |
| Tensile modulus | **6 – 9 GPa** |
| Flexural strength | **120 – 160 MPa** |
| Interlaminar shear strength (ILSS) | **15 – 22 MPa** |
| Fiber volume fraction ($V_f$) | **20 % – 30 %** |
| Void content | **3 % – 6 %** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**"Compare Hand Lay-Up and Spray Up processes for boat building. Justify which process is preferred for (i) a 6 m fishing boat hull and (ii) a high-performance racing yacht hull."**

#### Part (a) — Comparative Analysis [7 Marks]

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Hand Lay-Up | Spray Up |
|---|---|---|
| Fiber form | Continuous mat / woven fabric | Chopped fibers (10–50 mm) |
| Deposition rate | 0.5 – 1 kg/min | 2 – 10 kg/min |
| Fiber volume fraction | 30 % – 45 % | 20 % – 30 % |
| Void content | 2 % – 4 % | 3 % – 6 % |
| Mechanical properties | Higher (continuous fibers) | Lower (short fibers) |
| Labor cost | High (manual mat placement) | Lower (single operator) |
| Capital cost | Very low | Low (spray gun + compressor) |
| Part size suitability | Small to medium | Large structures |
| Skill required | High | Moderate |
| Resin emissions | High (open mold) | High (open mold) |

#### Part (b) — Process Justification for Specific Boat Types [7 Marks]

**(i) 6 m Fishing Boat Hull — Spray Up Preferred (3 marks):**

Spray Up is the industry standard for small-to-medium fishing trawlers because:
- **Cost advantage**: Tooling and labor costs are 30%–50% lower than hand lay-up.
- **Production speed**: Entire hull can be laid up in 1–2 days vs. 4–6 days for hand lay-up.
- **Adequate strength**: ISO 12215-5 scantling requirements for leisure/fishing craft are met at $V_f \approx 25\%$.
- **Geometry**: Complex hull curvature (chine, transom, sheer line) easily accommodated by spray.

**(ii) High-Performance Racing Yacht Hull — Vacuum Infusion or Prepreg Preferred (4 marks):**

Spray Up is **unsuitable** for racing yacht primary structure because:
- **Mechanical property gap**: Spray Up delivers only ~50% of the stiffness-to-weight ratio needed for racing displacement.
- **Void sensitivity**: Voids cause fatigue failure under cyclic wave loading; racing yachts must achieve >10⁶ cycle life.
- **Weight penalty**: Resin-heavy laminate (~70% resin) increases displacement, reducing speed.
- **Industry practice**: America's Cup and offshore racing yachts use **prepreg + autoclave** or **vacuum infusion** with $V_f = 50\%–60\%$ and void content <1%.

**Preferred processes:** Vacuum infusion for series-produced racers; prepreg/autoclave for one-off America's Cup class.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Process Justification Questions:**
> 1. Do **not** write generic "Spray Up is cheaper" without quantifying the advantage.
> 2. Always **link the process choice to a specific mechanical or economic requirement** (e.g., "Spray Up's $V_f$ of 25% meets the ISO 12215-5 minimum for 6 m hull plating").
> 3. For "unsuitable" questions, **propose the correct alternative** — don't just criticize Spray Up.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Spray Up (KTU PENST742, Module 1):**

- ☐ **Definition:** Open-mold process; **chopped rovings + catalyzed resin** sprayed simultaneously through a gun onto a mold.
- ☐ **Fiber length:** 10–50 mm (typically 25 mm); **chopped**, not continuous.
- ☐ **Fiber volume fraction:** **20%–30%** (low — distinguishes from hand lay-up and prepreg).
- ☐ **Resin systems:** Polyester or vinyl ester + **MEKP catalyst** + cobalt accelerator.
- ☐ **Air pressure:** 2–4 bar; **deposition rate:** 2–10 kg/min.
- ☐ **Compaction:** Hand rollers (paddle, grooved) — **no vacuum, no autoclave** in basic Spray Up.
- ☐ **Void content:** 3%–6% — higher than all other FRP processes.
- ☐ **Mold prep:** Release agent + **gel coat (0.4–0.6 mm)** for cosmetic and UV-protective outer skin.
- ☐ **Cure:** Ambient or 40–60 °C; full cure ~24 h.
- ☐ **Applications in boats:** Hulls (8–20 m), deckhouses, bulkheads, sanitary modules, small craft.
- ☐ **NOT suitable for:** Primary structure of high-performance racing yachts, naval vessels, aerospace components.
- ☐ **Key formulas:** $V_f = \dfrac{W_f/\rho_f}{W_f/\rho_f + W_r/\rho_r}$ ; $t_{lam} = \dfrac{m_{dep}}{A \cdot \rho_c}$
- ☐ **Comparison hooks:** vs. Hand Lay-Up (continuous vs. chopped fibers); vs. RTM/VIP (open vs. closed mold); vs. Prepreg (wet vs. pre-impregnated).
- ☐ **Industry standards:** ISO 12215-5 (small craft hull construction scantlings); relevant for KTU design questions.
- ☐ **Sustainability note:** Spray Up emits **styrene** (VOC); modern variants include **low-styrene resins** and **closed-mold Spray Up (CSM)** for emission control.

---
