topics.title: Hand Lay-up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hand Lay-up

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hand Lay-up (Open Moulding / Contact Moulding)** is the oldest, simplest, and most widely used **open-mould composite manufacturing process** in which **liquid thermoset resin** is manually deposited over **dry reinforcement fibers** (fabric, mat, or woven roving) placed inside an open mould, followed by hand-rolling using rollers or brushes to consolidate the laminate, remove entrapped air, and ensure wet-out of the fibers. Curing occurs at ambient temperature (or with mild heat) without applied external pressure.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for *Composite Boat Design* (PENST742), hand lay-up is classified as a **primary low-pressure, room-temperature processing route** for **FRP (Fibre-Reinforced Plastic) hulls, decks, and small watercraft**, where capital investment is low and part size can be very large (limited only by workshop floor area).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of making a paper-mâché school project.** You take a bowl (the mould), brush glue (resin) onto it, lay a strip of paper (fiberglass mat), then brush more glue over it and roll it flat with a brush to remove bubbles. Each layer is added one-by-one by hand, then it dries and hardens into a rigid shell. **Hand lay-up in boat building is the industrial, high-strength, marine-grade version of exactly this process** — except the "paper" is fiberglass/chopped strand mat and the "glue" is polyester or vinylester resin.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Material Constants

- **Mould surface area:** typically **2 m² – 200 m²** for marine structures.
- **Resin types:** Polyester, Vinylester, Epoxy (epoxy less common in hand lay-up due to cost).
- **Typical fibre volume fraction ($V_f$):** **0.20 – 0.35** (low compared to vacuum/press moulding).
- **Void content:** typically **2 % – 5 %** by volume.
- **Standard tooling:** **Gelcoat** (0.4 – 0.6 mm) is first sprayed onto mould for the cosmetic and barrier layer.

## 1.4 When is Hand Lay-up Preferred?

| Criterion | Hand Lay-up Suitability |
|---|---|
| Part size | **Very large** (boat hulls > 10 m) |
| Production volume | **Low** (1 – 500 units/yr) |
| Capital cost | **Very low** (no press/autoclave) |
| Skill dependence | **High** (operator-sensitive) |
| Mechanical performance | **Moderate** (lower $V_f$) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle (Step-wise Logic)

1. **Mould Preparation:** The mould (typically a female mould for hulls) is cleaned, polished, and treated with a **release agent** (wax/PVA) to ensure part demoulding.
2. **Gelcoat Application:** A pigmented **gelcoat** resin (≈ 0.4 – 0.6 mm thick) is sprayed or brushed onto the mould surface. This becomes the outer cosmetic/weather-resistant skin of the boat.
3. **Lay-up of Reinforcement:** Dry reinforcement layers — **chopped strand mat (CSM)**, **woven roving (WR)**, or **woven fabric** — are placed onto the gelcoat while it is still tacky.
4. **Resin Application:** Catalyzed liquid resin (with **MEKP** initiator for polyester, 1 % – 2 % by weight) is poured, brushed, or rolled onto the reinforcement.
5. **Consolidation (De-bulking):** **Paddle rollers, disc rollers, or brush rollers** are used to:
    - Wet-out the fibres completely,
    - Remove **entrapped air bubbles** (defects → voids → strength loss),
    - Compact the laminate to nominal thickness.
6. **Curing:** Exothermic cure occurs at **room temperature (25 °C – 35 °C)**. Post-cure may be applied for higher $T_g$.
7. **Demoulding & Trimming:** Part is removed from the mould and excess flash is trimmed.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why gelcoat?** Pure resin is not UV/weather-resistant; gelcoat protects against osmosis, UV, and abrasion.
- **Why roller?** Voids reduce interlaminar shear strength (ILSS) by up to **30 % per 1 % void content**.
- **Why room temperature?** Allows processing of large hulls without expensive autoclaves; suits low-volume boat-building economics.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fibre Volume Fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{\rho_m \, W_f}{\rho_f \, W_f + \rho_m \, W_m}$ | Where $W_f$, $W_m$ = weight fractions; $\rho_f$, $\rho_m$ = densities | dimensionless |
| 2 | Resin-to-Glass Ratio | $\dfrac{W_r}{W_g} \approx 2.0 \text{ to } 3.0$ | Typical ratio for CSM hand lay-up | — |
| 3 | Laminate Thickness | $t = \dfrac{n \cdot \rho_a}{\rho_f \cdot V_f}$ | $n$ = no. of plies; $\rho_a$ = aerial weight of fabric | mm |
| 4 | Void Content | $V_v = 1 - (V_f + V_m)$ | From rule of mixtures; voids degrade strength | dimensionless |
| 5 | Rule of Mixtures (density) | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Composite density | kg/m³ |
| 6 | Rule of Mixtures (modulus, longitudinal) | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Iso-strain (Voigt) model | GPa |
| 7 | Cure Shrinkage (Polyester) | $\varepsilon_{sh} \approx 5\%\text{–}8\%$ | Causes residual stresses, warpage | % |
| 8 | Exotherm Peak Temp | $T_{peak} \approx T_{amb} + \dfrac{Q_{rxn}}{m \cdot c_p}$ | Critical for thick laminates | °C |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Boat Building

- Used for **hulls, decks, hatches, console panels, mast steps, dagger-board cases** of sailing yachts, fishing boats, kayaks, and rescue craft.
- Enables moulding of **large, complex double-curvature surfaces** impossible with press moulding.
- Low tooling cost → allows **prototype and one-off custom boat construction** within KTU student projects and small-scale Kerala boatyards.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fibre Volume Fraction from Weight Fractions

We are given a hand-laid laminate with the following data from a KTU-style problem:
- Mass of glass fabric = $W_f = 0.450$ kg
- Mass of resin matrix = $W_m = 0.550$ kg
- Density of E-glass fibre = $\rho_f = 2540$ kg/m³
- Density of polyester resin (cured) = $\rho_m = 1240$ kg/m³

**Goal:** Derive $V_f$.

### Step 1 — Write Volume of Each Constituent

$$
V_f = \dfrac{m_f}{\rho_f} \quad \text{and} \quad V_m = \dfrac{m_m}{\rho_m}
$$

### Step 2 — Define Fibre Volume Fraction as Ratio of Fibre Volume to Total Volume

$$
V_f^{\text{(vol fraction)}} = \dfrac{V_f}{V_f + V_m} = \dfrac{\dfrac{m_f}{\rho_f}}{\dfrac{m_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{m_m}{\rho_m}}
$$

### Step 3 — Multiply Numerator and Denominator by $\rho_f \rho_m$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f &= \dfrac{m_f \, \rho_m}{m_f \, \rho_m + m_m \, \rho_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f &= \dfrac{0.450 \times 1240}{(0.450 \times 1240) + (0.550 \times 2540)} \\
&= \dfrac{558.0}{558.0 + 1397.0} \\
&= \dfrac{558.0}{1955.0} \\
&= 0.2854
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Express as Percentage

$$
V_f \approx 28.5\%
$$

This falls neatly within the typical hand lay-up range (**20 % – 35 %**), validating the calculation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Laminate Thickness from Aerial Weight

Given:
- 4 plies of CSM, each with aerial weight $\rho_a = 450$ g/m² = $0.450$ kg/m²
- Fibre volume fraction $V_f = 0.285$

### Step 1 — Define Aerial Weight Relation

$$
t = \dfrac{n \cdot \rho_a}{\rho_f \cdot V_f}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \dfrac{4 \times 0.450}{2540 \times 0.285} \\
&= \dfrac{1.80}{723.9} \\
&= 2.486 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to mm

$$
t \approx 2.49 \text{ mm}
$$

This is typical for a **non-structural interior boat panel**. A structural hull laminate is usually **6 mm – 25 mm**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Composite Density (Rule of Mixtures)

### Step 1 — Statement of RoM

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)
$$

### Step 2 — Substitution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_c &= (2540)(0.285) + (1240)(0.715) \\
&= 723.9 + 886.6 \\
&= 1610.5 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Cross-check via Mass/Volume

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{total} &= \dfrac{m_f}{\rho_f} + \dfrac{m_m}{\rho_m} \\
&= \dfrac{0.450}{2540} + \dfrac{0.550}{1240} \\
&= 1.772 \times 10^{-4} + 4.435 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 6.207 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\rho_c = \dfrac{1.000}{6.207 \times 10^{-4}} = 1611.1 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

> ✔ Matches within rounding — derivation verified.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Hand Lay-up Variants

| Parameter | Hand Lay-up (Open) | Hand Lay-up + Vacuum Bag | Wet Lay-up + Roller Only |
|---|---|---|---|
| External pressure | **0 kPa** (atmospheric only) | 70 – 100 kPa (vacuum) | 0 kPa |
| $V_f$ achievable | 0.20 – 0.30 | 0.35 – 0.45 | 0.20 – 0.30 |
| Void content | 3 – 5 % | 0.5 – 1.5 % | 3 – 5 % |
| ILSS (relative) | 1.0× | 1.3× – 1.5× | 1.0× |
| Equipment cost | **Very low** | Moderate | Very low |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start Mould Preparation] --> nodeB[Apply Release Agent Wax PVA]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Spray or Brush Gelcoat 0.4 to 0.6 mm]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Lay Reinforcement CSM WR Fabric]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Pour Catalyzed Resin MEKP Initiator]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Hand Roll with Paddle Roller]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Defect Inspection Air Bubbles}
    nodeG -->|Defects Found| nodeE
    nodeG -->|Defects Cleared| nodeH[Allow Room Temp Cure 24 hr]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Demould Part]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Trim Flash and Edges]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Quality Inspection Visual Ultrasonic]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Final Boat Component Ready]
```

## 4.2 Laminate Cross-Section Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph marineLam[Marine FRP Laminate Section]
        nodeM1[Outer Gelcoat Layer UV and Osmosis Barrier]
        nodeM2[First CSM Layer Chopped Strand Mat 450 gsm]
        nodeM3[Woven Roving Layer Structural Backing]
        nodeM4[Second CSM Layer Print Blocker]
        nodeM5[Inner Gelcoat or Flowcoat Surface]
    end
    nodeM1 --> nodeM2 --> nodeM3 --> nodeM4 --> nodeM5
```

## 4.3 Tooling & Equipment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT1[Female Hull Mould FRP or Timber] --> nodeT2[Hand Roller and Disc Roller Set]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3[Brush Set and Resin Bucket]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4[MEKP Catalyst Dispenser]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT5[Fiberglass Roll Stock CSM WR]
    nodeT5 --> nodeT6[Trim and Cutting Tools]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

### Q1. Define hand lay-up process. List any two advantages and two limitations for boat building.

**Model Answer (10–12 lines):**
Hand lay-up is an open-mould composite manufacturing process where liquid thermoset resin is manually applied over dry reinforcement layers placed inside a mould and consolidated using rollers without external pressure. **Advantages:** (i) Low capital cost and tooling investment — ideal for small Kerala boatyards, (ii) Suitable for very large parts like hulls (> 10 m) limited only by workshop size, (iii) Simple process with low skill threshold for entry-level workers. **Limitations:** (i) Low fibre volume fraction (20 % – 30 %) and high void content (3 – 5 %), reducing mechanical strength, (ii) Operator-dependent quality and high resin wastage through styrene emissions, (iii) Long cure times and emission of VOCs — health/environmental concern.

---

### Q2. What is the function of gelcoat in a hand-laid boat hull?

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**
Gelcoat is a pigmented, thixotropic polyester or vinylester resin layer (≈ 0.4 – 0.6 mm thick) applied first onto the mould surface before reinforcement lay-up. Its functions are: (i) provides a smooth, glossy outer cosmetic finish, (ii) acts as a **UV and weather barrier**, (iii) protects fibres from **osmosis and water ingress** (critical for marine service), (iv) improves abrasion and chemical resistance, (v) hides the fibre print-through that would otherwise appear on the outer surface. It must be fully cured (tack-free) before resin impregnation begins.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End Semester Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure of hand lay-up process** with a neat sketch for manufacturing a small fishing boat hull. Highlight the role of gelcoat, release agent, and consolidation rollers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hand-laid laminate uses 6 plies of chopped strand mat (aerial weight = 600 g/m² each). The fibre weight fraction is 0.45. Densities are: $\rho_f = 2540$ kg/m³, $\rho_m = 1240$ kg/m³. Calculate (i) fibre volume fraction $V_f$, (ii) laminate thickness $t$, (iii) composite density $\rho_c$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Mould Preparation:** Female mould cleaned, polished. **Release agent (wax/PVA)** applied in 3 – 4 coats to ensure clean demoulding.
2. **Gelcoat Application:** Pigmented polyester gelcoat (≈ 0.5 mm) sprayed onto mould — provides cosmetic and barrier surface.
3. **Reinforcement Lay-up:** Dry CSM layers are cut to shape and laid onto the still-tacky gelcoat.
4. **Resin Application:** Catalyzed resin (1.5 % MEKP by weight) poured over fabric.
5. **Consolidation:** **Paddle rollers** used to wet-out fibres and expel air. **Disc rollers** for corners and curves.
6. **Curing:** Ambient temperature, 24 hours.
7. **Demoulding & Finishing:** Hull removed, trimmed, post-cured.

**Role summary:** Release agent → demouldability; Gelcoat → aesthetics + barrier; Rollers → wet-out + de-airing + thickness control.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Fibre volume fraction:**

Convert weight fraction to volume fraction:

$$
V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m} = \dfrac{0.45 / 2540}{0.45 / 2540 + 0.55 / 1240}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f &= \dfrac{1.7717 \times 10^{-4}}{1.7717 \times 10^{-4} + 4.4355 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \dfrac{1.7717}{6.2072} \\
&= 0.2854 \approx 28.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Laminate thickness:**

$$
t = \dfrac{n \cdot \rho_a}{\rho_f \cdot V_f} = \dfrac{6 \times 0.600}{2540 \times 0.2854}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \dfrac{3.60}{724.9} \\
&= 4.967 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 4.97 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Composite density (RoM):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_c &= \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f) \\
&= 2540(0.2854) + 1240(0.7146) \\
&= 724.9 + 886.1 \\
&= 1611.0 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Answers:** $V_f = 0.285$, $t = 4.97$ mm, $\rho_c = 1611$ kg/m³.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and applications** of hand lay-up process with respect to **composite boat construction**. Compare it briefly with the vacuum bagging method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for **fibre volume fraction in terms of weight fractions and densities**. Using the derived expression, compute $V_f$ for a laminate with $W_f = 0.6$ kg, $W_m = 0.4$ kg, $\rho_f = 2560$ kg/m³, $\rho_m = 1200$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Advantages:** (i) Lowest tooling cost among FRP processes, (ii) No size limitation, (iii) Simple, (iv) Easy repair in field.

**Limitations:** (i) Quality depends on operator skill, (ii) Low $V_f$ (20 % – 30 %), (iii) High void content reduces ILSS, (iv) Styrene emissions — health hazard, (v) Slow production rate.

**Applications in boat building:** Hulls (up to 25 m), decks, cabin tops, console panels, mast steps, hatches, small dinghies, kayaks.

**Comparison with vacuum bagging:**

| Feature | Hand Lay-up | Vacuum Bagging |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure | Atmospheric (≈ 100 kPa) | 70 – 100 kPa additional (consolidation) |
| $V_f$ | 20 % – 30 % | 35 % – 45 % |
| Void content | 3 % – 5 % | 0.5 % – 1.5 % |
| Strength | Moderate | Higher by 30 % – 50 % |
| Cost | Low | Moderate (consumables + pump) |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation:**

Let total volume $V = V_f + V_m$, with $V_f = m_f / \rho_f$, $V_m = m_m / \rho_m$.

$$
V_f^{\text{(vol frac)}} = \dfrac{m_f / \rho_f}{(m_f / \rho_f) + (m_m / \rho_m)}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\rho_f \rho_m$:

$$
V_f = \dfrac{m_f \rho_m}{m_f \rho_m + m_m \rho_f}
$$

**Numerical substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f &= \dfrac{0.6 \times 1200}{(0.6 \times 1200) + (0.4 \times 2560)} \\
&= \dfrac{720}{720 + 1024} \\
&= \dfrac{720}{1744} \\
&= 0.4128 \approx 41.3\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This value exceeds the typical hand lay-up range — indicating either a glass-rich lay-up schedule or that **vacuum consolidation or press moulding** was used in conjunction.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing weight fraction with volume fraction** — they are *not* equal; must convert using densities (most common 1-mark loss).
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — aerial weight in g/m² vs kg/m²; thickness in m vs mm (2-mark loss).
> 3. **Missing the role of gelcoat** — students often only mention cosmetic function, forgetting UV and osmosis barrier (1 – 2 mark loss).
> 4. **Writing steps in random order** — KTU expects the logical sequence: mould prep → release → gelcoat → reinforcement → resin → roll → cure → demould. Marks are awarded sequentially.
> 5. **No sketch/diagram in 14-mark answer** — full marks require a **labelled process sketch** OR equivalent Mermaid/flowchart (loss of up to 2 marks).
> 6. **Skipping consolidation rollers** — many answers omit this step, but it is the **most operationally critical** step in hand lay-up.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Hand lay-up is the **most economical open-mould process** for FRP boat building.
- ✅ Standard sequence: **Mould → Release → Gelcoat → Reinforcement → Resin → Roll → Cure → Demould**.
- ✅ Fibre volume fraction in hand lay-up is **typically 20 % – 30 %**.
- ✅ Composite density formula (Rule of Mixtures): $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$.
- ✅ Laminate thickness: $t = \dfrac{n \rho_a}{\rho_f V_f}$.
- ✅ **Gelcoat thickness:** 0.4 – 0.6 mm; applied **before** reinforcement.
- ✅ **Release agent:** wax or PVA; applied **before** gelcoat.
- ✅ **Void content target:** < 5 %; achieved by thorough rolling.
- ✅ **Cure:** ambient temperature with MEKP initiator (1 % – 2 % by weight).
- ✅ Hand lay-up is **unsuitable for high $V_f$ aerospace-grade composites** — use autoclave instead.
- ✅ Always convert **weight fractions to volume fractions using densities** — KTU examiners test this concept repeatedly.
- ✅ When computing thickness, ensure **units of aerial weight (kg/m²)** match those of density (kg/m³) — yielding thickness in metres.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matched Dies Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Matched Dies Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Matched Die Moulding** (also referred to as **Matched Metal Die Moulding** or **Compression Moulding with Matched Dies**) is a **closed-mould composite manufacturing process** in which a measured charge of fibre-reinforced thermoset or thermoplastic material is placed between two precisely machined, heated metallic mould halves (the *matched die set*). The mould is then closed under high hydraulic or mechanical pressure, forcing the material to flow and conform to the cavity geometry, where it is simultaneously consolidated and cured to produce a finished, dimensionally accurate composite component with finished surfaces on both sides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> Matched die moulding is a high-pressure, closed-mould composite fabrication technique wherein two hardened, ground, and polished steel (or aluminium) mould halves register against each other (the "match") with a controlled gap equal to the part thickness, applying simultaneous heat (~120 °C – 160 °C) and pressure (~0.5 – 10 MPa) to convert a pre-weighed charge of bulk moulding compound (BMC), sheet moulding compound (SMC), prepreg, or glass mat thermoplastics (GMT) into a fully cured structural part.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine making a **sandwich using a heavy cast-iron panini press**:
1. You place the filling (the composite charge) on the bottom plate.
2. You bring the top plate down with a fixed gap so the bread thickness is uniform.
3. The press applies heat (from embedded heating elements) and pressure (from the hydraulic ram).
4. After a few minutes, the filling is cooked, compressed to the desired thickness, and you open the press to find a perfectly formed sandwich with **both top and bottom surfaces having a finished, patterned appearance**.

The **panini press = matched die set**, the **filling = SMC/BMC/prepreg charge**, and the **finished sandwich = cured composite boat part** (e.g., a hatch cover, console, or small hull section).

## 1.3 Key Process Metrics (Standard Industrial Values)

- **Mould closing pressure:** **0.5 – 10 MPa** (typical 1 – 5 MPa for boat components)
- **Mould surface temperature:** **120 °C – 160 °C** (depending on resin system)
- **Cure cycle time:** **30 seconds – 10 minutes**
- **Charge weight accuracy:** **± 2 %** (critical for part thickness control)
- **Mould material:** **P-20 tool steel, H-13, or hard-anodized aluminium**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters for Boat Building:** Matched die moulding produces **class-A finish** surfaces on *both* sides of the part, making it ideal for visible boat components like **decks, consoles, hatches, and small hull sections** where aesthetics, dimensional repeatability, and strength are all critical.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Six Logical Phases

### Phase 1 — Mould Preparation & Charge Loading
- The matched die set (cavity + core) is cleaned and a **release agent** is sprayed.
- A **pre-weighed charge** of SMC, BMC, or prepreg is placed on the lower (female or male) cavity.
- For SMC, the charge covers **40 – 70 %** of the cavity area (called *charge coverage ratio*).

### Phase 2 — Mould Closure
- The press ram advances at high speed initially, then **slows down** during the final closure phase to avoid trapping air.
- A **breathing cycle** (slight opening for 1 – 3 seconds at ~0.3 MPa) is often included to allow volatile escape.

### Phase 3 — Heat & Pressure Application
- The heated mould surfaces (typically **135 °C – 150 °C** for polyester/vinylester SMC) transfer heat into the charge.
- The applied pressure forces the material to flow, fill the cavity, and consolidate the fibres.
- Heat initiates the **exothermic cure reaction** in the thermoset resin.

### Phase 4 — Curing
- The resin cross-links under combined heat and pressure. The curing follows **Arrhenius kinetics**.
- Cure time depends on mould temperature, part thickness, and resin reactivity.

### Phase 5 — Mould Opening & Part Ejection
- The press opens, and **ejector pins** push the part out of the cavity.
- For undercuts, **split-cavity moulds** or side-action mechanisms are used.

### Phase 6 — Trimming & Post-Processing
- Excess material (flash) is trimmed by **pneumatic presses or CNC routers**.
- For boat parts, secondary operations include drilling, painting, and gel-coat finishing.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" and "How"

| Step | Why It Happens | How It Is Controlled |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat application** | Activates the peroxide initiator, triggering resin cross-linking | Electric cartridge heaters or oil-circulation channels in the mould |
| **Pressure application** | (i) Forces material flow, (ii) consolidates fibres, (iii) suppresses porosity, (iv) ensures heat transfer contact | Hydraulic ram with closed-loop pressure control |
| **Mould matching (register)** | Maintains uniform part thickness and balanced surface finish | Machined locating pins and shim blocks |
| **Charge pre-weighing** | Ensures correct part weight and density; minimizes flash | Volumetric feeders or precision scales (± 2 %) |
| **Breathing cycle** | Releases trapped air and volatiles | Press control with timed dwell at intermediate pressure |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Expression | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Clamping Force** | $F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \cdot A_{cavity}$ | N or kN | Force needed from hydraulic press |
| 2 | **Cavity Pressure (rule of thumb for SMC)** | $P_{cavity} \approx 7 \text{ MPa}$ (typical) | MPa | Required for Class-A finish |
| 3 | **Cure Reaction Rate (Arrhenius)** | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T}$ | s⁻¹ | Rate of resin cross-linking |
| 4 | **Heat Required for Cure** | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + m_{resin} \cdot H_{cure}$ | J or kJ | Total energy input per cycle |
| 5 | **Charge Coverage Ratio** | $CCR = \dfrac{A_{charge}}{A_{cavity}} \times 100\%$ | % | Usually 40 – 70 % for SMC |
| 6 | **Charge Mass for Target Thickness** | $m_{charge} = \rho_{compound} \cdot V_{cavity}$ | kg | Mass of material to load |
| 7 | **Cycle Time Components** | $t_{cycle} = t_{close} + t_{cure} + t_{open} + t_{load}$ | s | Production rate determinant |
| 8 | **Material Shrinkage Compensation** | $D_{mould} = D_{part} \cdot (1 + S_{r})$ | mm | Mould dimension enlargement |
| 9 | **Deflection of Cavity (plate bending)** | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{k \cdot P \cdot a^4}{E \cdot t^3}$ | mm | Critical for stiffness design |
| 10 | **Heat Transfer by Conduction** | $q = \dfrac{k_{mould}}{\Delta x} \cdot (T_{surface} - T_{core})$ | W/m² | Determines cure uniformity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation key:** $P$ = pressure, $A$ = area, $A$ = pre-exponential factor, $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/mol·K, $T$ = absolute temperature (K), $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K), $H_{cure}$ = heat of cure (J/kg), $S_r$ = moulding shrinkage (typically 0.1 % – 0.5 %), $k_{mould}$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K), $E$ = Young's modulus of mould steel, $t$ = mould plate thickness.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **marine composites industry**, matched die moulding is the workhorse process for:
- **Small-to-medium boat hulls** (up to ~8 m) where high finish quality is required on both sides
- **Structural deck panels** for fast ferries and naval craft
- **Console, dashboard, and seat modules** for recreational boats
- **Buoyancy modules and hatch covers**
- **Propeller blade fairings** where aerodynamic / hydrodynamic finish is essential

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Compared to **hand lay-up** (open mould), matched die moulding delivers **3 – 5× higher fibre volume fractions (Vf ≈ 50 – 60 %)**, **shorter cycle times (minutes vs. hours)**, and **minimal VOC emissions** — making it a cleaner, faster, and stronger option for serial boat production.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Clamping Force for a Boat Deck Panel

**Problem Statement:**
A boat builder wants to mould an SMC boat deck panel of plan dimensions **1200 mm × 800 mm** with a uniform thickness of **4 mm**. The required cavity pressure for class-A finish is **3.5 MPa**. Calculate:
(a) The total clamping force required from the hydraulic press.
(b) The hydraulic cylinder diameter if the system pressure is 200 bar.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the projected cavity area** (the area perpendicular to the press stroke):

$$A_{cavity} = L \times W = 1.2 \text{ m} \times 0.8 \text{ m} = 0.96 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the clamping force equation:**

$$F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \cdot A_{cavity}$$

$$F_{clamp} = 3.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \times 0.96 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$F_{clamp} = 3.36 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} = 3360 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the hydraulic cylinder diameter** for system pressure of 200 bar (= 20 MPa):

$$F_{clamp} = P_{hydraulic} \cdot \dfrac{\pi \cdot D^{2}}{4}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot F_{clamp}}{\pi \cdot P_{hydraulic}}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 3.36 \times 10^{6}}{\pi \times 20 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.344 \times 10^{7}}{6.283 \times 10^{7}}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{0.2139} = 0.4625 \text{ m} \approx 463 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:**
- The required press tonnage is **336 tonnes** — easily achievable with a standard 500-tonne hydraulic press commonly found in marine composite workshops.
- A single large-diameter cylinder (≈ 460 mm) or **two smaller cylinders** can be used.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Charge Mass Calculation for a Small Boat Hull Section

**Problem Statement:**
A small boat hull section is to be compression moulded from **SMC (density = 1800 kg/m³)**. The cavity volume is **0.0025 m³** (i.e., 2.5 litres). The expected moulding shrinkage is **0.3 %**, and process loss to flash is **5 %**. Calculate:
(a) The mass of SMC charge required.
(b) The corresponding number of plies if each SMC sheet has an areal weight of **4 kg/m²** and the charge must cover 60 % of the cavity area.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Cavity volume including shrinkage compensation:**

$$V_{effective} = V_{cavity} \times (1 + S_r) = 0.0025 \times 1.003 = 0.002508 \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical charge mass (without flash loss):**

$$m_{theoretical} = \rho_{SMC} \cdot V_{effective}$$

$$m_{theoretical} = 1800 \text{ kg/m}^{3} \times 0.002508 \text{ m}^{3} = 4.514 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Charge mass accounting for flash loss (5 %):**

$$m_{charge} = \dfrac{m_{theoretical}}{1 - f_{flash}}$$

$$m_{charge} = \dfrac{4.514}{1 - 0.05} = \dfrac{4.514}{0.95}$$

$$m_{charge} = 4.752 \text{ kg} \approx 4.75 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4 — Cavity plan area** (assuming average part thickness of 4 mm):

$$A_{cavity} = \dfrac{V_{cavity}}{t_{part}} = \dfrac{0.0025}{0.004} = 0.625 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Required charge footprint (60 % coverage):**

$$A_{charge} = 0.60 \times 0.625 = 0.375 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Number of plies from areal weight:**

$$n_{plies} = \dfrac{m_{charge}}{A_{charge} \times \rho_{A,SMC}}$$

$$n_{plies} = \dfrac{4.752 \text{ kg}}{0.375 \text{ m}^{2} \times 4 \text{ kg/m}^{2}} = \dfrac{4.752}{1.50} = 3.17$$

Round up to **4 plies** to ensure full cavity fill.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cycle Time Estimation (Heat Transfer Limitation)

**Problem Statement:**
An SMC boat part has a thickness of **6 mm**. The mould surface temperature is **145 °C**, and the resin initial temperature is **25 °C**. The thermal diffusivity of SMC is **α = 1.5 × 10⁻⁷ m²/s**. Estimate the minimum cure time using the lumped-capacitance half-thickness criterion (Fourier number Fo = 0.5).

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Characteristic length (half-thickness for double-sided heating):**

$$L_c = \dfrac{t_{part}}{2} = \dfrac{0.006}{2} = 0.003 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Fourier number criterion:**

$$F_o = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot t_{cure}}{L_c^{2}} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Solve for cure time:**

$$t_{cure} = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot L_c^{2}}{\alpha}$$

$$t_{cure} = \dfrac{0.5 \times (0.003)^{2}}{1.5 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$t_{cure} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 9 \times 10^{-6}}{1.5 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-6}}{1.5 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$t_{cure} = 30 \text{ seconds}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **heat-soak time only**. Total cycle time must add mould closing (~5 s), breathing (~3 s), opening (~4 s), and part ejection/loading (~10 s), giving a realistic **cycle time of ~52 – 60 seconds per part**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Sensitivity Table

| Parameter | Symbol | Effect of Increasing | Practical Range (Boat SMC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mould temperature | $T_{mould}$ | ↓ Cure time, ↑ Surface quality risk (pre-cure) | 135 °C – 155 °C |
| Cavity pressure | $P_{cavity}$ | ↓ Porosity, ↑ Flash, ↓ Fibre wash | 2 – 7 MPa |
| Charge coverage | CCR | ↑ Flow distance, ↑ Weld lines if too high | 40 % – 70 % |
| Cure time | $t_{cure}$ | ↑ Degree of cure, ↓ Productivity | 30 s – 5 min |
| Mould plate thickness | $t_{plate}$ | ↓ Deflection, ↑ Heat capacity | 50 mm – 150 mm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Matched Die Moulding Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start of Cycle] --> B[Clean and Apply Release Agent to Die Set]
    B --> C[Pre-weigh SMC/BMC/Prepreg Charge]
    C --> D[Load Charge into Lower Cavity]
    D --> E[Rapid Mould Closure Phase]
    E --> F[Breathing Cycle at Intermediate Pressure]
    F --> G[Apply Full Heat and Pressure for Curing]
    G --> H[Hold at Cure Temperature for Required Duration]
    H --> I[Mould Opening Phase]
    I --> J[Ejector Pins Release Finished Part]
    J --> K[Trimming and Post-processing]
    K --> L{More Parts Required?}
    L -- Yes --> B
    L -- No --> M[End of Production Run]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Matched Die Press System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PressAssembly[Hydraulic Press Assembly]
        HPU[Hydraulic Power Unit 200 bar] --> PCV[Pressure Control Valve]
        PCV --> RAM[Main Hydraulic Ram]
        RAM --> UPPER[Upper Die Half - Heated]
    end

    subgraph DieSystem[Matched Die Set]
        UPPER --> CAVITY[Mould Cavity with Charge]
        CAVITY --> LOWER[Lower Die Half - Heated]
        LOWER --> EJECT[Ejector Pin Array]
    end

    subgraph ThermalSystem[Thermal Control System]
        HEATER[Electric/Oil Heaters] --> UPPER
        HEATER --> LOWER
        SENSOR[Thermocouples at Multiple Zones] --> CONTROLLER[PID Temperature Controller]
        CONTROLLER --> HEATER
    end

    subgraph SafetySystem[Safety and PLC System]
        PLC[Programmable Logic Controller] --> HPU
        PLC --> PCV
        PLC --> SENSOR
        PLC --> GUARD[Light Curtain and Safety Gates]
    end

    EJECT --> OUT[Finished Composite Boat Part]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Force and Pressure Distribution in a Matched Die

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph TopHalf[Upper Platen Assembly]
        TOPPLATE[Upper Steel Platen]
    end

    subgraph CavityRegion[Mould Cavity Region]
        TOPCAVITY[Upper Die Cavity Surface]
        CHARGE[Composite Charge SMC/BMC]
        BOTCAVITY[Lower Die Cavity Surface]
    end

    subgraph BottomHalf[Lower Platen Assembly]
        BOTPLATE[Lower Steel Platen with Ejectors]
        EJPIN[Ejector Pins]
    end

    TOPPLATE --> TOPCAVITY
    TOPCAVITY --> CHARGE
    CHARGE --> BOTCAVITY
    BOTCAVITY --> BOTPLATE
    BOTPLATE --- EJPIN

    FORCEIN[Clamping Force F] -.applies to.-> TOPPLATE
    PRESSPROP[Pressure Distribution Across Cavity] -.equals.-> CHARGE
```

## 4.4 Material-Flow Logic Diagram — SMC Charge Behavior During Moulding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LOAD[Pre-weighed SMC Charge] --> HEAT[Heat Penetrates from Die Surfaces]
    HEAT --> SOFT[Resin Viscosity Drops]
    SOFT --> FLOW[Material Flows Under Pressure]
    FLOW --> FILL[Cavity Fill Phase 70 to 100 percent coverage]
    FILL --> COMP[Compression and Consolidation Phase]
    COMP --> CURE[Cross-linking Initiates by Peroxide]
    CURE --> HARD[Resin Hardens Part Achieves Final Geometry]
    HARD --> EJECT[Mould Opens and Part is Ejected]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]`

**Define matched die moulding. List any four advantages of the process over hand lay-up in composite boat construction.**

**Model Answer:**

Matched die moulding is a closed-mould composite manufacturing process where a pre-measured charge of fibre-reinforced thermoset material is compressed between two heated, registered metallic mould halves to produce a cured part with finished surfaces on both sides.

**Four advantages over hand lay-up:**
1. **Higher fibre volume fraction** (50 – 60 % vs. 25 – 35 %) leading to greater strength and stiffness.
2. **Shorter cycle times** (minutes per part vs. hours), enabling mass production.
3. **Better surface finish** on both faces (class-A finish), reducing secondary finishing work.
4. **Lower VOC emissions** and improved worker safety due to closed-mould operation.
5. **Greater dimensional consistency** across production batches.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]`

**What is the function of a "breathing cycle" in matched die moulding? At what stage of the closure stroke is it typically executed?**

**Model Answer:**

A breathing cycle is a **brief dwell (1 – 3 seconds)** at intermediate pressure (typically **0.3 – 0.5 MPa**) during the final closure phase of the mould.

**Functions:**
- Allows **trapped air and volatile gases** to escape from the cavity before full pressure is applied.
- Prevents **porosity, blisters, and burn marks** in the finished part.
- Provides a **safety margin** against dry spots and incomplete wet-out.

It is executed just **before the final high-pressure phase**, when the mould gap is approximately 2 – 3 times the final part thickness.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU ESE - KTU Pattern Module 1 Internal Choice]`

#### **(a)** Explain the working principle of matched die moulding with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of heat, pressure, and cure time on the quality of the finished composite part. **(7 Marks)**

#### **(b)** A boat hull section is to be compression moulded from SMC. The cavity has plan dimensions 1500 mm × 1000 mm and is 5 mm thick. Required cavity pressure is 4 MPa. Calculate: **(i)** Total clamping force, **(ii)** Number of plies needed if SMC sheet areal density is 5 kg/m² and charge coverage is 65 %, **(iii)** Mass of charge assuming flash loss of 4 % and shrinkage of 0.2 %. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Working Principle:**
Matched die moulding uses **two precisely machined metallic mould halves** registered against each other. A pre-weighed charge of SMC/BMC/prepreg is placed in the lower cavity. The press closes, applies heat and pressure simultaneously, the resin flows to fill the cavity, cures to a rigid part, and the mould opens for ejection.

**Role of the Three Parameters (in a table for clarity):**

| Parameter | Effect on Part Quality |
|---|---|
| **Heat (135 – 155 °C)** | Initiates cure; too low → under-cure (weak part), too high → pre-cure at surface, voids, discoloration |
| **Pressure (2 – 7 MPa)** | Consolidates fibres, eliminates voids; too low → porosity, too high → fibre wash and excessive flash |
| **Cure time (30 s – 5 min)** | Allows complete cross-linking; too short → tacky part, too long → productivity loss |

The **combined effect** of these three parameters determines the **degree of cure (α)**, **void content (Vv)**, and **fibre volume fraction (Vf)** — all of which directly govern mechanical performance.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Step 1 — Cavity plan area:**

$$A_{cavity} = 1.5 \text{ m} \times 1.0 \text{ m} = 1.5 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Clamping force:**

$$F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \cdot A_{cavity} = 4 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \times 1.5 \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$F_{clamp} = 6.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} = 6000 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Cavity volume:**

$$V_{cavity} = A_{cavity} \times t_{part} = 1.5 \text{ m}^{2} \times 0.005 \text{ m} = 0.0075 \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical mass (without losses), ρ_SMC = 1800 kg/m³:**

$$m_{theoretical} = 1800 \times 0.0075 = 13.5 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 5 — Mass including shrinkage (0.2 %) and flash (4 %):**

$$m_{charge} = \dfrac{m_{theoretical} \cdot (1 + S_r)}{1 - f_{flash}} = \dfrac{13.5 \times 1.002}{0.96}$$

$$m_{charge} = \dfrac{13.527}{0.96} = 14.09 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6 — Charge footprint area at 65 % coverage:**

$$A_{charge} = 0.65 \times 1.5 = 0.975 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Number of plies:**

$$n_{plies} = \dfrac{m_{charge}}{A_{charge} \times \rho_{A,SMC}} = \dfrac{14.09}{0.975 \times 5} = \dfrac{14.09}{4.875} = 2.89$$

Round up to **3 plies**.

**Final Answers:**
(i) $F_{clamp} = 6000$ kN
(ii) **3 plies**
(iii) $m_{charge} = 14.09$ kg

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU ESE - KTU Pattern Module 1 Internal Choice]`

#### **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, describe the major components of a hydraulic press used for matched die moulding. Explain how mould alignment and parallelism are maintained during the operation. **(7 Marks)**

#### **(b)** Discuss the various types of raw materials used in matched die moulding (SMC, BMC, GMT, prepregs). Compare their suitability for marine structural applications based on fibre content, cycle time, and mechanical properties. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Major Components of a Hydraulic Press:**

1. **Hydraulic Power Unit (HPU)** — supplies pressurized oil (typically 200 – 350 bar).
2. **Main Cylinder / Ram** — converts hydraulic pressure into linear force to close the mould.
3. **Platen (Top and Bottom)** — rigid steel plates that carry the mould halves.
4. **Heating System** — electric cartridge heaters or oil-circulation channels embedded in the platens.
5. **Control System (PLC + PID controllers)** — manages pressure, temperature, stroke position, and dwell timing.
6. **Ejector System** — hydraulic ejector pins beneath the lower platen to release the part.
7. **Safety Devices** — light curtains, two-hand controls, and pressure relief valves.

**Mould Alignment & Parallelism:**
- **Guide pillars (4)** and **bushings** maintain horizontal alignment.
- **Tie bars (4)** keep platens parallel under load.
- **Self-levelling hydraulic cylinders** use individual pressure regulators at each corner.
- **Parallelism tolerance** is typically maintained within **0.05 mm across 1000 mm**.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Comparison of Raw Materials for Marine Applications:**

| Property | SMC (Sheet Moulding Compound) | BMC (Bulk Moulding Compound) | GMT (Glass Mat Thermoplastic) | Prepregs |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fibre type | Chopped glass, 25 – 50 mm | Chopped glass, 6 – 12 mm | Continuous random mat | Unidirectional / woven |
| Fibre volume (%) | 20 – 35 | 15 – 25 | 30 – 45 | 50 – 65 |
| Resin type | Polyester / Vinylester | Polyester | Polypropylene / Nylon | Epoxy / Vinylester |
| Cycle time | 1 – 5 min | 1 – 3 min | 30 s – 2 min | 5 – 30 min |
| Marine suitability | **High** (decks, hatches) | Moderate (small fittings) | **High** (structural panels) | **Very High** (hulls, structural) |
| Cost | Low | Lowest | Moderate | High |
| Class-A finish | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Requires paint |

**Conclusion for Boat Design:**
- For **large structural hulls** → **Prepregs** (highest fibre content, best mechanicals).
- For **deck panels and consoles** → **SMC** (best balance of cost, finish, and strength).
- For **impact-resistant fenders and bumpers** → **GMT** (tough thermoplastic behaviour).
- For **small hardware and fittings** → **BMC** (lowest cost, complex shapes).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — Students frequently write clamping force in Pa·m² but forget to convert to kN or tonnes. Always express final answer in standard engineering units (kN, tonnes, MPa).
> 2. **Ignoring flash loss** — Real charge mass is **always higher** than the theoretical cavity mass; missing the flash factor loses 1 – 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing SMC with BMC** — SMC is supplied as sheets; BMC is supplied as a bulk dough. Material handling differences must be mentioned.
> 4. **Skipping the breathing cycle** — Many answers omit this critical phase, losing marks on the "process steps" question.
> 5. **Forgetting shrinkage compensation** — Mould dimensions must be larger than the final part dimensions by the shrinkage factor (~0.1 % – 0.5 %).
> 6. **No mention of charge coverage ratio** — Examiners expect students to know the 40 – 70 % range for SMC.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Matched die moulding is a CLOSED-MOULD, HIGH-PRESSURE process** that produces composite parts with finished surfaces on **both sides**.
- **Key industrial metrics:** Pressure **0.5 – 10 MPa**, Temperature **120 – 160 °C**, Cycle time **30 s – 10 min**.
- **Clamping force equation:** $F_{clamp} = P_{cavity} \cdot A_{cavity}$ — the fundamental sizing equation for press selection.
- **Charge coverage ratio (CCR) for SMC** is typically **40 – 70 %** of cavity plan area.
- **Charge mass = (cavity mass × shrinkage factor) / (1 − flash loss)** — never forget the flash and shrinkage corrections.
- **The breathing cycle** (1 – 3 s dwell at ~0.3 MPa) is critical for venting volatiles and avoiding porosity.
- **Mould materials** are typically P-20 tool steel (for moderate volumes) or hard-anodized aluminium (for prototypes).
- **Arrhenius kinetics** govern the cure reaction: $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / R T}$ — higher temperature exponentially speeds up cure.
- **Marine applications** include boat hulls (small to medium), decks, consoles, hatches, and structural panels.
- **SMC = Sheet** (used as plies), **BMC = Bulk** (dough-like), **GMT = Glass Mat Thermoplastic**, **Prepregs = Pre-impregnated** fibres.
- **Advantages over hand lay-up:** higher Vf, faster cycles, closed-mould (lower emissions), two finished sides, better consistency.
- **Disadvantages:** high tooling cost, limited to smaller parts (press size limits), less design flexibility than hand lay-up.
- **Cycle time = closing + breathing + curing + opening + loading/unloading** — total ~1 – 5 minutes per part for typical boat SMC.
- **Fourier number criterion (Fo ≈ 0.5)** gives a quick estimate of cure time based on thermal diffusivity and part thickness.
- **Mould parallelism tolerance ≈ 0.05 mm per 1000 mm** — critical for uniform part thickness.
- **Always round UP the number of plies** calculated from the charge equation to ensure complete cavity fill.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pultrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pultrusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Pultrusion** is a continuous, automated, closed-mold manufacturing process for producing fiber-reinforced polymer (FRP) composite profiles of constant cross-section. The term *pultrusion* is a portmanteau of **"pull"** and **"extrusion"**, signifying that the reinforcement is mechanically *pulled* (as opposed to *pushed* in extrusion) through a heated steel die where thermoset resin impregnation and curing take place simultaneously, yielding a cured, dimensionally stable structural profile.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PENST742):** Pultrusion is a continuous molding process in which dry reinforcing fibers are drawn (pulled) through a resin impregnation bath and then through a heated shaping die, wherein polymerization and consolidation occur to yield constant-cross-section composite profiles used in marine structural applications.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine making **continuous spaghetti, but with fiberglass instead of dough**. 

In a pasta machine, dough is *pushed* through a die to form long strands. Now flip the process: imagine long glass fibers (like thousands of thin, parallel fishing lines) being *pulled* through a tub of liquid plastic resin, then through a hot metal mold shaped like the desired boat part (say, an I-beam or a tube). The heat cures the plastic while the fiber bundle is still inside the mold, and what comes out the other end is a hard, lightweight, rock-solid structural shape that can run continuously for hundreds of meters before being cut into boat-frame pieces.

**Real-world marine analogy:** Every commercial GRP (Glass Reinforced Plastic) boat you have seen on Kerala's backwaters or in Cochin shipyards has pultruded parts hidden inside — mast tubes, handrail stanchions, hull stringers, ladder rungs, and rudder stocks are commonly pultruded.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Pultrusion Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Line speed:** typically **0.3 – 1.5 m/min** (slow, but continuous)
> - **Die temperature:** typically **120 °C – 180 °C** for polyester resins
> - **Resin systems:** Polyester, Vinyl Ester, Epoxy, Phenolic
> - **Fibers:** E-glass, S-glass, Carbon, Aramid (Kevlar)
> - **Typical fiber volume fraction (Vf):** **45 – 65 %** (much higher than hand lay-up's 25 – 35 %)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' and 'How' — Step-by-Step Process Logic

Pultrusion is essentially a **7-stage linear manufacturing chain**. Each stage must be tightly controlled; any deviation compounds downstream defects.

1. **Creel / Roving Rack (Stage 1):** Continuous glass-fiber rovings (bundles of 4,000–16,000 filaments per roving) are mounted on a creel. *Why?* To feed thousands of parallel tension-controlled fibers into the line without snagging.
2. **Reinforcement Pre-Placement (Stage 2):** Continuous filament mats (CFM), woven rovings, veils, or surfacing veils are interleaved with the rovings. *Why?* Roving alone gives unidirectional strength; mats add transverse (cross-wise) strength needed in boat hulls.
3. **Resin Impregnation / Wet-Out Bath (Stage 3):** The dry fiber bundle is drawn through a tank of catalyzed thermoset resin (often heated to 25–40 °C for viscosity control). *Why?* To fully wet-out fibers and coat them with the polymer matrix.
4. **Pre-Former (Stage 4):** The wetted bundle passes through a series of forming guides that compress it toward the final die cross-section (removing excess resin and squeezing out air). *Why?* Pre-compaction prevents dry spots and voids in the cured profile.
5. **Heated Curing Die (Stage 5):** The heart of the process. The bundle enters a long, chrome-plated, electrically heated steel die (typically 0.6 – 1.5 m long). Inside, the resin gels, exotherms, and fully cures via free-radical polymerization (for polyester/vinyl ester) or addition reaction (for epoxy). *Why heated?* To trigger the cure reaction at controlled rate.
6. **Pulling Mechanism (Stage 6):** Reciprocating hydraulic clamps or caterpillar tracks grip the cured profile and pull it forward at constant line speed. *Why reciprocating?* The clamp releases, returns, and re-grips — providing continuous, non-slip traction.
7. **Cut-Off Saw (Stage 7):** An in-line abrasive or diamond saw cuts the continuous profile to specified lengths as commanded by the operator.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Pultrusion's Use in Boat Design

| Boat Design Need | How Pultrusion Solves It |
|---|---|
| High strength-to-weight ratio for masts/spars | Unidirectional rovings give **V_f up to 65 %** along load axis |
| Corrosion resistance in saltwater | FRP does not rust, ideal for hull stiffeners |
| Consistent, repeatable hull frame geometry | Closed-mold die ensures ±0.2 mm tolerance |
| Long continuous stringers without joints | Continuous process yields up to **kilometre-long** pieces |
| Fire/smoke compliance (e.g., lifeboat parts) | Phenolic or fire-retardant resin variants available |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Pultrusion & Composite Profile Design**

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_f = \dfrac{A_f}{A_c}$ | Fiber volume fraction; $A_f$ = fiber cross-section area, $A_c$ = total composite area | dimensionless |
| 2 | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f \, V_f}{\rho_f \, V_f + \rho_m \, (1-V_f)}$ | Weight fraction; $\rho_f$ fiber density, $\rho_m$ matrix density | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m (1-V_f)$ | **Rule of Mixtures** (longitudinal modulus); valid for pultruded UD profiles | GPa |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_{fT}} + \dfrac{(1-V_f)}{E_m}$ | Inverse Rule of Mixtures (transverse modulus) | $\text{GPa}^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $t_{die} = \dfrac{L_{die}}{v_{line}}$ | Die residence / cure time; $L_{die}$ die length, $v_{line}$ pull speed | minutes |
| 6 | $Q_{cure} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + \Delta H_{rxn}$ | Heat balance in die; $m$ mass flow, $c_p$ specific heat, $\Delta H_{rxn}$ cure enthalpy | kW |
| 7 | $F_{pull} \geq \sigma_{friction} \cdot A_{wall} + F_{viscous}$ | Minimum pulling force; overcomes die-wall friction + viscous drag | N |
| 8 | $K = \dfrac{F_{pull}}{A_c \cdot v_{line}}$ | Pulling pressure index (process monitoring KPI) | $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s/m}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Equation 3 (Rule of Mixtures):** It is **highly accurate** for pultrusion because the process yields near-ideal unidirectional fiber alignment ($\eta_l \approx 0.95$–$1.0$), unlike hand lay-up where misalignment drops $\eta_l$ to 0.3–0.5.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Wing-spar stiffeners, helicopter rotor blade spars
- **Civil:** Bridge decks, rebar replacement, cooling tower structural members
- **Marine (your course):** Mast tubes, hull stringers, rudder posts, ladder rungs, handrails, transom stiffeners
- **Electrical:** Third-rail covers, ladder rails, cable trays
- **Sports:** Ski poles, arrow shafts, tent poles

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Longitudinal Modulus of a Pultruded Profile (Rule of Mixtures)

We start from the **iso-strain assumption**: in a unidirectional (UD) pultruded profile, both the fiber and the matrix experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon$ along the pull direction because they are perfectly bonded and loaded in parallel (like resistors in parallel, but for stiffness).

**Step 1 — Load sharing in a UD composite element:**

Consider a small slice of the pultruded cross-section of total area $A_c$. The fiber occupies area $A_f = V_f \cdot A_c$ and the matrix occupies $A_m = (1 - V_f) \cdot A_c$.

The total axial load $P$ carried by the slice is the sum of the loads carried by the fiber and the matrix:

$$
P = P_f + P_m
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Hooke's Law for each phase:**

Since $P = \sigma \cdot A = E \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A$, and iso-strain gives $\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$:

$$
P = E_f \, \varepsilon \, A_f \; + \; E_m \, \varepsilon \, A_m
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out common strain:**

$$
P = \varepsilon \left( E_f \, A_f + E_m \, A_m \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Define effective composite modulus:**

By definition, $P = E_L \, \varepsilon \, A_c$, where $E_L$ is the longitudinal Young's modulus. Equating the two expressions for $P$:

$$
E_L \, A_c = E_f \, A_f + E_m \, A_m
$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $A_c$ and substitute $V_f$:**

$$
E_L = E_f \, \dfrac{A_f}{A_c} + E_m \, \dfrac{A_m}{A_c} = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_L = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)\,}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the same form as the parallel-spring analogy, because the fiber and matrix phases are mechanically *parallel* along the loading direction.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Cure Time in the Heated Die (Residence Time)

The line speed and die length directly determine how long the resin is inside the heated die — this must equal or exceed the time required for the resin to fully gel and cure.

**Step 1 — Kinematic relation:**

If the line speed is $v_{line}$ (m/min) and the die length is $L_{die}$ (m), the time a given point on the profile spends inside the die is:

$$
t_{die} = \dfrac{L_{die}}{v_{line}}
$$

**Step 2 — Cure requirement:**

The resin manufacturer's data sheet specifies a **gel time** $t_{gel}$ and **demold / peak-exotherm time** $t_{cure}$ at a given die temperature $T_{die}$. For a defect-free profile, we require:

$$
t_{die} \geq t_{cure}(T_{die})
$$

**Step 3 — Solved worked example (typical KTU numerical):**

Given: Die length $L_{die} = 1.2$ m, line speed $v_{line} = 0.6$ m/min, and the resin system requires $t_{cure} = 1.8$ min at the set die temperature.

**Compute $t_{die}$:**

$$
t_{die} = \dfrac{1.2 \text{ m}}{0.6 \text{ m/min}} = 2.0 \text{ min}
$$

**Compare with requirement:**

$$
t_{die} = 2.0 \text{ min} \;\geq\; t_{cure} = 1.8 \text{ min} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The line speed is safe; the profile will be fully cured. If the line speed were raised to 0.8 m/min, then $t_{die} = 1.5$ min < 1.8 min, leading to under-cured, tacky profiles — a common practical defect.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Fiber Volume Fraction from Resin and Fiber Weights

In a pultrusion quality-control lab, the technician weighs the fiber input $W_f$ and the cured profile $W_c$.

**Step 1 — Mass balance:**

$$
W_c = W_f + W_m
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to volumes using densities:**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_c / \rho_c} = \dfrac{W_f \, \rho_c}{W_c \, \rho_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Composite density from rule of mixtures:**

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)
$$

**Step 4 — Worked example:**

Given: $W_f = 1.20$ kg, $W_c = 1.85$ kg, $\rho_f = 2.55 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (E-glass), $\rho_m = 1.20 \text{ g/cm}^3$ (polyester).

**Convert weights to volume shares (mass fraction first):**

$$
m_f = \dfrac{1.20}{1.85} = 0.6486
$$

**Convert mass fraction to volume fraction (using density ratio):**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{m_f / \rho_f}{m_f / \rho_f + (1 - m_f) / \rho_m}
$$

**Substitute values:**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{0.6486 / 2.55}{0.6486 / 2.55 \; + \; 0.3514 / 1.20}
$$

**Compute numerator and denominator:**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{0.2544}{0.2544 + 0.2928} = \dfrac{0.2544}{0.5472}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_f \approx 0.465 \;\; \text{or}\;\; 46.5\,\%\,}
$$

This falls within the typical pultrusion range of **45 – 65 %**, confirming a well-tuned resin bath.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pultrusion vs. Other Composite Processes

| Parameter | Hand Lay-Up | Filament Winding | **Pultrusion** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fiber volume fraction | 25 – 35 % | 50 – 65 % | **45 – 65 %** |
| Production rate | Slow (manual) | Medium | **Very high (continuous)** |
| Part geometry | Any complex shape | Axisymmetric only | **Constant cross-section only** |
| Fiber alignment control | Poor | Excellent (hoop + helical) | **Excellent (UD)** |
| Capital cost | Low | High | **High** |
| Typical boat application | Hull shell | Pressure hull, tanks | **Masts, stringers, stiffeners** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Pultrusion Line

The diagram below maps the functional flow of reinforcements and materials through the seven stages of a pultrusion line, including the feedback/control loop used by modern CNC-controlled systems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CREEL RACK\nMulti-end roving spools] --> B[GUIDE PLATES\nFiber alignment & tensioning]
    B --> C[REINFORCEMENT FEED\nCFM mat + woven roving + veil]
    C --> D[RESIN IMPREGNATION BATH\nCatalyzed thermoset resin]
    D --> E[PRE-FORMER STATION\nCompaction & air removal]
    E --> F[HEATED CURING DIE\n120 to 180 degree C controlled]
    F --> G[PULLER UNIT\nReciprocating clamps or caterpillar]
    G --> H[CUT-OFF SAW\nIn-line abrasive saw]
    H --> I[FINISHED PROFILE\nStacking & QC station]
    F -. Temperature and cure feedback .-> J[CONTROL CONSOLE\nPLC and SCADA]
    G -. Pull force telemetry .-> J
    J -. Speed and clamp actuation .-> G
    J -. Die heater power .-> F
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Cross-Section of a Typical Pultrusion Die (Tapered)

The heated die has a **tapered entrance** to accommodate resin volume shrinkage during cure (typically 4 – 7 % volumetric shrinkage for polyester). A simplified cross-section flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DIE_BLOCK["HEATED STEEL DIE BLOCK"]
        direction TB
        ENTRY["ENTRANCE BELL\nLarger cross-section\nto allow wet fibers in"]
        TAPER["TAPERED LAND\nCompensates resin shrinkage\nAngle 1 to 3 degrees"]
        LAND["STRAIGHT LAND\nCuring section\n0.6 to 1.5 m long"]
        EXIT["EXIT LAND\nFinal dimensional control\nChrome plated"]
        ENTRY --> TAPER --> LAND --> EXIT
    end
    HEATER["Electric cartridge heaters\nembedded in die walls"] -. Heat flux .-> DIE_BLOCK
    COOLER["Cooling section\nair or water spray"] --> EXIT
```

### 4.3 Process Control Feedback Loop (Modern Pultrusion)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[DIE TEMP SENSOR\nThermocouple] --> PLC
    S2[LINE SPEED SENSOR\nEncoder] --> PLC
    S3[PULL FORCE LOAD CELL] --> PLC
    PLC[PLC CONTROLLER\nPID loops active] --> A1[Die heater driver]
    PLC --> A2[Puller hydraulic servo]
    PLC --> A3[Saw trigger actuator]
    HMI[OPERATOR HMI SCREEN\nSCADA dashboard] --- PLC
    DB[QUALITY DATABASE\nPull force, temp, cure logs] --- PLC
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define pultrusion. Name any two reinforcement materials and two resin systems commonly used in pultrusion for marine applications.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Pultrusion is a continuous manufacturing process in which dry reinforcing fibers are pulled through a resin impregnation bath and then through a heated shaping die, where the resin cures to produce a constant cross-section fiber-reinforced polymer composite profile.

**Reinforcement materials (1 mark — any two):**
1. E-glass rovings
2. Carbon fiber tows
3. Aramid (Kevlar) fibers
4. Continuous filament mat (CFM)

**Resin systems (1 mark — any two):**
1. Unsaturated polyester resin (UPR)
2. Vinyl ester resin
3. Epoxy resin
4. Phenolic resin (for fire-retardant marine applications)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*List any three advantages of pultrusion over hand lay-up in boat component manufacturing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Higher and more consistent fiber volume fraction (45–65 %)** versus hand lay-up (25–35 %), giving superior specific strength.
2. **Continuous, automated production** yields long, joint-free profiles (stringers, masts) with uniform mechanical properties.
3. **Lower long-run labor cost and higher repeatability** because the closed die guarantees consistent cross-sectional dimensions (±0.2 mm), unlike manual lay-up variability.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **pultrusion process** in detail, listing the function of **each station** from creel to cut-off saw. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pultrusion line has a die of length **1.0 m** and is running at a line speed of **0.5 m/min**. The polyester resin used requires a **cure time of 2.2 min** at the operating die temperature. **(i)** Calculate the die residence time. **(ii)** State whether the profile will be fully cured. **(iii)** If the manufacturer wants to increase production rate by 25 %, what is the maximum allowable line speed? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) Process Explanation (7 marks — 1 mark per key point + 1 for diagram):**

A pultrusion line is a continuous, seven-station manufacturing system:

1. **Creel (1 mark):** Holds multiple spools of continuous rovings (typically 24–60 ends). Tensioners maintain uniform fiber tension.
2. **Reinforcement guide and pre-placement (1 mark):** Continuous filament mats, woven rovings, and surface veils are interleaved with rovings to give multi-directional strength.
3. **Resin bath (1 mark):** Fibers pass through a tank of catalyzed thermoset resin (polyester/vinyl ester at 25–40 °C). Resin viscosity and bath residence time are controlled.
4. **Pre-former (1 mark):** Squeezes out excess resin and compacts the bundle toward the final die shape, removing trapped air and preventing dry spots.
5. **Heated die (1 mark):** Chrome-plated steel die (0.6–1.5 m long) at 120–180 °C. The resin gels, exotherms, and fully cures. The die has a tapered entrance to compensate for cure shrinkage.
6. **Puller (1 mark):** Reciprocating hydraulic clamps or caterpillar tracks pull the cured profile at a constant line speed (0.3–1.5 m/min). A load cell monitors pull force in real time.
7. **Cut-off saw (1 mark):** Abrasive or diamond saw cuts the profile to specified lengths automatically.

**Block diagram (drawn by student; key feature):** Linear left-to-right flow of seven stages with resin bath marked separately.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $L_{die} = 1.0$ m
- $v_{line} = 0.5$ m/min
- $t_{cure} = 2.2$ min

**(i) Die residence time (3 marks):**

$$
t_{die} = \dfrac{L_{die}}{v_{line}} = \dfrac{1.0 \text{ m}}{0.5 \text{ m/min}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_{die} = 2.0 \text{ min}\,}
$$

**(ii) Is the profile fully cured? (2 marks):**

Compare $t_{die}$ with $t_{cure}$:

$$
t_{die} = 2.0 \text{ min} \;<\; t_{cure} = 2.2 \text{ min}
$$

**Conclusion:** The residence time is **less than** the required cure time, so the profile will be **under-cured (tacky, low mechanical strength)** at the given line speed. The line speed must be reduced, or the die temperature must be raised to accelerate cure.

**(iii) Maximum allowable line speed for 25 % production increase (2 marks):**

First, find the **maximum line speed** that still satisfies the cure constraint $t_{die} \geq 2.2$ min:

$$
v_{line,\,max} = \dfrac{L_{die}}{t_{cure}} = \dfrac{1.0 \text{ m}}{2.2 \text{ min}} \approx 0.4545 \text{ m/min}
$$

Now, **25 % increase in current production rate** means the *target* line speed is:

$$
v_{target} = 1.25 \times 0.5 = 0.625 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
v_{target} = 0.625 \text{ m/min} \;>\; v_{line,\,max} = 0.4545 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 25 % production-rate increase is **NOT feasible** at the current die length and resin system. To achieve 0.625 m/min, the manufacturer must either:
- Extend the die to at least $L_{die} = 0.625 \times 2.2 = 1.375$ m, or
- Switch to a faster-curing resin system, or
- Raise the die temperature to reduce $t_{cure}$ below 1.6 min.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** What is the **Rule of Mixtures** for a unidirectional pultruded composite? Derive the expression for the **longitudinal Young's modulus** $E_L$ in terms of fiber and matrix moduli and the fiber volume fraction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pultruded GRP (glass-reinforced polyester) boat mast has a fiber volume fraction of **0.55**. Given: $E_f = 72$ GPa (E-glass), $E_m = 3.5$ GPa (polyester). Calculate: **(i)** the longitudinal modulus $E_L$ of the mast, **(ii)** the mass fraction of fiber $W_f$ (using $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³), and **(iii)** the longitudinal tensile strength if the fiber strength is 2400 MPa and matrix strength is 55 MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) Derivation of $E_L$ (7 marks — 1 for statement, 1 for iso-strain, 2 for load balance, 2 for algebra, 1 for final boxed answer):**

The **Rule of Mixtures** is a weighted-average formula for composite properties based on the volumetric contribution of each constituent. For longitudinal modulus:

**Assumption — Iso-strain (1 mark):**
Both the fiber and the matrix phases experience the same axial strain $\varepsilon$ under tensile load along the fiber direction, because they are perfectly bonded and deform as a parallel mechanical system.

**Load balance (2 marks):**
For a small slice of the composite cross-section:

$$
P_c = P_f + P_m
$$

Using Hooke's law with iso-strain $\varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon_c = \varepsilon$:

$$
E_L \, \varepsilon \, A_c = E_f \, \varepsilon \, A_f + E_m \, \varepsilon \, A_m
$$

**Algebra (2 marks):**
Divide both sides by $A_c$ and $\varepsilon$:

$$
E_L = E_f \left(\dfrac{A_f}{A_c}\right) + E_m \left(\dfrac{A_m}{A_c}\right)
$$

Substitute $A_f/A_c = V_f$ and $A_m/A_c = (1 - V_f)$:

$$
\boxed{\,E_L = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)\,}
$$

**Final statement (1 mark):**
This is the longitudinal Rule of Mixtures, accurate for pultruded UD composites because of their near-perfect fiber alignment.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $V_f = 0.55$, $E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa
- $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³
- $\sigma_f = 2400$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 55$ MPa

**(i) Longitudinal modulus (2 marks):**

$$
E_L = (72)(0.55) + (3.5)(1 - 0.55)
$$

$$
E_L = 39.60 + 3.5 \times 0.45 = 39.60 + 1.575
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_L = 41.175 \text{ GPa}\,}
$$

**(ii) Mass (weight) fraction of fiber (3 marks):**

$$
W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)}
$$

**Compute numerator:**

$$
\rho_f V_f = 2.55 \times 0.55 = 1.4025
$$

**Compute denominator term 2:**

$$
\rho_m (1 - V_f) = 1.20 \times 0.45 = 0.540
$$

**Substitute:**

$$
W_f = \dfrac{1.4025}{1.4025 + 0.540} = \dfrac{1.4025}{1.9425}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,W_f \approx 0.7220 \;\; \text{or}\;\; 72.20\,\%\,}
$$

**(iii) Longitudinal tensile strength (2 marks):**

Using Rule of Mixtures for strength (valid for UD, brittle-fiber, brittle-matrix):

$$
\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m (1 - V_f)
$$

$$
\sigma_L = (2400)(0.55) + (55)(0.45)
$$

$$
\sigma_L = 1320 + 24.75
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\sigma_L = 1344.75 \text{ MPa}\,}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully):**
> 1. **Confusing line speed units:** Many students mix m/min and mm/s. KTU expects m/min. Convert 1 m/s = **60 m/min** before solving.
> 2. **Forgetting the iso-strain assumption** when writing the derivation. Examiners deduct 2 marks if it is not explicitly stated.
> 3. **Mixing $V_f$ and $W_f$** in formulas. A classic error is using $V_f$ in the mass-fraction equation. Always check which fraction the equation requires.
> 4. **Pultrusion ≠ Filament Winding:** Do not interchange the diagrams. Pultrusion has a die; filament winding has a rotating mandrel.
> 5. **Cure time vs. die residence time:** A common 1-mark loss is failing to state whether the profile is fully cured after the numerical comparison.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Pultrusion = Pull + Extrusion** — fibers are *pulled* (not pushed) through a heated die.
- ✅ **Seven stages:** Creel → Guide → Resin Bath → Pre-former → Heated Die → Puller → Cut-off Saw.
- ✅ **Typical process parameters:** Line speed 0.3–1.5 m/min; die temperature 120–180 °C; die length 0.6–1.5 m.
- ✅ **V_f range = 45–65 %** — much higher than hand lay-up (25–35 %).
- ✅ **Common marine parts:** Mast tubes, hull stringers, transom stiffeners, handrail stanchions, rudder stocks, ladder rungs.
- ✅ **Resin systems:** Polyester (cheap, common), Vinyl ester (marine-grade, chemical-resistant), Epoxy (high strength, premium boats), Phenolic (fire-retardant).
- ✅ **Key formulas to memorize cold:**
  - $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m (1 - V_f)$ (Rule of Mixtures)
  - $E_T$ from inverse rule of mixtures
  - $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m (1 - V_f)}$
  - $t_{die} = L_{die} / v_{line}$ (must exceed $t_{cure}$)
- ✅ **Process defect signatures:** Under-cure (tacky, dark patches) → too high line speed; voids → poor pre-former setup; delamination → wet-out issue; die-stick (excessive pull force) → over-tapered die or resin starved.
- ✅ **Compared to hand lay-up:** pultrusion gives higher V_f, lower labor cost, but is limited to **constant cross-section** parts.
- ✅ **Compared to filament winding:** pultrusion is for **linear prismatic** parts; filament winding is for **axisymmetric** parts (pipes, tanks, pressure hulls).
- ✅ **Die design rule:** Tapered entrance angle of 1°–3° compensates for 4–7 % volumetric cure shrinkage of polyester.
- ✅ **KTU favourite exam question pattern:** "Calculate $t_{die}$, comment on cure, and find max line speed" — practice this style.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Filament Winding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Filament Winding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Filament Winding** is an advanced, automated composite manufacturing process in which continuous reinforcing fibers (filaments, tows, or rovings) pre-impregnated with a thermosetting resin system are wound under controlled tension over a rotating mandrel in precisely defined geometric patterns to produce high-strength, hollow, axisymmetric or non-axisymmetric composite structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Pearl:** The process is defined by *three* mandatory simultaneous actions — (1) **continuous fiber delivery**, (2) **resin impregnation/wetting**, and (3) **winding over a mandrel at a controlled helical/geodesic angle α (alpha)**.

The process is governed by the standard **ASTM D2291** (mandrel preparation) and **ASTM D5421** (winding pattern specification), with typical production speeds ranging from **30–120 m/min** for marine and pressure vessel applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Candy Cane on a Spinning Rod"

Imagine you are wrapping a candy cane stripe around a slowly rotating wooden dowel (the mandrel) while slowly walking the candy parallel to the rod's length:

- The **rotating dowel** is analogous to the **mandrel rotation (ω, rad/s)**.
- Your **walking speed** is the **fiber payout/traverse velocity (v, m/s)**.
- The **angle** at which you lay the candy stripe is the **winding angle (α)**.
- The **stickiness of the candy** represents the **resin viscosity (η, Pa·s)**.

If you walk too fast relative to the rotation, the candy stripe becomes nearly straight (low α ≈ 0°, a *hoop* pattern). If you walk slowly, the candy wraps tightly at a steep angle (high α ≈ 90°, an *axial/helical* pattern). The angle is your **design freedom** — you choose the strength direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marine Application Note:** In composite boat hulls and masts, α ≈ 45° (helical) gives quasi-isotropic in-plane stiffness, which is critical for resisting biaxial wave-loads in marine service per **ISO 12215-5** scantling rules.

## 1.3 Classification of Filament Winding (Process Taxonomy)

| Process Variant | Resin Delivery | Cure State at Laydown | Typical Marine Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wet Winding** | Fiber passes through a resin bath just before mandrel contact | Liquid (B-stage: uncured) | Boat hulls, large tanks |
| **Dry Winding** | Prepreg tow (B-staged resin on fiber) is wound | Partially cured (B-stage) | Aerospace, masts |
| **Polar Winding** | Fiber passes through a "polar" eye near the end of the mandrel | Wet or prepreg | Closed-end pressure bottles |
| **Helical Winding** | Multi-angle helical pattern via carriage reciprocation | Wet or prepreg | Pipes, driveshafts |
| **Hoop Winding** | Nearly circumferential (α ≈ 90°) | Wet | Reinforcement of cylindrical sections |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Winding Geometry — Step-by-Step Logic

A filament-wound surface is governed by a single kinematic relationship between the **mandrel rotation (ω)** and the **carriage/fiber-payout traverse velocity (v)** at the **mandrel radius (R)**.

### Step 1 — Establish the Reference Frame
- The mandrel rotates about its longitudinal axis (Z).
- The fiber-payout carriage translates parallel to Z at velocity **v**.
- The contact point traces a helix on the cylinder.

### Step 2 — Define the Instantaneous Winding Angle α
At any instant, the fiber touches the cylinder at angle α measured from the **generatrix (axial/hoop line)**:

- α → 0° → purely **circumferential (hoop)** reinforcement → resists hoop/burst stress.
- α → 90° → purely **axial** reinforcement → resists end-cap/bending loads.
- α → 55° (for closed pressure vessels) → optimal **netting analysis** angle for equal biaxial stress (Tsai classical result).

### Step 3 — Connect Kinematics to Geometry
The fiber path on a cylinder of radius R follows:

$$
\tan(\alpha) \;=\; \frac{v}{\omega R}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A *small* numerator (slow traverse) + *large* denominator (fast rotation, large radius) yields small α — the fiber hugs the equator (hoop). Inverting this gives axial winding.

### Step 4 — Band Width and Coverage
The fiber tow has finite width **b** (typically 3–25 mm). As it winds, adjacent bands must overlap or abut to give **100% coverage** without gaps. The number of circuits **N** required to cover a single band position is:

$$
N \;=\; \frac{b}{\pi D \cos(\alpha)}
$$

where **D = 2R** is the mandrel diameter.

## 2.2 Geodesic vs. Isotensoid Winding — The Two Holy Grails

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Examiners frequently ask the *difference* between **Geodesic** and **Isotensoid** paths. Memorize the table below.

| Property | Geodesic Winding | Isotensoid Winding |
|---|---|---|
| **Fiber tension** | Variable along the path | Constant (iso-tension) |
| **Path shape** | Shortest path on the surface (Fermat principle) | Non-geodesic, controlled by carriage |
| **Mathematical form** | Clairaut's relation: $R \sin(\alpha) = \text{const}$ | Numerical/iterative (Lubin's solution) |
| **Implementation** | Open path, simpler mechanics | Closed-form non-geodesic, complex programming |
| **Use case** | Pressure vessels, pipes | Rocket motor casings, high-performance marine |

### Clairaut's Relation (Geodesic)
For geodesic winding on any surface of revolution:

$$
R(x) \cdot \sin(\alpha(x)) \;=\; R_0 \sin(\alpha_0) \;=\; k \; (\text{constant})
$$

where **x** is the axial coordinate. This is the **classical Clairaut differential relation** for geodesic curves on surfaces of revolution.

## 2.3 Netting Analysis — Classical Stress Prediction

For a closed-end filament-wound pressure vessel, classical **netting analysis** (ignoring matrix stiffness) gives the relationship between winding angle, internal pressure **p**, and fiber stress **σ_f**:

$$
\sigma_f \;=\; \frac{pR}{2 t \cos^2(\alpha)}
$$

where:
- **p** = internal gauge pressure (Pa)
- **R** = vessel inner radius (m)
- **t** = laminate total thickness (m)
- **α** = winding angle
- **σ_f** = tensile stress in the fiber

> [!WARNING]
> **Netting Analysis Limitation:** This analysis assumes the **matrix carries NO load** (pure fiber network). It is conservative and only accurate at high fiber volume fractions (V_f > 0.55). KTU questions often ask when netting fails — answer: at low V_f or when matrix cracking precedes fiber failure.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers ~70% of numerical Part B questions.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{v}{\omega R}$ | Kinematic winding angle | (rad or deg) |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{b}{\pi D \cos(\alpha)}$ | Band coverage circuits | dimensionless |
| 3 | $R \sin(\alpha) = k$ | Clairaut's geodesic invariant | m |
| 4 | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{pR}{2t\cos^2(\alpha)}$ | Netting hoop-fiber stress | Pa |
