topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hull to Deck

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hull-to-Deck Joint (HTD Joint)** — In composite boat design, the Hull-to-Deck joint is the structural interface where the upper edge (sheer line / gunwale) of the hull shell laminates are mechanically and/or adhesively connected to the underside of the deck shell laminates. It is the most critical structural discontinuity in a single-skin or sandwich composite vessel, responsible for transferring **global bending moments, shear loads, localized wave impact, and torsional stiffness** between the two primary shells.

The hull-to-deck region typically comprises:
- **Hull flange (hull shear cap)**: The turned-up upper edge of the hull laminate.
- **Deck flange (deck return)**: The downward-turned edge of the deck laminate.
- **Joint interface**: The mating surface where bonding, mechanical fastening, or a combination occurs.
- **Core material (if sandwich)**: Foam, balsa, or honeycomb terminating at the joint.
- **Reinforcement**: Additional laminate layers (doublers, tapers, scarves) to manage stress.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Tin Can and Its Lid**
> Think of a composite boat like a large tin can floating in water. The can's body is the **hull**, and the lid is the **deck**. Now imagine the lid isn't crimped on (like a sardine can) but is glued and bolted. That glued-and-bolted rim is the **Hull-to-Deck joint**. If the rim is weak, the lid flies off the moment the boat flexes in a wave. The HTD joint is what keeps the "lid" (deck) attached to the "body" (hull) when the boat bends and twists in the sea. The strength of the entire boat is essentially limited by this joint — a poorly designed HTD joint will fail long before the hull or deck themselves fail.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used in HTD Joint Design (KTU Board-Exam Standard):**
> - **Flange width (b)** — typically **40 mm to 100 mm** in small craft, up to **150 mm** in larger vessels.
> - **Adhesive shear strength (τ)** — polyester ≈ **6–10 MPa**, epoxy ≈ **15–30 MPa**, vinylester ≈ **10–18 MPa**.
> - **Laminate overlap length (L)** — designed to provide a **shear lag** length of at least **10× flange thickness**.
> - **Bolt spacing (s)** — typically **75 mm to 150 mm** centre-to-centre for mechanically fastened joints.
> - **Safety Factor** — **3 to 5** for primary structural joints in pleasure craft; **2.5 to 3** for high-performance racing yachts.

## 1.4 Why HTD Joints Are Critical

The HTD joint must resist:
1. **Vertical bending (hogging/sagging)** — generates in-plane tension/compression at the deck and hull flanges.
2. **Transverse shear** — produces out-of-plane shear across the joint.
3. **Torsion** — twisting of the hull in quartering seas.
4. **Local impact loads** — wave slamming, docking.
5. **Cyclic fatigue** — millions of load cycles over the vessel's life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PENST742 Module 2):** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests the student's ability to **select appropriate HTD joint geometry, calculate adhesive bond line shear stress, design flange widths, and recommend combined mechanical-adhesive fastening strategies** for a given composite boat specification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of HTD Joints

The hull-to-deck joint can be classified by **geometry** and **fastening method**:

### 2.1.1 By Geometry

| Joint Type | Description | Typical Application |
|------------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Overlap (L-joint)** | Hull flange overlaps deck flange with adhesive between | Most common in small FRP craft |
| **Flush (butt joint)** | Hull and deck terminate at the same plane, joined by an internal cover strip | Used in sandwich panels with limited flange width |
| **Stepped (scarf joint)** | Tapered transition reduces stress concentration | High-performance racing yachts |
| **Land-joint** | A raised "land" is moulded on either hull or deck; the other part lands on it | Large production boats |
| **Coamed** | A vertical raised lip moulded integrally, with the deck sliding over it | Workboats, commercial vessels |

### 2.1.2 By Fastening Method

| Method | Description | Pros | Cons |
|--------|-------------|------|------|
| **Adhesive bonding only** | Resin/glue line transfers all load | Smooth hull, no fasteners, no stress concentrations | Requires surface prep, quality control |
| **Mechanical fastening only** | Bolts, rivets, screws transfer load | High reliability, disassembly possible | Stress concentrations, leak paths |
| **Combined (hybrid)** | Adhesive + mechanical fasteners | Redundant load path, fatigue-resistant | Most expensive, complex quality |

## 2.2 Load Transfer Mechanism

The HTD joint must transfer three primary stress components:

1. **Shear stress (τ) in the adhesive bond line** — acts parallel to the mating surface.
2. **Peel stress (σ_peel)** — acts normal to the bond line at the ends of the joint (most critical failure mode).
3. **Through-thickness tensile/compressive stress (σ_z)** — generated by bending moments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Pearl:** Pure shear is the IDEAL loading for an adhesive joint. **Peel and cleavage loads must be minimized** by joint geometry (e.g., using a tapered/scarfed end rather than a square-cut termination).

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Design Logic

1. **Determine the global loads** — Calculate the maximum hull girder bending moment (M) and shear force (V) at the midship section for the design wave condition.
2. **Calculate the in-plane force per unit length (N)** at the deck/hull flange: N = M / y, where y is the distance from the neutral axis to the flange centroid.
3. **Choose the joint geometry** — based on hull form, materials, and manufacturing route.
4. **Design the flange width (b_f)** — large enough to carry the shear stress (τ = N / b_f) below the allowable adhesive shear strength.
5. **Design the laminate layup** — thickness and orientation to carry in-plane and bending stresses.
6. **Design the fastening (if hybrid)** — bolt diameter, spacing, and edge distance to carry residual/fatigue loads.
7. **Check for peel stress** — use a tapered end or radius to minimize.
8. **Apply a safety factor** of 3 to 5.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains the canonical KTU-board-exam equations for HTD joint design. Memorize these.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{M}{y}$ | In-plane force per unit length at flange (from global bending) | N/mm |
| 2 | $\tau_{bond} = \dfrac{N}{b_f}$ | Average shear stress in adhesive bond line | MPa (N/mm²) |
| 3 | $b_f \geq \dfrac{N \cdot SF}{\tau_{allow}}$ | Required flange width (with safety factor SF) | mm |
| 4 | $F_{bolt} = \dfrac{N \cdot s}{n_{bolts}}$ | Load per bolt (s = spacing, n = number of bolt rows) | N |
| 5 | $\sigma_{bolt} = \dfrac{4 F_{bolt}}{\pi d^2}$ | Tensile stress in bolt | MPa |
| 6 | $\sigma_{bearing} = \dfrac{F_{bolt}}{d \cdot t_{lam}}$ | Bearing stress on laminate | MPa |
| 7 | $\tau_{core} = \dfrac{V}{b \cdot d_{core}}$ | Shear stress in sandwich core at HTD transition | MPa |
| 8 | $L_{overlap} = 10 \cdot t_{lam}$ | Minimum overlap length (shear lag rule of thumb) | mm |
| 9 | $I_{flange} = \dfrac{b \cdot t^3}{12}$ | Second moment of area of flange (for bending) | mm⁴ |
| 10 | $\sigma_{bending} = \dfrac{M_{local} \cdot c}{I_{flange}}$ | Bending stress in flange | MPa |
| 11 | $M_{local} = \dfrac{N \cdot L_{overlap}}{2}$ | Local bending moment at end of overlap | N·mm |
| 12 | $\sigma_{peel,max} \approx \dfrac{N \cdot t_{lam}}{2 L_{overlap}^2}$ | Maximum peel stress at square-cut end (Volkersen-type) | MPa |
| 13 | $d_{bolt,min} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 F_{bolt} \cdot SF}{\pi \cdot \sigma_{allow,bolt}}}$ | Minimum bolt diameter | mm |
| 14 | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ult}}{SF}$ | Allowable stress design | MPa |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The HTD joint design methodology is used in:
- **Recreational boat building** (ISO 12215 structural design standard).
- **Naval architecture** for patrol boats and fast attack craft.
- **Racing yacht design** (America's Cup, TP52, IMOCA 60 — extreme optimization of joint weight vs strength).
- **Composite bridge and wind-turbine blade joints** — same Volkersen shear-lag theory applies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Shear Stress in the HTD Bond Line

**Problem Context:** A composite sailing yacht has a maximum hull-girder bending moment in still-water hogging condition of M = 250 kN·m. The neutral axis of the hull girder is located 350 mm below the deck flange centroid. The chosen joint geometry is an overlap joint with a target flange width of b_f = 80 mm. Verify the average bond line shear stress and determine if it is safe for a vinylester adhesive with an allowable shear strength of 8 MPa (after safety factor 4).

### Step 1: Calculate the in-plane force per unit length at the deck flange

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{M}{y} \\
  &= \frac{250 \times 10^{3} \text{ N·m}}{0.350 \text{ m}} \\
  &= 714.29 \times 10^{3} \text{ N/m} \\
  &= 714.29 \text{ N/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The global bending moment is divided by the lever arm to convert it into a force couple at the deck/hull interface. This force must be transmitted across the joint.

### Step 2: Calculate the average shear stress in the bond line

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{bond} &= \frac{N}{b_f} \\
            &= \frac{714.29 \text{ N/mm}}{80 \text{ mm}} \\
            &= 8.93 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The in-plane force is spread over the flange width. The average shear stress assumes a uniform stress distribution (a simplification — the real distribution is non-uniform, peaking at the ends of the overlap due to shear lag).

### Step 3: Compare with allowable stress

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{allow} &= 8.00 \text{ MPa} \\
\tau_{bond} &= 8.93 \text{ MPa} > \tau_{allow} = 8.00 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The calculated stress **exceeds** the allowable. The flange width must be increased.

### Step 4: Calculate the required flange width

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{f,req} &= \frac{N \cdot SF}{\tau_{allow,ult}} \\
          &= \frac{714.29 \times 4}{12.0} \\
          &= 238.10 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Where $\tau_{allow,ult} = 12$ MPa is the ultimate shear strength of the vinylester adhesive, and SF = 4.)

> **Logic:** Working from ultimate strength and applying the safety factor gives the minimum geometric dimension. A practical flange width of **240 mm** is recommended.

### Step 5: Check overlap length (shear lag)

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{overlap} &= 10 \times t_{lam} \\
            &= 10 \times 6 \text{ mm (assumed flange thickness)} \\
            &= 60 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** This is the empirical rule to ensure the load has fully transferred before the end of the overlap, minimizing peel stress.

## 3.2 Derivation: Peel Stress at the End of an Overlap Joint (Volkersen-Style)

The Volkersen analysis treats the bonded overlap as two elastic adherents of different stiffnesses joined by a thin elastic adhesive layer. The maximum **peel stress** at the end of the overlap is approximated by:

### Step 1: Define the geometric and material parameters

Let:
- E_1, t_1 = Young's modulus and thickness of adherent 1 (deck flange)
- E_2, t_2 = Young's modulus and thickness of adherent 2 (hull flange)
- L = overlap length
- N = in-plane load per unit width
- G_a, t_a = shear modulus and thickness of adhesive

### Step 2: Define the shear-lag parameter

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \sqrt{\frac{G_a}{t_a} \left( \frac{1}{E_1 t_1} + \frac{1}{E_2 t_2} \right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** $\omega$ has units of 1/length. It governs how rapidly the load transfers from one adherent to the other across the overlap.

### Step 3: Maximum peel stress at the end of the overlap

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{peel,max} &\approx \frac{N \cdot t_{1}}{2 L^2} \cdot \left[ \frac{1 + \tanh(\omega L / 2)}{\tanh(\omega L / 2)} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The peel stress is proportional to the adherent thickness and inversely proportional to the square of the overlap length. This is why **tapered/scarfed ends** and **long overlaps** are used in high-performance joints.

### Step 4: Numerical example

Let E_1 = E_2 = 20 GPa (glass/polyester), t_1 = t_2 = 6 mm, L = 60 mm, N = 714.29 N/mm, G_a = 1 GPa, t_a = 0.5 mm.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega &= \sqrt{\frac{1000}{0.5} \left( \frac{1}{20000 \times 6} + \frac{1}{20000 \times 6} \right)} \\
       &= \sqrt{2000 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-5}} \\
       &= \sqrt{0.0333} \\
       &= 0.1826 \text{ /mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega L / 2 &= 0.1826 \times 60 / 2 = 5.478
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\tanh(5.478) \approx 1.0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{peel,max} &\approx \frac{714.29 \times 6}{2 \times 60^2} \times \frac{1 + 1}{1} \\
                  &= \frac{4285.7}{7200} \times 2 \\
                  &= 1.19 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A peel stress of 1.19 MPa is significant for an adhesive (peel strength of vinylester is typically 2–4 MPa). The factor of safety is only ~2, which is insufficient. **Solution:** use a tapered (scarfed) termination or add a fillet/radius at the joint end to reduce peel stress by 50–70%.

## 3.3 Derivation: Bolt Design for Hybrid (Adhesive + Mechanical) HTD Joint

### Step 1: Determine the residual load on the bolts

In a hybrid joint, the adhesive carries the long-term static load, while the bolts carry:
- **Peel loads** at joint ends
- **Fatigue/cyclic loads** (adhesive degrades in fatigue)
- **Assembly/peel-back loads** during cure

A conservative assumption is that bolts carry the **entire design load** in the worst case (adhesive failed):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{bolt} &= \frac{N \cdot s}{n_{rows}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where s = bolt spacing (say 100 mm) and n_rows = 1 row of bolts.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{bolt} &= \frac{714.29 \times 100}{1} = 71.43 \text{ kN per bolt}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Required bolt diameter (A4-80 stainless steel, $\sigma_{ult} = 800$ MPa, SF = 3)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{allow,bolt} &= \frac{800}{3} = 266.67 \text{ MPa} \\
d_{min} &= \sqrt{\frac{4 F_{bolt}}{\pi \cdot \sigma_{allow,bolt}}} \\
        &= \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 71430}{\pi \times 266.67}} \\
        &= \sqrt{341.13} \\
        &= 18.47 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Select standard bolt size

**Use M20 bolts** (d = 20 mm, A4-80 stainless steel).

### Step 4: Check bearing stress on the laminate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{bearing} &= \frac{F_{bolt}}{d \cdot t_{lam}} \\
                  &= \frac{71430}{20 \times 6} \\
                  &= 595.25 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **exceeds** the typical bearing strength of glass/polyester (~250 MPa). **Solution:** increase laminate thickness locally with a doubler, or reduce bolt load by adding a second row of bolts.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Design Parameter | Overlap Adhesive | Flush + Cover Strip | Stepped Scarf | Hybrid (Adhesive + Bolts) |
|------------------|:----------------:|:------------------:|:-------------:|:-------------------------:|
| Ease of manufacture | High | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Peel resistance | Low | Medium | **High** | High |
| Fatigue life | Medium | Medium | High | **Very High** |
| Leak-tightness | High | Medium | High | Medium (gasket required) |
| Weight penalty | Low | Low | Low | **High** (bolts, washers, nuts) |
| Cost | Low | Low | Medium | **High** |
| Inspection | Difficult | Easy | Difficult | Easy (bolts visible) |
| Typical boat size | < 10 m | 8–15 m | Racing yachts | Commercial, naval |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — HTD Joint Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Global Hull Loads: Bending M, Shear V, Torsion T] --> B[Load Distribution at Midship]
    B --> C[Deck Flange: In-plane Tension/Compression N]
    B --> D[Hull Flange: In-plane Compression/Tension N]
    C --> E[Hull-to-Deck Joint Interface]
    D --> E
    E --> F{Joining Method}
    F --> F1[Adhesive Bond Only]
    F --> F2[Mechanical Fasteners Only]
    F --> F3[Hybrid: Adhesive plus Bolts]
    F1 --> G1[Bond Line Shear Stress tau]
    F1 --> G2[Peel Stress at Joint Ends]
    F2 --> G3[Bolt Shear and Tension]
    F2 --> G4[Laminate Bearing Stress]
    F3 --> G5[Adhesive Carries Static Load]
    F3 --> G6[Bolts Carry Peel and Fatigue]
    G1 --> H[Check vs Allowable Stress with SF 3 to 5]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    G5 --> H
    G6 --> H
    H --> I{Acceptable?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Approved Joint Design]
    I -- No --> K[Iterate: Increase Flange Width, Use Scarf, Add Doublers, More Bolts]
    K --> E
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stress States in HTD Bond Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[In-plane Load N] --> S1[Shear Stress tau in Adhesive]
    L1 --> S2[Peel Stress sigma_peel at Joint End]
    L1 --> S3[Through-thickness Stress sigma_z from Bending]
    S1 --> R1[Ideally Distributed]
    S2 --> R2[Concentrated at Overlap End]
    S3 --> R3[Concentrated at Step]
    R1 --> D1[Tapered or Scarfed End Recommended]
    R2 --> D1
    R3 --> D2[Fillets and Radii Recommended]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow — HTD Joint Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1: Define Design Loads M, V] --> S2[Step 2: Calculate N equals M over y]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Select Joint Geometry]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Calculate Required Flange Width]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Calculate Adhesive Shear Stress]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Check Peel Stress]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: If Hybrid, Design Bolts]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Apply Safety Factor 3 to 5]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Document and Draw]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term "Hull-to-Deck Joint" in composite boat construction. List the three primary stress components that the joint must resist.**

**Model Answer:**
The Hull-to-Deck (HTD) Joint is the structural interface connecting the upper edge (sheer line) of the hull laminate to the deck laminate, transferring all global and local loads between the two shells.

The three primary stress components are:
1. **In-plane shear stress (τ)** in the adhesive bond line.
2. **Peel stress (σ_peel)** at the termination of the joint.
3. **Through-thickness normal stress (σ_z)** from global bending.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Compare the "overlap" and "stepped (scarf) joint" geometries for HTD connections. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Overlap Joint | Stepped (Scarf) Joint |
|---------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Geometry** | Hull flange overlaps deck flange flat | Tapered transition at joint end |
| **Advantage** | Simple to manufacture in hand lay-up; good for production boats | Lower peel stress concentration; used in high-performance racing yachts |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**A composite sailing yacht of length 12 m has a maximum still-water bending moment of M = 180 kN·m at the midship section. The neutral axis of the hull girder is 320 mm below the deck flange. The chosen HTD joint is an overlap joint with vinylester adhesive (ultimate shear strength = 12 MPa, allowable with SF = 4 → 3 MPa).**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the required minimum flange width. Assume a shear stress safety factor of 4.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the joint is redesigned as a hybrid (adhesive + bolts) with bolt spacing of 100 mm and one row of M16 (A4-80) bolts ($\sigma_{ult}$ = 800 MPa, $\sigma_{allow}$ with SF 3 = 266.67 MPa), check whether the bolts alone (with adhesive assumed failed) can carry the entire in-plane load. Laminate thickness = 6 mm, bearing strength = 250 MPa.

---

#### Solution to Question A (a):

**Step 1:** Calculate the in-plane force per unit length N:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{M}{y} = \frac{180 \times 10^{3}}{0.320} = 562.5 \text{ N/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the required flange width with safety factor 4:

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{f,req} &= \frac{N \cdot SF}{\tau_{ult}} = \frac{562.5 \times 4}{12} = 187.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Practical recommendation — use **b_f = 190 mm** (next standard size up).

**Step 4:** Verify the working shear stress:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{actual} = \frac{N}{b_f} = \frac{562.5}{190} = 2.96 \text{ MPa} < 3 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{SAFE}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Solution to Question A (b):

**Step 1:** Load per bolt:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{bolt} = N \cdot s = 562.5 \times 100 = 56.25 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Tensile stress in M16 bolt (d = 16 mm, root area A_s ≈ 157 mm² for M16):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{bolt} = \frac{F_{bolt}}{A_s} = \frac{56250}{157} = 358.3 \text{ MPa} > 266.67 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Bolt fails by tension — NOT SAFE.**

**Step 3:** Check bearing stress on the laminate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{bearing} = \frac{F_{bolt}}{d \cdot t_{lam}} = \frac{56250}{16 \times 6} = 585.9 \text{ MPa} > 250 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Bearing failure on laminate — NOT SAFE.**

**Step 4:** Recommendations:
- Increase bolt diameter to **M20** (A_s = 245 mm²).
- Add a **doubler plate** at the bolt location to increase local laminate thickness to 10 mm.
- OR add a **second row of bolts** to halve the load per bolt.

**Revised calculation with M20 bolts + 10 mm doubler:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{bolt} &= \frac{56250}{245} = 229.6 \text{ MPa} < 266.67 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{SAFE} \\
\sigma_{bearing} &= \frac{56250}{20 \times 10} = 281.25 \text{ MPa} > 250 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{STILL HIGH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final recommendation:** Use **M20 bolts with 12 mm doubler**, OR add a second bolt row.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**A 10 m composite planing powerboat uses a stepped (scarf) HTD joint with a 1:20 taper. The maximum in-plane load per unit length at the deck flange is N = 400 N/mm. The adhesive is epoxy with ultimate shear strength 18 MPa and ultimate peel strength 3 MPa. The overlap length is L = 100 mm and the flange thickness t = 5 mm.**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the average bond line shear stress and verify whether it satisfies a safety factor of 4 against shear failure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the maximum peel stress at the joint end using the simplified formula $\sigma_{peel,max} \approx N \cdot t / (2 L^2)$, and recommend a joint modification if the safety factor against peel is less than 3.

---

#### Solution to Question B (a):

**Step 1:** Shear stress for a 1:20 scarf joint (effective bond width = L/taper ratio):

For a scarf joint, the average shear stress is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{avg} &= \frac{N}{L_{eff}} = \frac{N}{L / \tan(\theta)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\theta$ is the scarf angle. For 1:20 taper, $\tan(\theta) = 1/20$, so $L_{eff} = L \times 20 = 2000$ mm. This is the projected length of the scarf onto the in-plane direction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{avg} &= \frac{400}{2000} = 0.20 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with allowable stress:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{allow} = \frac{18}{4} = 4.5 \text{ MPa} \\
\tau_{avg} = 0.20 \text{ MPa} \ll 4.5 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{VERY SAFE in shear}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Solution to Question B (b):

**Step 1:** Calculate the peel stress:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{peel,max} &= \frac{N \cdot t}{2 L^2} = \frac{400 \times 5}{2 \times 100^2} = \frac{2000}{20000} = 0.10 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare with allowable peel stress:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{peel,allow} = \frac{3}{3} = 1.0 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_{peel,max} = 0.10 \text{ MPa} < 1.0 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{SAFE in peel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Conclusion — The stepped scarf joint provides very high safety margins in both shear and peel. No modification is required. The 1:20 taper is excellent for low peel stress concentration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor** — Students often use the ultimate strength directly without dividing by the safety factor. **Always** apply SF = 3 to 5 in HTD joint design.
> 2. **Confusing flange width with flange thickness** — Flange WIDTH (b_f) governs bond shear; flange THICKNESS (t) governs peel and bending.
> 3. **Ignoring peel stress** — Many students calculate only shear and forget that **peel is the most common failure mode** in HTD joints.
> 4. **Using M16 nominal diameter for bolt stress** — Always use the **thread root (stress) area A_s**, not the nominal diameter.
> 5. **Mixing units** — Convert kN·m to N·mm and m to mm **before** substituting into the formulas.
> 6. **Not iterating the design** — A single calculation is never the final answer. Always **check** all failure modes and iterate.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hull to Deck (PENST742 Module 2)**

- ✅ The **HTD joint** is the most critical structural interface in a composite boat — it transfers global bending, shear, and torsion between hull and deck.
- ✅ Three primary stress components: **shear (τ), peel (σ_peel), through-thickness (σ_z)**.
- ✅ The fundamental design equation is **N = M/y**, where y is the lever arm from the neutral axis to the flange centroid.
- ✅ Bond line shear stress: **τ = N / b_f**. Required flange width: **b_f ≥ N·SF / τ_ult**.
- ✅ Overlap length rule of thumb: **L_overlap ≥ 10·t_lam** (shear lag).
- ✅ Peel stress is proportional to **t / L²** — taper the joint end and use a long overlap.
- ✅ **Hybrid (adhesive + bolts)** is the most reliable but heaviest and most expensive.
- ✅ **Stepped scarf joints** give the best peel performance — used in racing yachts.
- ✅ **Bolt design** uses thread root area A_s, not nominal diameter.
- ✅ **Bearing stress** on the laminate: $\sigma_{bearing} = F_{bolt} / (d \cdot t_{lam})$ — limit to ~250 MPa for glass/polyester.
- ✅ **Safety factor of 3 to 5** must be applied to ultimate material strength.
- ✅ Always **iterate**: increase flange width, use a scarf, add doublers, or add more bolts to satisfy all failure modes.
- ✅ In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic maps to **CO2: Design composite structural joints** and is tested at **Apply / Analyze** level of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bulkhead to Hull
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bulkhead to Hull

# Bulkhead to Hull — Structural Joint Engineering in Composite Boat Construction

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bulkhead-to-Hull Joint:** A bulkhead-to-hull joint is the structural interface region where a transverse vertical watertight or structural bulkhead (panel) meets the inner surface of the hull shell (bottom, side, or deck) of a composite boat. It is engineered to transfer hydrostatic, dynamic, slamming, and inertial loads between the stiffening bulkhead and the load-bearing hull skin while maintaining the geometric continuity of the vessel's monocoque or semi-monocoque structure.

In composite boat design (FRP — Fibre Reinforced Polymer, sandwich, or hybrid laminates), this joint is **not** a simple mechanical fastener connection as in steel/aluminium vessels. Instead, it is a **laminated, bonded, and mechanically fastened hybrid transition zone** that maintains fibre continuity, prevents stress concentrations, and ensures watertightness.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine the human ribcage connected to the spine. The **spine** is like the **keel and hull shell** (the long, continuous load-bearing member). The **ribs** are like the **bulkheads** (stiff, transverse elements). The **intercostal muscles and ligaments** (the soft tissue binding the ribs to the spine) represent the **composite laminate bond, fillet, and tabbing** in the bulkhead-to-hull joint. Just as a rib can detach from the spine under severe twisting (causing serious injury), a poorly designed bulkhead-to-hull joint can fail catastrophically under wave slamming or torsion — flooding the boat.

In a boat, the bulkhead **stiffens** the hull against panel buckling and distributes local impact loads. The hull **supports** the bulkhead against hydrostatic pressure. The joint must do three jobs simultaneously:
1. **Stiffen** (prevent panel flexing)
2. **Seal** (keep water out)
3. **Survive** (transfer loads without cracking or delaminating)

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Terminology (KTU 2024)

| Term | Standard Metric / Symbol |
|---|---|
| Hull-to-bulkhead bondline thickness | **$t_b$ = 1.5 mm to 3.0 mm** (typical FRP) |
| Hull laminate thickness | **$t_h$ = 4 mm to 25 mm** (depends on vessel size) |
| Bulkhead laminate thickness | **$t_bh$ = 3 mm to 15 mm** |
| Fillet radius at the joint | **$R_f$ ≥ 25 mm** (for FRP) |
| Overlap length (tabbing) | **$L_{ov}$ ≥ 50 mm** or 30× laminate thickness |
| Stiffener spacing | **$s$ = 0.4 m to 0.6 m** (small craft) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Role of the Bulkhead-to-Hull Joint

The joint performs four primary engineering functions in a composite boat:

1. **Load Transfer Function:** Distributes transverse hydrostatic pressure ($P_{hs}$) and slamming pressure ($P_{sl}$) from the hull skin into the bulkhead webs.
2. **Stiffening Function:** Reduces the effective panel span ($L_e$) of the hull plating, increasing its buckling resistance by a factor of $(L_e / L_0)^2$.
3. **Sealing Function:** Maintains compartmentation (subdivision) required by classification societies (RCD, ISO 12215, DNV-GL).
4. **Geometric Continuity Function:** Provides a smooth load path to avoid stress singularities at the bulkhead edge.

### 2.2 Types of Bulkhead-to-Hull Joints in Composite Boats

| Joint Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Bonded + Tabbed** | Bulkhead flange is overlapped onto the hull inner skin with additional layers of reinforcement (tabbing) | Small-to-medium GRP craft |
| **Bonded Fillet (Chocked)** | A triangular fillet of putty/mastic/fibreglass is laid at the intersection | Small dinghies, RIBs |
| **Mechanical Fastened (Bolted)** | Stainless or titanium bolts through a bonded flange | Larger composite vessels, naval craft |
| **Infusion-Integrated (Co-cured)** | Both hull and bulkhead flange are laid up/woven together in a single infusion cycle | High-performance racing yachts |
| **Core-Bonded Sandwich** | Bulkhead edge is cored and bonded to the hull sandwich inner skin | Modern catamarans, FRP ferries |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the choice matters:** In a **chined** (hard-corner) hull, the joint must absorb bending around the chine line. In a **round-bilge** hull, the bulkhead must be **tortured** (twisted out-of-plane) to fit the curved hull, introducing pre-stress that must be accounted for in the design.

### 2.3 Load Path Mechanics — The "Why" and the "How"

**Step-by-step load transfer logic:**

- **Hydrostatic pressure** acts on the outer hull skin → generates in-plane **membrane forces** ($N_x$, $N_y$) and out-of-plane **shear** ($V_z$).
- The **bulkhead** acts as a line support, so the hull skin tries to **deflect into the bulkhead**, creating a **reaction force** ($R$) along the bulkhead-hull line.
- This reaction is transferred via the **bondline** as **interlaminar shear stress** ($\tau$) and **peel stress** ($\sigma_{peel}$).
- The **tabbing overlap** acts as a doubler, spreading the load over a wider area, reducing peak stress.
- For **mechanical fasteners**, the load is shared between the **bolt shear** and the **bondline**, in a ratio depending on joint stiffness.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (2024 Scheme)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{hs} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa |
| 2 | $P_{sl} = k_{sl} \cdot v^2$ | Slamming pressure (velocity $v$) | Pa |
| 3 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{b \cdot L_{ov}}$ | Bondline shear stress | Pa |
| 4 | $\sigma_{peel} = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Peel stress at fillet root | Pa |
| 5 | $F_{bolt} = \dfrac{V}{n \cdot \mu}$ | Load per bolt ($n$ bolts, friction factor $\mu$) | N |
| 6 | $L_e = \dfrac{s}{\sqrt[4]{1 + (s/L_0)^4}}$ | Effective panel span with stiffener | m |
| 7 | $t_{min} = \dfrac{0.5 \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{P}}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | Min. hull skin thickness (Roark/Mitchell) | m |
| 8 | $R_f = \dfrac{t_h \cdot \tau_{allow}}{\sigma_{peel,allow}}$ | Required fillet radius | m |
| 9 | $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{L_{ov}}{t_h}}$ | Stress concentration factor (bonded lap) | — |
| 10 | $\sigma_{admissible} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ult}}{n_{sf}}$ | Allowable stress with safety factor $n_{sf} = 3$ to **5** | Pa |

> [!WARNING]
> **Symbol conflict rule:** When writing absolute values in your answer sheet, ALWAYS use $\vert x \vert$ — never a plain pipe — to avoid LaTeX/markdown table corruption during the KTU digital valuation upload.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

In industry, the bulkhead-to-hull joint is one of the **most inspected regions** during classification surveys (Bureau Veritas, Lloyd's Register, RINA, DNV). Failure here caused the famous **1987 Herald of Free Enterprise** disaster (ferry) and multiple FRP fishing boat sinkings. Mastery of this joint is therefore a **professional safety competency**, not just an exam topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bondline Shear Stress in a Tabbed Joint

**Given:** A bulkhead flange of width $b = 600$ mm is tabbed to the hull inner skin with an overlap length $L_{ov} = 100$ mm. The compartment experiences a hydrostatic head of $h = 1.2$ m of seawater.

**Step 1: Compute the hydrostatic pressure acting on the unsupported hull panel between bulkheads.**

$$
P_{hs} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h
$$

Where:
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ (seawater density)
- $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$
- $h = 1.2 \ \text{m}$

Substituting the values:

$$
P_{hs} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1.2
$$

Now perform the multiplication step by step:

$$
1025 \times 9.81 = 1025 \times (10 - 0.19) = 10250 - 194.75 = 10055.25
$$

$$
10055.25 \times 1.2 = 10055.25 + 2011.05 = 12066.30 \ \text{Pa}
$$

**Result:** $P_{hs} \approx 12.07 \ \text{kPa}$

**Step 2: Compute the total transverse shear force per unit length of bulkhead.**

The total shear per metre of bulkhead equals the pressure times the unsupported panel span $s$:

$$
V = P_{hs} \cdot s
$$

With $s = 0.5$ m (typical small-craft bulkhead spacing):

$$
V = 12066.30 \times 0.5 = 6033.15 \ \text{N/m}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the bondline shear stress.**

The shear stress is the total shear force divided by the bonded area per unit length:

$$
\tau = \dfrac{V}{L_{ov}}
$$

$$
\tau = \dfrac{6033.15}{0.100} = 60331.5 \ \text{Pa} = 60.33 \ \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 4: Compare with the allowable shear strength of polyester/vinylester FRP.**

For a hand-laid GRP bondline, $\tau_{allow} \approx 4$ to **$6$ MPa**. Therefore:

$$
\tau_{actual} = 0.060 \ \text{MPa} \ll \tau_{allow} = 4 \ \text{MPa}
$$

**Result:** The joint is **safe by a factor of 66×**. The design is conservative, which is desirable for fatigue and impact margin.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Fillet Radius for Peel Stress Control

**Given:** The same bulkhead, with hull laminate thickness $t_h = 8$ mm and bulkhead laminate thickness $t_{bh} = 6$ mm. The allowable peel stress is $\sigma_{peel,allow} = 25$ MPa, and the allowable bondline shear is $\tau_{allow} = 4$ MPa.

**Step 1: Apply the fillet radius formula from Section 2.4, equation 8.**

$$
R_f = \dfrac{t_h \cdot \tau_{allow}}{\sigma_{peel,allow}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
R_f = \dfrac{0.008 \times 4 \times 10^6}{25 \times 10^6}
$$

**Step 3: Simplify.**

$$
R_f = \dfrac{32 \times 10^3}{25 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{32}{25000} = 0.00128 \ \text{m}
$$

$$
R_f = 1.28 \ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4: Compare with KTU/design code minimum.**

The KTU/ISO 12215 minimum is **$R_f \geq 25$ mm**. Our calculation gives only 1.28 mm, which is much smaller — this means the **peel stress would actually be the limiting factor**, and we must use a fillet radius of at least **25 mm** to be code-compliant.

**Result:** Required fillet radius $R_f = 25$ mm (governed by code, not by simple mechanics).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bolted Joint Share of the Load

**Given:** A bonded + bolted bulkhead connection carries $V = 6033$ N/m. Bondline carries 60% of load. Find force per bolt if bolts are spaced $p = 150$ mm apart, and the joint is $L_j = 2$ m long.

**Step 1: Compute total load on the joint.**

$$
V_{total} = V \cdot L_j = 6033 \times 2 = 12066 \ \text{N}
$$

**Step 2: Bolts carry 40% of the total load.**

$$
V_{bolt} = 0.40 \times 12066 = 4826.4 \ \text{N}
$$

**Step 3: Number of bolts along the joint.**

$$
n = \dfrac{L_j}{p} = \dfrac{2000}{150} = 13.33 \Rightarrow 14 \ \text{bolts (round up)}
$$

**Step 4: Force per bolt.**

$$
F_{bolt} = \dfrac{V_{bolt}}{n} = \dfrac{4826.4}{14} = 344.7 \ \text{N/bolt}
$$

For an M8 stainless steel bolt, allowable shear is approx. **$3$ kN** → joint is **vastly under-utilised**. This indicates that **bondline alone** would suffice, and bolts are added for **redundancy and safety**, not for primary load.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Bulkhead-to-Hull Load Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Hydrostatic and Slamming Load] --> B[Hull Outer Skin Laminate]
    B --> C{Stress Distribution in Hull}
    C --> D[Membrane Tension Nx Ny]
    C --> E[Transverse Shear Vz]
    E --> F[Bulkhead-Hull Bondline]
    F --> G[Tabbing Overlap Doubler]
    G --> H[Fillet Radius Transition]
    H --> I[Bulkhead Web Laminate]
    I --> J[Frame and Stiffener Network]
    J --> K[Keel and Longitudinals]
    K --> L[Reaction to Sea via Hull Form]
    D --> M[Load Path 2: Direct Membrane]
    M --> L
    F --> N[Mechanical Fastener Backup]
    N --> I
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style L fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style F fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
```

### 4.2 Joint Configuration Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Boat Type and Size] --> B{Vessel Length}
    B --> C[Under 6 m]
    B --> D[6 to 12 m]
    B --> E[Above 12 m]
    C --> F[Bonded Fillet and Tabbing]
    D --> G[Bonded Flange plus Tabbing]
    E --> H[Bonded plus Mechanical Fastened]
    F --> I[Hand Layup GRP]
    G --> J[Hand Layup or Vacuum Infusion]
    H --> K[Prepreg or Resin Infusion with SS316 Bolts]
    style A fill:#e6f3ff
    style K fill:#ffe6e6
```

### 4.3 Schematic Block — Failure Mode Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Bulkhead-Hull Joint] --> B[Exceeds Design Load]
    B --> C{Failure Type}
    C --> D[Adhesive Peel Failure at Fillet Root]
    C --> E[Bondline Shear Delamination]
    C --> F[Bolt Shear Yielding]
    C --> G[Substrate Laminate Cracking]
    C --> H[Sandwich Core Crushing]
    D --> I[Flooding and Compartment Loss]
    E --> I
    F --> J[Progressive Joint Loosening]
    G --> K[Loss of Hull-Bulkhead Continuity]
    H --> K
    style B fill:#ffe6e6
    style I fill:#ffcccc
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024, PENST742]**

**Q1. Define the term "bulkhead-to-hull joint" and list its three primary functions in a composite boat.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A bulkhead-to-hull joint is the structural interface where a transverse bulkhead meets the hull shell of a composite vessel. Its three primary functions are:

1. **Load transfer** — distributes hydrostatic, slamming, and inertial loads between the bulkhead and the hull skin. (1 mark)
2. **Stiffening** — reduces the effective panel span of the hull plating, increasing its buckling resistance. (1 mark)
3. **Sealing/Compartmentation** — maintains watertight subdivision of the vessel per classification rules. (1 mark)

> *(Allocate 1 mark per correct function with brief justification.)*

---

**Q2. Why is a fillet radius of at least 25 mm recommended at the bulkhead-hull intersection in FRP construction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The fillet radius **reduces stress concentration** at the sharp intersection where the vertical bulkhead meets the curved hull. A larger radius:

- Lowers the **peel stress** at the fillet root (which is a tensile failure mode in resins). (1 mark)
- Smooths the **load transfer path** from hull to bulkhead. (1 mark)
- Prevents **resin cracking** and **delamination initiation** that would otherwise start at sharp corners. (1 mark)

> *(Examiner tip: 1 mark for the principle, 1 mark for the mechanism, 1 mark for consequence.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023, PENST742 — Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Bonded Tabbed Joint Analysis**

**(a)** A watertight bulkhead in a $9$ m GRP fishing boat is bonded and tabbed to the inner hull skin. The unsupported hull panel span is $s = 0.5$ m, the tabbing overlap is $L_{ov} = 80$ mm, and the maximum hydrostatic head of seawater expected is $h = 1.5$ m. The allowable shear strength of the polyester GRP bondline is $\tau_{allow} = 4$ MPa. Determine whether the joint is safe. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the role of **fillet radius, tabbing, and mechanical fasteners** in the bulkhead-to-hull joint. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1: Hydrostatic pressure.**

$$
P_{hs} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 = 15083.0 \ \text{Pa} \approx 15.08 \ \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 2: Transverse shear per metre of bulkhead.**

$$
V = P_{hs} \cdot s = 15083.0 \times 0.5 = 7541.5 \ \text{N/m}
$$

**Step 3: Bondline shear stress.**

$$
\tau = \dfrac{V}{L_{ov}} = \dfrac{7541.5}{0.080} = 94268.75 \ \text{Pa} = 0.094 \ \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 4: Factor of safety.**

$$
FS = \dfrac{\tau_{allow}}{\tau_{actual}} = \dfrac{4.0}{0.094} \approx 42.4
$$

**Conclusion:** The joint is **safe** with a factor of safety of approximately **42**. The design is conservative.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

- **Fillet radius ($R_f \geq 25$ mm):** Acts as a smooth stress transition. Reduces the peak **peel stress** at the corner where a sharp re-entrant would otherwise concentrate stress. Implemented as a glass-fibre reinforced fillet of thickened resin.
- **Tabbing:** Additional layers of reinforcement cloth (chopped strand mat + woven roving) laminated over the joint to **increase the bonded area** and **spread the load**. The longer the tab, the lower the average bondline shear, but the weight increases.
- **Mechanical fasteners (SS bolts):** Provide **redundancy** in case the bondline fails due to impact, fatigue, or delamination. They act as a **fail-safe**, not as a primary load path. In composite boats, bolts are typically used in **combination with** bonding, never alone.

*(Allocate ~2.5 marks for the role of each element with sketch description.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative: Joint Types and Failure Modes**

**(a)** Classify the different types of bulkhead-to-hull joints used in composite boat construction. Discuss the merits of bonded + bolted hybrid joints over purely bonded joints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulkhead in a $12$ m patrol boat is connected to the hull by **12 stainless steel M10 bolts** of $8.8$ grade, spaced at $p = 200$ mm along a $2.4$ m joint. The total transverse load is $V_{total} = 18$ kN, of which **40%** is shared with the bondline. Verify the bolts are adequate if the allowable shear per bolt is $F_{allow} = 6$ kN. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Joint types (5 main types, classify as per Section 2.2):**

1. Bonded tabbed
2. Bonded fillet (chocked)
3. Bonded + mechanical fastened
4. Infusion-integrated (co-cured)
5. Core-bonded sandwich

**Merits of hybrid (bonded + bolted):**

- **Redundancy:** If the bondline fails (impact, delamination, fire), bolts carry the load. (2 marks)
- **Improved fatigue life:** Bolts relieve the bonded joint of cyclic stress, especially under wave slamming. (2 marks)
- **Easier inspection:** Bolt torque can be re-checked; a delaminated bond is hidden. (1.5 marks)
- **Tolerance to manufacturing defects:** Bonding alone is sensitive to surface prep; bolts compensate. (1.5 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1: Load on bolts (40% of total).**

$$
V_{bolt} = 0.40 \times 18000 = 7200 \ \text{N}
$$

**Step 2: Number of bolts.**

$$
n = \dfrac{2400}{200} = 12 \ \text{bolts}
$$

**Step 3: Force per bolt.**

$$
F_{bolt} = \dfrac{7200}{12} = 600 \ \text{N/bolt} = 0.6 \ \text{kN/bolt}
$$

**Step 4: Factor of safety.**

$$
FS = \dfrac{F_{allow}}{F_{bolt}} = \dfrac{6.0}{0.6} = 10
$$

**Conclusion:** The bolts are **vastly over-designed** ($FS = 10$), confirming that bolts are used here as a **redundancy/safety feature** rather than as the primary load path.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU 2024 Mark-Deduction Traps in "Bulkhead to Hull" Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor of 3 to 5.** Always divide ultimate strength by $n_{sf}$ to get allowable. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Mixing seawater ($\rho = 1025 \ \text{kg/m}^3$) and freshwater ($\rho = 1000 \ \text{kg/m}^3$) densities.** Sea is heavier — wrong density gives a 2.5% under-design that examiners WILL catch. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Reporting units inconsistently** (Pa vs. kPa vs. MPa). Use SI base units in derivation, then convert at the end. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Omitting the sketch** in part (b) questions. KTU mandates a diagram for full marks in 14-mark questions. (–2 to –3 marks)
> 5. **Confusing bondline shear with peel stress.** Shear is parallel to the joint; peel is perpendicular (out-of-plane). Examiners specifically test this distinction. (–2 marks)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Bulkhead-to-Hull Joints:**
> - The joint is a **bonded + mechanical hybrid transition zone**, not a simple connection.
> - Three functions: **load transfer, stiffening, sealing**.
> - Minimum **fillet radius $R_f \geq 25$ mm** for FRP.
> - Minimum **tabbing overlap $L_{ov} \geq 50$ mm** or $30 \times$ laminate thickness.
> - Seawater density = **$\rho_{sw} = 1025 \ \text{kg/m}^3$** (not 1000).
> - Hydrostatic pressure formula: **$P_{hs} = \rho g h$**.
> - Bondline shear: **$\tau = V / L_{ov}$**.
> - Safety factor for FRP: **$n_{sf} = 3$ to $5$**.
> - Bolts provide **redundancy**, not primary load path.
> - Five joint types: **bonded tabbed, fillet, bolted, co-cured, core-bonded**.
> - Common failure modes: **peel at fillet root, bondline shear, bolt shear, substrate cracking, core crushing**.
> - Always include a **sketch** in 14-mark answers.
> - KTU standard code references: **ISO 12215-5, RCD Category B/C, DNV-GL Pt.3 Ch.4**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hull Moulding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hull Moulding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hull Moulding** is the fundamental manufacturing process in composite boat construction wherein a boat hull is fabricated by laying down successive layers of fibre reinforcements (glass, carbon, or aramid) impregnated with a thermosetting resin matrix (polyester, vinylester, or epoxy) onto a prepared mould surface, followed by controlled curing, consolidation, and demoulding to yield the final monolithic or sandwich hull structure.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, hull moulding integrates **process engineering**, **materials science**, and **marine design** to translate a 2D lines plan into a 3D load-bearing composite shell that meets classification society rules (e.g., DNV, Lloyd's Register, IRS).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Jello Mould Analogy:** Think of hull moulding like making a shaped gelatin dessert. The *mould* is the container (cake tin, bundt pan) that decides the dessert's final geometry. The *release agent* is the thin oil/butter film that prevents sticking. The *gelcoat* is the shiny outer skin. The *fibreglass + resin* is the structural body — analogous to the gelatin itself, which sets into a rigid shape after cooling. If the mould is dirty, warped, or under-released, the dessert (hull) will be flawed, sticking, or distorted. Just as a chef selects the right tin for the cake, a naval architect chooses the right mould type (male/female, single/multi-piece) for the hull.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold)

- **Gelcoat thickness:** 0.4 mm to **0.6 mm** (typical marine standard)
- **Standard mould release wax application:** 4 to 6 coats with buffing
- **Curing temperature for polyester resins:** 18 °C to **25 °C** ambient workshop
- **Room temperature cure epoxy post-cure:** 50 °C to **80 °C**
- **Standard laminate layup rate:** 1 layer of 450 g/m² CSM ≈ **0.5 mm** laminate thickness
- **Demoulding time (polyester, room temp):** **24 hours** (typical)
- **Mould shrinkage allowance:** **0.1 % to 0.3 %** of mould dimension

### Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Plug** | The master pattern (usually timber/foam) from which the mould is taken |
| **Mould** | The working tool that shapes the hull |
| **Gelcoat** | Pigmented resin surface layer (0.4–0.6 mm) |
| **Layup** | The hand-placement of reinforcement + resin |
| **Cure** | Cross-linking reaction converting resin to solid |
| **Demoulding** | Separation of cured laminate from mould |
| **Flash line** | Joint/seam line on moulded part |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hull Moulding Process — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Hull moulding follows a strict sequence. Skipping or misordering any step causes **delamination, voids, warping, or mould damage**.

**Step 1 — Design & Lines Plan Translation**
- Naval architect drafts the hull lines plan (sheer, profile, body plan, half-breadth).
- Lines are translated to a 1:1 plug or master surface using lofting (manual or CNC).

**Step 2 — Plug Fabrication**
- Plug materials: timber, plaster, high-density polyurethane foam, CNC-machined MDF.
- Plug surface must be mirror-smooth (sanded to P400–P600 grit) and sealed with multiple coats of polyester primer.

**Step 3 — Mould Fabrication from the Plug**
- Apply release agent → gelcoat → first chop-strand mat (CSM) layer → progressively thicker laminates → cure → demould the mould → trim and reinforce mould edges with steel/FRP stiffeners.

**Step 4 — Mould Preparation for Production**
- 4–6 coats of release wax (Carnauba-based) buffed between coats.
- Application of PVA (polyvinyl alcohol) liquid release as a backup barrier.
- Final wipe with mould cleaner (styrene/MIBK solvent).

**Step 5 — Gelcoat Application**
- Spray or brush **pigmented gelcoat resin** at **0.4–0.6 mm** wet film thickness.
- Allow to reach "tack-free but still chemically active" state (typically 1–4 hrs depending on temperature).

**Step 6 — Structural Laminate Layup**
- First ply: 100–225 g/m² surface mat (prevents print-through of CSM).
- Subsequent plies: 450 g/m² CSM alternating with 600–800 g/m² woven roving (WR) for balanced strength.
- Each layer rolled with metal/serpentine rollers to consolidate and expel trapped air.

**Step 7 — Cure and Demoulding**
- Ambient cure (18–25 °C) for 24 hours, or accelerated with heat (50–80 °C post-cure).
- Demould using wooden wedges, compressed air, or hydraulic jacks at strategic flange points.
- **NEVER use metal pry bars** — they chip mould surfaces.

**Step 8 — Trimming and Post-Cure**
- Trim excess laminate (flash) using diamond or carbide cutters.
- Post-cure at elevated temperature for full cross-linking (improves chemical resistance and HDT).

### 2.2 Types of Moulds (KTU Syllabus Coverage)

| Mould Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Female (Cavity) Mould** | Concave surface; gelcoat on inside; hull exterior quality is high | Small boats, dinghies, canoes |
| **Male (Plug-side) Mould** | Convex surface; gelcoat on outside; hull interior quality is high | Large yachts, deck moulds |
| **Single-piece Mould** | One-piece construction | Hulls without undercuts |
| **Multi-piece Mould** | 2–5 piece split (with flanges bolted together) | Complex hulls with propeller tunnels, swim platforms |
| **Open Mould (Contact Moulding)** | Hand layup, no pressure applied | 90 % of small craft worldwide |
| **Closed Mould (RTM / Vacuum Infusion)** | Mould pairs closed; resin injected/infused under pressure | High-performance, low-emission builds |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Critical formulas and metrics for hull moulding problems.

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Laminate thickness per CSM layer | $t_{CSM} = \dfrac{w_{CSM}}{\rho_{f} \cdot V_{f}}$ | $\approx 0.5$ mm for 450 g/m² |
| 2 | Fibre Volume Fraction | $V_{f} = \dfrac{w_{f}}{\rho_{f} \cdot t}$ | 25 %–35 % (hand layup) |
| 3 | Resin-to-Fibre Ratio (mass) | $R = \dfrac{m_{r}}{m_{f}}$ | 2:1 to 3:1 (hand layup CSM) |
| 4 | Gelcoat Coverage | $A_{g} = \dfrac{V_{g}}{t_{g}}$ | m² coverage per kg resin |
| 5 | Mould Shrinkage Compensation | $L_{mould} = L_{hull} (1 + S)$ | $S = 0.001$ to $0.003$ |
| 6 | Vacuum Infusion Pressure Drop | $\Delta P = \dfrac{12 \mu Q L}{w h^{3}}$ (planar flow) | Pa; Hagen-Poiseuille planar form |
| 7 | Cure Exotherm Peak (Polyester) | $T_{peak} \approx T_{amb} + \dfrac{H_{r}}{m \cdot c_{p}}$ | 90 °C–110 °C in thick laminates |
| 8 | Demoulding Force (rough estimate) | $F \approx \mu \cdot A \cdot P_{atm}$ | N; depends on adhesion |
| 9 | Composite Density | $\rho_{c} = V_{f} \rho_{f} + (1 - V_{f}) \rho_{m}$ | kg/m³ |
| 10 | Rule of Mixtures (Strength) | $\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{f} V_{f} + \sigma_{m} (1 - V_{f})$ | MPa (parallel loading) |

> **Standard Reference Values Used:**
> - $\rho_{glass} = 2550 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$
> - $\rho_{polyester} = 1150 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$
> - $\rho_{epoxy} = 1200 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$
> - Atmospheric pressure $P_{atm} = 101.3$ kPa

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hull moulding is the **gateway process** that determines:
- **Surface finish quality** (gelcoat integrity)
- **Structural performance** (fibre orientation, void content)
- **Production rate** (mould turnaround time)
- **Cost per boat** (labour vs. material, mould amortisation)

In shipyards, mould selection is a strategic decision — a single female mould may cost ₹15–50 lakhs but produces 500+ hulls over its lifetime, amortising the cost to under ₹10,000 per hull.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Laminate Thickness for a Given Hull Plating

**Problem context:** A boat designer specifies a hull bottom laminate of **6 mm** total thickness using alternating layers of **450 g/m² CSM** and **800 g/m² WR**. Determine the number of layers required and the final fibre volume fraction.

#### Step 1 — Establish Layer Properties

For **450 g/m² CSM** with 30 % fibre volume fraction in cured laminate:

$$t_{CSM} = \dfrac{w_{CSM}}{\rho_{c}} = \dfrac{0.450 \text{ kg/m}^{2}}{1450 \text{ kg/m}^{3}} \approx 0.310 \text{ mm (per ply)}$$

For **800 g/m² WR** with 45 % fibre volume fraction in cured laminate:

$$t_{WR} = \dfrac{w_{WR}}{\rho_{c}} = \dfrac{0.800 \text{ kg/m}^{2}}{1550 \text{ kg/m}^{3}} \approx 0.516 \text{ mm (per ply)}$$

#### Step 2 — Define the Stacking Sequence

Layup sequence (standard marine practice): **CSM / WR / CSM / WR / CSM / WR / ...**

A pair (CSM + WR) gives:

$$t_{pair} = t_{CSM} + t_{WR} = 0.310 + 0.516 = 0.826 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate Number of Layers for 6 mm

$$\text{Number of pairs} = \dfrac{6.0}{0.826} \approx 7.26$$

Round up to **8 pairs** (= 16 plies), giving actual thickness:

$$t_{actual} = 8 \times 0.826 = 6.608 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 4 — Final Fibre Volume Fraction

Total mass of fibre per m² of laminate:

$$m_{f} = 8 \times (0.450 + 0.800) = 10.0 \text{ kg/m}^{2}$$

Total mass of laminate per m²:

$$m_{c} = \rho_{c} \cdot t_{actual} = 1500 \times 0.006608 \approx 9.91 \text{ kg/m}^{2}$$

Overall fibre volume fraction:

$$V_{f} = \dfrac{m_{f}/\rho_{f}}{m_{c}/\rho_{c}} = \dfrac{10.0 / 2550}{9.91 / 1500} \approx 0.594$$

$$V_{f} \approx 35.0\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A fibre volume fraction between **30 %–40 %** is the target range for hand-laid hull laminates. Below 25 % → resin-rich, weak, heavy. Above 45 % → wetting-out problems, dry spots, voids.

### 3.2 Derivation: Mould Shrinkage Compensation

**Problem context:** A designer wants a moulded hull length of exactly **12.000 m**. The moulding system exhibits a total volumetric shrinkage of **0.2 %**. What dimension must the mould (mould cavity) be cut to?

#### Step 1 — Define Shrinkage Direction

Shrinkage in hand layup polyester is approximately isotropic in the laminate plane:

$$S = 0.002 \quad (\text{0.2 \% linear})$$

#### Step 2 — Apply Compensation Formula

Mould dimension must be **larger** than the final hull dimension:

$$L_{mould} = L_{hull} (1 + S) = 12.000 \times (1 + 0.002)$$

$$L_{mould} = 12.000 \times 1.002 = 12.024 \text{ m}$$

#### Step 3 — Verify Result

Final demoulded length after shrinkage:

$$L_{final} = \dfrac{L_{mould}}{1+S} = \dfrac{12.024}{1.002} = 12.000 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Resin Demand for Hull Layup

**Problem context:** A hull bottom of **40 m²** surface area is to be laminated using the sequence in §3.1. Calculate total resin required (assume 2.5:1 resin-to-fibre ratio by mass).

#### Step 1 — Mass of Fibre

Total fibre mass (from §3.1):

$$m_{f} = 10.0 \text{ kg/m}^{2} \times 40 \text{ m}^{2} = 400 \text{ kg}$$

#### Step 2 — Mass of Resin

$$m_{r} = 2.5 \times m_{f} = 2.5 \times 400 = 1000 \text{ kg}$$

#### Step 3 — Volume of Resin (for storage/tank sizing)

$$V_{r} = \dfrac{m_{r}}{\rho_{r}} = \dfrac{1000}{1150} \approx 0.870 \text{ m}^{3} = 870 \text{ L}$$

#### Step 4 — Gelcoat Volume (separate)

$$V_{gel} = A \times t_{g} = 40 \text{ m}^{2} \times 0.0005 \text{ m} = 0.020 \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$V_{gel} = 20 \text{ L of gelcoat resin (plus 2\% MEKP catalyst)}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Moulding Techniques

| Parameter | Hand Layup (Open) | Vacuum Infusion (VARTM) | RTM (Closed) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{f}$ achievable | 25–35 % | 45–55 % | 50–60 % |
| Void content | 3–5 % | < 1 % | < 0.5 % |
| VOC emissions | High | Moderate | Low |
| Labour intensity | High | Moderate | Low |
| Cycle time | 24–48 h | 4–8 h | 1–2 h |
| Mould cost | Low | Moderate | High (steel) |
| Part thickness | Limited uniformity | Excellent | Excellent |
| Suitable for hulls | Yes | Yes (premium) | Yes (series) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hull Moulding Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Lines Plan and Lofting] --> B0[Plug Fabrication]
    B0 --> C0[Plug Surface Finish and Sealing]
    C0 --> D0[Release Agent Application on Plug]
    D0 --> E0[Mould Layup CSM plus WR Layers]
    E0 --> F0[Mould Cure and Demould]
    F0 --> G0[Mould Trimming and Stiffening]
    G0 --> H0[Working Mould Ready]
    H0 --> I0[Production Release Agent Wax]
    I0 --> J0[Gelcoat Spray 0.4 to 0.6 mm]
    J0 --> K0[Tack Stage Wait 1 to 4 hr]
    K0 --> L0[Structural Layup CSM plus WR Alternating]
    L0 --> M0[Roller Consolidation and De-airing]
    M0 --> N0[Optional Sandwich Core Insertion]
    N0 --> O0[Final Outer Skin Layup]
    O0 --> P0[Cure Period 24 hr at 25 deg C]
    P0 --> Q0[Demould with Wedges and Air Pressure]
    Q0 --> R0[Trimming and Post-cure]
    R0 --> S0[Finished Hull Component]
```

### 4.2 Mould Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Hull Design Concept] --> B1{Undercuts Present?}
    B1 -- No --> C1[Single-piece Female Mould]
    B1 -- Yes --> D1[Multi-piece Split Mould]
    C1 --> E1{Production Volume?}
    D1 --> E1
    E1 -- Low less than 50 units --> F1[Open Hand Layup Mould]
    E1 -- Medium 50 to 500 units --> G1[Vacuum Infusion Mould]
    E1 -- High above 500 units --> H1[RTM Closed Mould]
    F1 --> I1[Material FRP on Timber Frame]
    G1 --> I1
    H1 --> J1[Material Steel or Invar with Heated Platen]
```

### 4.3 Laminate Stacking Sequence Cross-Section (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Layer 1 Gelcoat 0.5 mm] --> S2[Layer 2 Surface Mat 100 gsm]
    S2 --> S3[Layer 3 CSM 450 gsm]
    S3 --> S4[Layer 4 WR 800 gsm]
    S4 --> S5[Layer 5 CSM 450 gsm]
    S5 --> S6[Layer 6 WR 800 gsm]
    S6 --> S7[Layer 7 CSM 450 gsm]
    S7 --> S8[Layer 8 WR 800 gsm]
    S8 --> S9[Layer 9 CSM 450 gsm]
    S9 --> S10[Layer 10 Surface Mat 100 gsm]
```

### 4.4 Hull Layup Layperson Schematic

> The Mermaid syntax cannot realistically depict a 3D boat hull — therefore the following **block-level conceptual schematic** maps the interactions in the layup environment:

