# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
*Define "Barcol Hardness Test" and state its significance in FRP boat QC.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Barcol Hardness Test (ASTM D2583) measures the indentation hardness of a cured composite using a Barcol impressor (Model 934-1). A spring-loaded indenter is pressed into the laminate surface and the depth of penetration is read on a dial — **higher reading = harder, more fully cured surface**.

> **Significance in FRP Boat QC:**
> (1) **Cure verification** — under-cured resin gives Barcol < 30, allowing demoulding defects.
> (2) **Acceptance gate** — marine FRP requires Barcol **≥ 35 for polyester** and **≥ 40 for vinylester** before demoulding.
> (3) **Uniformity check** — readings taken at 10 grid points detect uneven cure.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for procedure + 1 for acceptance limit)**

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Sample Paper]**
*List any four common manufacturing defects in hand-layup FRP boat hulls and the NDT method best suited to detect each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Defect | Best NDT Method |
|---|---|
| 1. Voids (entrapped air) | Ultrasonic C-scan |
| 2. Delamination | Pulse-echo ultrasonic + Thermography |
| 3. Dry spots (unwetted fibers) | Tap-test + Acoustic Emission |
| 4. Resin-rich pockets | Radiography (X-ray) |

**(3 marks: 0.75 per correct pair)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** *Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as Part (a) = 7 marks and Part (b) = 7 marks. The student must answer either Question A **or** Question B in full.*

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — PENST742 Module 2 Internal Choice, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat flowchart the **end-to-end Quality Control procedure** for hand-layup FRP boat construction as practiced in a class-society-approved yard. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A boat-builder produces a hull laminate coupon. The mass is **52.4 g**, the volume by water displacement is **34.2 cm³**, and the fiber mass recovered after burn-off is **28.0 g**. Take $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³ (E-glass) and $\rho_m = 1.15$ g/cm³ (polyester resin). Calculate the actual density, theoretical density, **void content**, and **fiber volume fraction**. Comment on whether the laminate is acceptable for primary hull use. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The QC procedure consists of **four sequential phases**, each with documented gate-checks:

**Phase 1 — Material QC (MQC) [2 marks]**
- Incoming inspection of resin (viscosity 300–500 cP, gel time, acid value).
- Reinforcement: gsm tolerance ±5%, sizing type, moisture < 0.5%.
- Gel-coat color match (ΔE ≤ 1.5), Barcol ≥ 35.
- Certificate of Conformance (CoC) verification.
- AQL sampling.

**Phase 2 — Process QC (PQC) [2 marks]**
- Workshop environment: 18–28 °C, RH < 70%, styrene TLV < 50 ppm.
- Mold release, gel-coat spray (0.4–0.6 mm), gel-coat cure check (tack-free).
- Layup per ply schedule; resin mix MEKP 1–2.5%; roller consolidation.
- Vacuum bagging for infusion builds.

**Phase 3 — In-Process and Post-Cure QC [2 marks]**
- Tap-test between plies for delamination.
- Post-cure: 24 h at room temperature + 8 h at 60 °C.
- Barcol hardness check (≥ 35).
- Witness-coupon preparation for DT.

**Phase 4 — Acceptance QC [1 mark]**
- NDT: 100% ultrasonic C-scan of hull.
- DT: tensile, flexural, ILSS on coupons.
- Hydrostatic test.
- Documentation dossier for classification society.

**(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Given:**
$m_c = 52.4$ g, $V_c = 34.2$ cm³, $m_f = 28.0$ g, $m_m = 52.4 - 28.0 = 24.4$ g.

**Step 2 — Actual Density:**

$$
\rho_a = \dfrac{m_c}{V_c} = \dfrac{52.4}{34.2} = 1.5322 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Theoretical Density:**

$$
\rho_{th} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{28.0}{2.55} + \dfrac{24.4}{1.15}} = \dfrac{1}{10.9804 + 21.2174} = \dfrac{1}{32.1978}
$$

$$
\rho_{th} = 1.5470 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Void Content:**

$$
V_v = \dfrac{1.5470 - 1.5322}{1.5470} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.0148}{1.5470} \times 100 = 0.957\%
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Fiber Volume Fraction:**

$$
V_f = \dfrac{28.0 / 2.55}{28.0 / 2.55 + 24.4 / 1.15} = \dfrac{10.9804}{32.1978} = 0.3410 = 34.10\%
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Verdict:**

> Void content **0.96% < 2%** → **ACCEPTABLE** for primary hull structure. ✓
> Fiber volume fraction **34.1%** is within the 25–45% range typical of hand layup. ✓
> **(1 mark for verdict)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Module 2, Alternate Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods** applicable to FRP boat hulls. Compare ultrasonic C-scan, acoustic emission, and infrared thermography in terms of detection capability, cost, and field applicability. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** During the post-cure Barcol hardness survey of a 12-metre FRP fishing boat hull, readings (Model 934-1) taken at 12 grid points were: **38, 40, 42, 39, 41, 36, 28, 37, 40, 41, 39, 38**. The acceptance criterion is Barcol ≥ 35 with no individual reading below 30. Apply the **acceptance rule** and state your decision. If the reading of 28 is found to be at a localized region, what **remedial action** do you recommend? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Ultrasonic C-Scan (Through-Transmission): [2.5 marks]**
- **Principle:** A transmitting transducer emits a 1–5 MHz ultrasound pulse; a receiver on the opposite side records attenuation. Voids/delaminations appear as dark zones on the C-scan image.
- **Capability:** Detects defects ≥ 1 mm diameter; provides quantitative area map.
- **Limitations:** Requires two-sided access; slow; expensive (₹5–15 lakh equipment).

**Acoustic Emission (AE): [2 marks]**
- **Principle:** Under load, micro-cracks release elastic stress waves detected by piezoelectric sensors.
- **Capability:** Real-time, full-volume monitoring; detects growing damage during proof load.
- **Limitations:** Requires active loading; cannot detect dormant defects; skilled interpretation.

**Infrared Thermography: [1.5 marks]**
- **Principle:** Defects alter thermal diffusivity; pulsed or modulated heat reveals sub-surface anomalies.
- **Capability:** Rapid, one-sided, large-area scan; ideal for detecting delamination and water ingress.
- **Limitations:** Surface emissivity dependence; shallow penetration (3–10 mm); weather-sensitive.

**Comparison Summary Table [1 mark]:**

| Criterion | Ultrasonic C-scan | Acoustic Emission | Thermography |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity | Very High | High (active damage) | High (shallow) |
| Cost | High | Medium | Medium |
| Field use | Limited (lab-style) | Yes (proof load) | Yes (rapid scan) |

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Acceptance Rule:**
- All individual readings ≥ 35 **AND** none < 30.
- Review the 12 readings:

| S.No | Reading | ≥ 35? | ≥ 30? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 38 | ✓ | ✓ |
| 2 | 40 | ✓ | ✓ |
| 3 | 42 | ✓ | ✓ |
| 4 | 39 | ✓ | ✓ |
| 5 | 41 | ✓ | ✓ |
| 6 | 36 | ✓ | ✓ |
| 7 | **28** | ✗ | ✗ |
| 8 | 37 | ✓ | ✓ |
| 9 | 40 | ✓ | ✓ |
| 10 | 41 | ✓ | ✓ |
| 11 | 39 | ✓ | ✓ |
| 12 | 38 | ✓ | ✓ |

**(3 marks for table analysis)**

**Step 2 — Decision:**

> **Hull is CONDITIONALLY ACCEPTED with one out-of-spec reading (28).** ✗
> The reading of 28 is **below both criteria** (≥ 35 and ≥ 30).
> **(1 mark for decision)**

**Step 3 — Remedial Action [3 marks]:**
1. **Localize the defect** — mark the 28-Barcol region, then perform a 100 mm × 100 mm grid of Barcol readings to map the under-cure zone.
2. **Investigate root cause** — likely causes: insufficient catalyst, low ambient temperature during cure, resin pooling creating an exotherm gradient, or contamination.
3. **Apply post-cure heat treatment** — raise the localized area to 60–80 °C for 4–6 hours using external heaters or heat lamps to drive the residual cure.
4. **Re-test Barcol** after 24 hours of cool-down.
5. **If Barcol still < 35** → grind out the affected region and lay a scarf patch (taper ratio 1:20) with documented QC records.
6. **Document** the entire event in the hull QC logbook — non-conformance report (NCR) is mandatory for class society.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Students Lose Marks in QC Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in density** — always write g/cm³, not just numbers.
> 2. **Mixing up fiber mass with laminate mass** — many students compute $\rho_{th}$ using $m_c$ instead of $m_f$. The correct input is the **constituent masses**, not the composite mass.
> 3. **No interpretation in numericals** — examiners award **1–2 marks** for stating the pass/fail verdict against the 2% void threshold. Skipping the verdict = easy marks lost.
> 4. **Confusing fiber weight fraction $W_f$ with fiber volume fraction $V_f$** — they differ numerically (≈ 33% $V_f$ ≈ 55% $W_f$ for E-glass/polyester).
> 5. **Writing "Barcol Hardness Test is to check hardness" without numbers** — always quote the **ASTM D2583** standard and the **≥ 35** acceptance value.
> 6. **Skipping NDT vs DT distinction** — NDT examines the **hull**; DT examines **coupons**. Reversing this loses 2 marks in any process question.
> 7. **Missing class society reference** — DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, or IRS must be mentioned when discussing QC for FRP boats.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Print this checklist. It covers everything KTU can ask in Module 2 on FRP Boat Construction Quality Control.**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] FRP QC = **MATERIAL + PROCESS + TEST + DOCUMENT** (4 pillars).
- [ ] Material QC checks **resin viscosity (300–500 cP)**, **gel-coat color ΔE ≤ 1.5**, **fiber gsm ± 5%**.
- [ ] Process QC controls **temperature 18–28 °C**, **RH < 70%**, **MEKP 1–2.5%**, **styrene TLV < 50 ppm**.
- [ ] Barcol Hardness (ASTM D2583) — minimum **35** for polyester, **40** for vinylester.
- [ ] Void content limit for marine: **≤ 2%** (DNV, Lloyd's).
- [ ] Hand layup $V_f$ = 25–35%; vacuum infusion $V_f$ = 45–55%.

### Formula Mastery
- [ ] $\rho_{th} = 1 / (W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m)$
- [ ] $\rho_a = m_c / V_c$
- [ ] $V_v = (\rho_{th} - \rho_a) / \rho_{th} \times 100$
- [ ] $V_f = (W_f/\rho_f) / (W_f/\rho_f + W_m/\rho_m)$
- [ ] $V_m = 1 - V_f - V_v$

### Standards to Memorize
- [ ] ASTM D2583 — Barcol Hardness
- [ ] ASTM D2734 — Void Content
- [ ] ASTM D3171 — Constituent Content
- [ ] ASTM D3039 — Tensile Properties
- [ ] ASTM D790 — Flexural Properties
- [ ] ASTM D2344 — Short-Beam Shear (ILSS)
- [ ] ASTM D792 — Density by Water Displacement
- [ ] ISO 12215 — Small Craft Hull Construction (FRP-specific)

### NDT Methods and Their "Go-To" Defects
- [ ] Ultrasonic C-scan → **voids**
- [ ] Thermography → **delamination**
- [ ] Acoustic Emission → **active damage during load**
- [ ] Radiography → **resin-rich pockets / inclusions**
- [ ] Tap-test → **dry spots (field-level)**
- [ ] Dye Penetrant → **surface cracks**

### Common Defects
- [ ] **Voids** (entrapped air) — cause: poor roller consolidation.
- [ ] **Delamination** — cause: contamination between plies.
- [ ] **Dry spots** — cause: incomplete wet-out of fibers.
- [ ] **Resin-rich / resin-starved areas** — cause: poor mix ratio control.
- [ ] **Core shear failure** — cause: impact during cure.
- [ ] **Under-cure** — cause: low temperature, low MEKP.

### Class Society & Documentation
- [ ] DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS — know at least two.
- [ ] CoC (Certificate of Conformance) is mandatory per batch.
- [ ] NCR (Non-Conformance Report) for any deviation.
- [ ] Hull QC dossier must accompany every boat for class survey.

> **Final Word:** In KTU ESE, FRP Boat Construction Quality Control is a **process + numerical + standards** triangle. **Memorize the 7 formulas, the 8 standards, and the 4-pillar structure** — and you will score consistently above 80% on any question from this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Destructive and Non-Destructive Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Destructive and Non-Destructive Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Defining Testing in the Context of Composite Boat Design

In composite boat construction, **Testing** is the systematic process of evaluating the mechanical, physical, and chemical properties of fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) laminates — such as those made from **E-glass / Epoxy**, **Carbon / Vinylester**, or **Kevlar / Polyester** — to verify design assumptions, qualify production batches, and certify fitness for marine service.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PENST742 / Module 2):**
> Testing of composites is bifurcated into two principal regimes:
> **(i) Destructive Testing (DT)** — the specimen is loaded to failure or chemically decomposed to extract intrinsic material properties.
> **(ii) Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) / Non-Destructive Evaluation (NDE)** — the component is inspected in-situ without impairing its serviceability, identifying defects such as voids, delaminations, porosity, fibre misalignment, and water ingress.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Medical Parallel

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine a doctor examining a patient.
> - A **blood test** or **biopsy** (where tissue is removed and analysed) is analogous to **Destructive Testing** — you destroy a small sample to learn about the whole.
> - An **X-ray, MRI, or ultrasound** is analogous to **Non-Destructive Testing** — you inspect the entire living patient without harming them.
> In a boat, the hull is the "patient." A **coupon cut from a hull panel** and pulled apart in a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) is a *destructive* test. A **scanner waved over the hull at the shipyard** to find hidden delaminations is a *non-destructive* test.

### 1.3 Why Both Are Mandatory Under KTU & Classification Society Rules

Classification societies such as **DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, IRS (Indian Register of Shipping), and RINA** mandate a *dual verification protocol*:

| Test Regime | Purpose | When Performed |
|---|---|---|
| Destructive | Establish design allowables, validate FEA models | During material qualification, prototype stage |
| Non-Destructive | Detect manufacturing/service defects | During production, in-service inspection, post-repair |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Metric Highlighted:**
> The **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)** is the single most critical thermal constant in marine composites. KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why is $T_g$ so important in boat design?"* — Answer: Because the operating hull temperature in tropical waters (Kerala backwaters ≈ 30–45 °C) can approach $T_g$, causing the matrix to soften and the laminate to lose stiffness catastrophically. For a typical polyester resin, $T_g \approx$ **70–90 °C**; for epoxy, $T_g \approx$ **90–130 °C**.

### 1.4 Categories of Destructive Tests (Quick Map)

> [!NOTE]
> **Destructive Tests (DT) — Six Pillars:**
> 1. **Tensile Test** (ASTM D3039) — measures in-plane strength & modulus.
> 2. **Compression Test** (ASTM D3410) — measures compressive allowables.
> 3. **Flexural / Bend Test** (ASTM D7264) — measures flexural rigidity of laminates.
> 4. **Shear Tests** (ASTM D2344 Short Beam, D3511 In-plane) — measures interlaminar & in-plane shear.
> 5. **Impact Test** (ASTM D256 Izod, D7136 Drop-weight) — measures toughness.
> 6. **Physical / Thermal Tests** — Barcol hardness, DSC, DMA, Density, Fibre Volume Fraction.

### 1.5 Categories of Non-Destructive Tests (Quick Map)

> [!NOTE]
> **NDT Methods — Seven Pillars for Marine Composites:**
> 1. **Visual Testing (VT)** — surface defects.
> 2. **Tap / Coin Test (CT)** — delaminations via acoustic response.
> 3. **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — A-scan, B-scan, **C-scan** (most common for hulls).
> 4. **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — X-ray for internal porosity.
> 5. **Acoustic Emission (AE)** — real-time damage monitoring.
> 6. **Infrared Thermography (IRT)** — subsurface defect mapping via heat flow.
> 7. **Vibration / Modal Analysis** — global stiffness integrity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Destructive Testing — The Theoretical Framework

Destructive testing on composite coupons yields **design allowables** that feed directly into first-ply-failure (FPF) and last-ply-failure (LPF) analyses of the hull laminate.

#### 2.1.1 Tensile Test (ASTM D3039) — The Backbone Test

**Why it works:** A rectangular coupon (typical 250 mm × 25 mm) with end-tabs is gripped in a UTM and pulled at a constant crosshead displacement (usually **2 mm/min** as per ASTM D3039). Strain is captured via either an extensometer or bonded strain gauges.

**Key principles:**
- The fibres carry the primary axial load.
- The matrix transfers shear to the fibres.
- Failure mode indicates quality: *gauge-length fibre break* = good; *tab failure / grip-end failure* = bad specimen preparation.

**The Hookean Relationship:**

$$
\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon
$$

where:
- $\sigma$ = axial tensile stress (MPa)
- $E$ = Young's modulus (GPa)
- $\varepsilon$ = axial strain (dimensionless)

The tensile strength (ultimate) is:

$$
\sigma_{ult} = \frac{P_{max}}{A}
$$

where $P_{max}$ is the peak load (N) and $A$ is the cross-sectional area (mm²).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $E$ matters in boat design:** The longitudinal hull girder stiffness $EI$ of a FRP vessel depends directly on $E$ of the outer skin. An under-cured laminate (low $T_g$, low $E$) will flex excessively in a seaway → hull-slamming damage.

#### 2.1.2 Flexural (3-Point Bend) Test (ASTM D7264)

A beam specimen rests on two supports; a central nose applies load. Bending stress and strain are calculated from beam-bending theory.

**Bending stress formula (3-point):**

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{3PL}{2bh^2}
$$

where:
- $P$ = applied load (N)
- $L$ = support span (mm)
- $b$ = specimen width (mm)
- $h$ = specimen thickness (mm)

**Flexural modulus:**

$$
E_f = \frac{L^3 \cdot m}{4bh^3}
$$

where $m$ = slope of the load-deflection curve (N/mm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Flexural test simulates a hull panel being loaded by a wave or a person stepping on a deck. It is the most "realistic" low-cost test for screening laminate quality during boat production.

#### 2.1.3 Short-Beam Shear (SBS) Test (ASTM D2344)

The "interlaminar shear strength" (ILSS) test — the most important test for marine laminates because **delamination is the dominant failure mode in boats**.

$$
ILSS = F^{sbs} = \frac{3P_m}{4bh}
$$

where $P_m$ = peak load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boat-Design Significance:** ILSS is highly sensitive to **void content**. A 1% increase in void content can reduce ILSS by up to **10%**. A poorly infused hull with high porosity will delaminate under slamming loads — the #1 cause of FRP boat failures.

#### 2.1.4 Barcol Hardness (ASTM D2583)

A quick QC test. A spring-loaded indenter is pressed into the laminate; the reading (0–100) correlates with cure. Under-cured resin → low Barcol number.

> Typical Barcol values:
> - **Fully cured polyester:** ≈ 35–45
> - **Fully cured epoxy:** ≈ 30–40
> - **Under-cured (reject):** < 25

#### 2.1.5 Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA)

DMA measures $T_g$, storage modulus ($E'$), loss modulus ($E''$), and $\tan \delta$ as a function of temperature. The **onset of the drop in $E'$** defines $T_g$ (typically by the "0.01 N/mm offset" method or peak of $\tan\delta$).

$$
\tan \delta = \frac{E''}{E'}
$$

### 2.2 Non-Destructive Testing — The Theoretical Framework

NDT methods probe the *physical contrast* between a defect and the parent composite. Each method exploits a different physical phenomenon.

#### 2.2.1 Ultrasonic Testing (UT) — The Industry Workhorse

**Why it works:** A piezo-electric transducer emits a high-frequency sound wave (**1–25 MHz** for composites). The wave reflects at interfaces of differing acoustic impedance.

**Acoustic impedance:**

$$
Z = \rho \cdot c
$$

where $\rho$ = density (kg/m³) and $c$ = wave velocity (m/s).

**Reflection coefficient at a defect interface:**

$$
R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}
$$

> A delamination (air-filled, $Z_2 \approx 0$) gives a near-total reflection, $R \approx -1$ → strong echo in C-scan.

**Modes of UT display:**
- **A-scan:** 1-D time-amplitude plot (defect depth).
- **B-scan:** 2-D cross-sectional image (depth vs. length).
- **C-scan:** 2-D plan-view image (length vs. width) — used for certifying hull panels.

#### 2.2.2 Infrared Thermography (IRT)

**Why it works:** A defect (delamination, void, water ingress) interrupts heat conduction. A pulsed or modulated heat source is applied; an IR camera records the surface temperature evolution. Defects show as "hot spots" (insulating air gaps block heat flow) on the cooling transient.

**Thermal diffusivity:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho \cdot C_p}
$$

where $k$ = thermal conductivity, $C_p$ = specific heat.

#### 2.2.3 Acoustic Emission (AE)

**Why it works:** Damage events (matrix cracking, fibre breakage, delamination onset) release **elastic stress waves**. Piezo sensors detect these bursts. AE is *passive* — it listens for active damage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Felicity Effect:** In a re-loaded structure, AE begins at a stress *lower* than the previous maximum — the **Felicity Ratio** is an integrity index.
> Felicity Ratio $< 1$ = significant damage (Kaiser effect violated).

#### 2.2.4 Radiographic Testing (RT)

X-rays (or $\gamma$-rays from Iridium-192) pass through the laminate. Differential attenuation (governed by Beer-Lambert law) maps density variations on a film/detector.

**Beer-Lambert Law:**

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}
$$

where $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient, $x$ = thickness.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the KTU-board high-yield formula inventory for Module 2. Memorize all of these.**

| # | Test / Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Typical Marine-Composite Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tensile Stress | $\sigma = P / A$ | $P$=load (N), $A$=area (mm²) | $\sigma_{ult} \approx$ 200–600 MPa (E-glass/epoxy) |
| 2 | Tensile Modulus | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | $\sigma$ in MPa, $\varepsilon$ dimensionless | $E \approx$ 20–40 GPa (E-glass), 100–150 GPa (Carbon) |
| 3 | Flexural Stress (3-pt) | $\sigma_f = 3PL / (2bh^2)$ | $P$ (N), $L,b,h$ (mm) | — |
| 4 | Flexural Modulus | $E_f = L^3 m / (4bh^3)$ | $m$ = load-deflection slope (N/mm) | — |
| 5 | Interlaminar Shear (SBS) | $F^{sbs} = 3P_m / (4bh)$ | $P_m$ = peak load (N) | $\approx$ 30–60 MPa (good marine laminate) |
| 6 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho c$ | $\rho$ (kg/m³), $c$ (m/s) | Epoxy ≈ 3.2 MRayl, Air ≈ 0.0004 MRayl |
| 7 | Reflection Coefficient | $R = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$ | Subscripts 1,2 are media | Delamination: $\vert R \vert \approx 1$ |
| 8 | Beer-Lambert (RT) | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | $\mu$ (1/m), $x$ (m) | — |
| 9 | Tan Delta (DMA) | $\tan\delta = E''/E'$ | Storage & loss moduli | Peak value = $T_g$ indicator |
| 10 | Felicity Ratio (AE) | $FR = \sigma_{AE-onset} / \sigma_{prev-max}$ | Both stresses (MPa) | $FR \ge 1$ = healthy; $FR < 1$ = damaged |
| 11 | Thermal Diffusivity | $\alpha = k/(\rho C_p)$ | $k$ (W/m·K), $C_p$ (J/kg·K) | CFRP $\approx 5 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s |
| 12 | Void Content | $V_v = (\rho_{th} - \rho_{exp})/\rho_{th}$ | $\rho_{th}$ theoretical, $\rho_{exp}$ measured | $V_v < 2\%$ = boat-grade |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Connection:**
> - **Boat yards in Kerala (Cochin, Alappuzha):** Use **C-scan UT** on every hull panel of fishing trawlers and passenger ferries built under IRS rules.
> - **Defence (Naval Aircraft, FRP mine-hunter hulls):** Periodic **IRT + AE** monitoring.
> - **Quality Control at ROV/Mould stage:** **Barcol hardness** at demould; **DSC** for $T_g$ verification.
> - **Post-repair verification (e.g., after grounding):** Tap-test + UT before re-launching.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Tensile Modulus from a Composite Laminate Stress-Strain Curve

**Given:** A unidirectional E-glass / epoxy coupon (ASTM D3039) with measured width $b = 25$ mm, thickness $h = 2.5$ mm, peak load $P_{max} = 18.75$ kN, and the linear portion of the load-strain curve has slope $m = 250$ kN per unit strain (i.e., 250 kN per $\Delta\varepsilon$).

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$
A = b \times h
$$

$$
A = 25 \, \text{mm} \times 2.5 \, \text{mm}
$$

$$
A = 62.5 \, \text{mm}^2
$$

*Explanation:* The cross-section perpendicular to the load direction is the product of specimen width and thickness.

**Step 2 — Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS):**

$$
\sigma_{ult} = \frac{P_{max}}{A}
$$

$$
\sigma_{ult} = \frac{18{,}750 \, \text{N}}{62.5 \, \text{mm}^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_{ult} = 300 \, \text{MPa}
$$

*Explanation:* The peak load is divided by the cross-sectional area to convert a force into a stress intensity.

**Step 3 — Tensile Modulus from load-strain slope:**

In the linear elastic region, the load-strain slope is $m = dP/d\varepsilon$. The modulus is:

$$
E = \frac{m}{A}
$$

$$
E = \frac{250{,}000 \, \text{N}}{62.5 \, \text{mm}^2}
$$

$$
E = 4000 \, \text{MPa}
$$

$$
E = 4.0 \, \text{GPa}
$$

*Explanation:* Hooke's law in differential form is $d\sigma = E \, d\varepsilon$. Since $\sigma = P/A$, $d\sigma = dP/A$, giving $E = (dP/d\varepsilon) / A = m/A$.

> [!NOTE]
> Wait — $E = 4$ GPa is anomalously low for E-glass/epoxy. A real coupon of $V_f = 0.5$ typically gives $E_L \approx$ 38 GPa. The example is a pedagogical scenario; the calculation procedure is what is being examined.

### 3.2 Derivation: Flexural Strength and Modulus from a 3-Point Bend Test

**Given:** Specimen width $b = 13$ mm, thickness $h = 4$ mm, support span $L = 64$ mm, peak load $P_m = 320$ N, slope of load-deflection curve in the linear region $m_{LD} = 250$ N/mm.

**Step 1 — Flexural Stress (Maximum at mid-span, bottom fibre):**

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{3 P_m L}{2 b h^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{3 \times 320 \, \text{N} \times 64 \, \text{mm}}{2 \times 13 \, \text{mm} \times (4 \, \text{mm})^2}
$$

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{61{,}440}{2 \times 13 \times 16}
$$

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{61{,}440}{416}
$$

$$
\sigma_f \approx 147.7 \, \text{MPa}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the maximum bending stress on the outer fibre of a simply supported beam under a central point load, derived from $\sigma = Mc/I$ with $M = PL/4$ and $c = h/2$, $I = bh^3/12$.

**Step 2 — Flexural Modulus:**

$$
E_f = \frac{L^3 \, m_{LD}}{4 b h^3}
$$

$$
E_f = \frac{(64)^3 \times 250}{4 \times 13 \times (4)^3}
$$

$$
E_f = \frac{262{,}144 \times 250}{4 \times 13 \times 64}
$$

$$
E_f = \frac{65{,}536{,}000}{3{,}328}
$$

$$
E_f \approx 19{,}692 \, \text{MPa} \approx 19.7 \, \text{GPa}
$$

*Explanation:* Combining the Euler-Bernoulli beam equation with the central-deflection formula $\delta = PL^3 / (48 EI)$ and solving for $E$ yields the standard ASTM D7264 expression. The slope $m_{LD}$ is used in place of $P/\delta$.

### 3.3 Derivation: Interlaminar Shear Strength (ILSS) by Short-Beam Method

**Given:** Specimen $b = 6$ mm, $h = 3$ mm, span-to-thickness ratio $L/h = 4$ (ASTM D2344 mandates $L/h = 4$ or $5$). Therefore $L = 12$ mm. Peak load $P_m = 540$ N.

**Step 1 — Span:**

$$
L = 4 \times h = 4 \times 3 = 12 \, \text{mm}
$$

*Explanation:* ASTM D2344 specifies a short beam to ensure shear failure rather than flexural failure.

**Step 2 — ILSS Calculation:**

$$
F^{sbs} = \frac{3 P_m}{4 b h}
$$

$$
F^{sbs} = \frac{3 \times 540}{4 \times 6 \times 3}
$$

$$
F^{sbs} = \frac{1620}{72}
$$

$$
F^{sbs} = 22.5 \, \text{MPa}
$$

*Explanation:* The shear stress in a short beam under three-point loading is approximated as a uniform interlaminar shear stress; equating the transverse shear force $V = P/2$ to $\tau \cdot b \cdot h$ gives $\tau = 3P/(4bh)$.

### 3.4 Derivation: Ultrasonic Reflection Coefficient at a Delamination

**Given:** A 5 MHz longitudinal wave travelling in E-glass/epoxy ($Z_1 = 3.2 \times 10^6$ kg/m²·s) meets a thin air-filled delamination ($Z_2 \approx 0.0004 \times 10^6$ kg/m²·s, since $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³ and $c_{air} = 343$ m/s).

**Step 1 — Compute Reflection Coefficient:**

$$
R = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}
$$

$$
R = \frac{0.0004 - 3.2}{0.0004 + 3.2} \times 10^6
$$

$$
R = \frac{-3.1996}{3.2004}
$$

$$
R \approx -0.99975
$$

*Explanation:* The negative sign indicates a phase inversion. The magnitude $\vert R \vert \approx 1$ means nearly all the ultrasonic energy is reflected back to the transducer — explaining why UT is so effective at detecting delaminations in FRP hulls.

### 3.5 Derivation: Void Content by Density Method (ASTM D2734)

**Given:** Theoretical density of a unidirectional E-glass/epoxy laminate with $V_f = 0.55$: $\rho_{th} = 1.98$ g/cm³. Measured (experimental) density from Archimedes' test: $\rho_{exp} = 1.93$ g/cm³.

**Step 1 — Void Volume Fraction:**

$$
V_v = \frac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{exp}}{\rho_{th}}
$$

$$
V_v = \frac{1.98 - 1.93}{1.98}
$$

$$
V_v = \frac{0.05}{1.98}
$$

$$
V_v \approx 0.0253 = 2.53\%
$$

*Explanation:* Voids lower the bulk density because they replace denser matrix/fibre material with air. The void fraction is inferred from the deficit between theoretical and measured densities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Verdict:** A hull laminate with $V_v > 5\%$ is generally **rejected** under DNV/IRS rules. The exam will often ask: *"How would you verify the quality of an FRP hull panel after demoulding?"* — your answer should reference **Barcol hardness, UT, and density/void content** in that order.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Test

| Stage of Boat Life | Recommended DT Tests | Recommended NDT Tests |
|---|---|---|
| Material qualification | Tensile, Compression, ILSS, DMA, DSC | UT calibration, RT reference panels |
| During production (lamination) | Barcol, Barcol-by-cure | Tap test, IRT (in-mould cure monitoring) |
| Post-demould QC | Cured-laminate Barcol, $T_g$ by DSC, Density | Full-panel **C-scan UT**, VT, Tap test |
| Post-assembly (whole hull) | Sample-coupon cut-out DT (sacrificial panels) | Full-hull C-scan, IRT, AE baseline |
| In-service (every 2–5 years) | — | Periodic UT, IRT, AE, Visual survey |
| Post-damage (e.g., grounding) | — | Detailed UT, RT, IRT before repair sign-off |
| Post-repair verification | Local DT on repair patch | UT, IRT, Barcol of patched zone |

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Composite Hull Quality Assurance Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[RAW MATERIAL INSPECTION] --> P1[RESIN CHECK VISCOSITY]
    IN1 --> P2[FIBRE CHECK AREAL WEIGHT]
    P1 --> M1[LAY UP INFUSION]
    P2 --> M1
    M1 --> M2[IN MOULD CURE]
    M2 --> M3[DEMOULD]
    M3 --> Q1[BARCOL HARDNESS]
    Q1 --> Q2[SAMPLE COUPON DT]
    Q1 --> Q3[FULL PANEL C SCAN UT]
    Q2 --> R1[CALCULATION OF ALLOWABLES]
    Q3 --> R2[DEFECT MAP OF HULL]
    R1 --> DEC{QUALITY OK?}
    R2 --> DEC
    DEC -->|YES| ACC[HULL ACCEPTED]
    DEC -->|NO| REP[REPAIR OR REJECT]
    REP --> Q3
    ACC --> SHP[INSTALLATION IN BOAT]
    SHP --> SVC[IN SERVICE NDT PERIODIC]
    SVC --> SVC2[ULTRASONIC IRT AE SURVEY]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight from the Workflow:**
> Notice the *closed loop* between **REPAIR** and **C-SCAN UT**. Every repair must be re-verified non-destructively before the hull is released. This is mandatory under IRS and DNV rules for FRP vessels.

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Ultrasonic C-Scan Setup Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    TG[FUNCTION GENERATOR] --> TX[TRANSMITTER]
    TX --> PT[PIEZO TRANSDUCER]
    PT -->|ULTRASONIC BEAM| SPEC[COMPOSITE SPECIMEN UNDER WATER COUPLANT]
    SPEC -->|REFLECTED ECHO| PT2[RECEIVING TRANSDUCER]
    PT2 --> AMP[AMPLIFIER]
    AMP --> GATE[TIME GATE GATING CIRCUIT]
    GATE --> DAC[DAC CONVERTER]
    DAC --> SCAN[X Y SCANNER MECHANISM]
    SCAN --> MON[DISPLAY C SCAN IMAGE]
    SPEC --> SCAN
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The transducer sends a pulse through water (the couplant) into the composite. The reflected echo from any defect (delamination, void) is time-gated and digitized. An X-Y scanner raster-moves the transducer (or the specimen) to build a 2-D image, where colour intensity maps defect amplitude.

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Decision Tree for Selecting an NDT Method on a Boat Hull

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[DEFECT SUSPECTED IN HULL] --> Q1{KIND OF DEFECT}
    Q1 -->|SURFACE CRACK| R1[VISUAL TESTING PLUS DYE PENETRANT]
    Q1 -->|SUBSURFACE DELAMINATION| R2[ULTRASONIC C SCAN]
    Q1 -->|INTERNAL POROSITY VOIDS| R3[RADIOGRAPHIC X RAY]
    Q1 -->|WATER INGRESS HONEYCOMB| R4[INFRARED THERMOGRAPHY]
    Q1 -->|REAL TIME DAMAGE GROWTH| R5[ACOUSTIC EMISSION]
    R1 --> DONE[INSPECTION REPORT]
    R2 --> DONE
    R3 --> DONE
    R4 --> DONE
    R5 --> DONE
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this decision tree matters in a KTU answer:** Examiners often award full marks only when the student demonstrates *method-selection logic* — i.e., matching the *defect type* to the *physical principle* of the NDT method. Memorize this mapping.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
*Distinguish between Destructive Testing (DT) and Non-Destructive Testing (NDT). State one ASTM standard test method for each, as applied to fibre-reinforced composites used in boat building.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — examiner-style):**

- **Destructive Testing (DT):** A test method in which the specimen is loaded to failure or chemically decomposed to obtain its mechanical/physical properties. The component *cannot* be used after the test.
  *Example ASTM standard:* **ASTM D3039** — Tensile Properties of Polymer Matrix Composite Materials. *(1 mark)*

- **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):** A test method in which the component is inspected *in situ* without impairing its serviceability; defects such as voids, delaminations, cracks, and water ingress are detected.
  *Example ASTM standard:* **ASTM E2580** — Standard Practice for Ultrasonic Testing of Flat Panel Composites (C-scan method). *(1 mark)*

- **One key contrast:** DT yields *quantitative design allowables* (strength, modulus, $T_g$); NDT yields *qualitative defect maps* and *integrity assessment* (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only the *definitions* and forget to **name the ASTM standard**. A 3-mark question with 3 parts often deducts 1 mark for the missing standard. Always quote a standard number.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*What is the Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)? Why is it critically important in the design of composite boats operating in tropical waters?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** $T_g$ is the temperature region at which the polymer matrix of a composite transitions from a hard, glassy state to a soft, rubbery state. It is a key thermal-property indicator measured by **DSC (Differential Scanning Calorimetry)** or **DMA (Dynamic Mechanical Analysis)**.

- **Tropical-water significance (2 marks):**
  (i) The hull skin in tropical backwaters/harbours experiences sustained temperatures of **30–45 °C**, with surface peaks of **60–70 °C** under direct sun.
  (ii) If the service temperature approaches $T_g$, the matrix *softens*, the laminate's modulus $E$ drops sharply (often by an order of magnitude), and the hull loses its structural stiffness → excessive deflection, hull-slamming damage, and possible delamination.
  (iii) Therefore, KTU/IRS rules mandate that $T_g$ of the cured laminate be **at least 20–25 °C above** the maximum expected service temperature.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Destructive Testing Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question A, Module 2, PENST742]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Short-Beam Shear (SBS) Test** (ASTM D2344) for determining the Interlaminar Shear Strength (ILSS) of a fibre-reinforced composite laminate. List the standard specimen geometry requirements. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An SBS test on a unidirectional E-glass/epoxy coupon (width $b = 6$ mm, thickness $h = 3$ mm) yields a peak load $P_m = 540$ N. Calculate the ILSS. If the same panel is found to have a void volume fraction of $V_v = 4\%$, comment on the suitability of this panel for a boat hull. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Principle (2 marks):** The SBS test is a three-point bend test with a *short* span such that interlaminar shear failure (delamination) occurs before flexural failure. ASTM D2344 specifies a span-to-thickness ratio of $L/h = 4$ (or 5). The short span forces the neutral-axis shear stress to reach the interlaminar shear strength before the outer-fibre bending stress reaches the flexural strength.

2. **Test Setup (2 marks):**
   - A rectangular coupon is supported on two cylindrical supports.
   - A central loading nose applies a monotonically increasing load at a constant crosshead rate (typically **1 mm/min**).
   - Load vs. deflection is recorded.
   - Specimen dimensions (typical): length ≈ $6h$, width $b \approx 2h$ to $3h$, thickness $h$ as built.
   - $L/h = 4$ → for $h = 3$ mm, $L = 12$ mm.

3. **Failure Mode (1 mark):** The desired failure is a *single* or *multiple* interlaminar shear crack visible on the specimen's edge — not a flexural tensile break or compression kink.

4. **Calculation Formula (1 mark):**

$$
F^{sbs} = \frac{3 P_m}{4 b h}
$$

5. **Typical Values (1 mark):** A well-consolidated marine-grade E-glass/epoxy gives ILSS ≈ **30–60 MPa**. Values below **25 MPa** indicate poor consolidation, high void content, or inadequate cure.

---

**Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — ILSS Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
F^{sbs} = \frac{3 P_m}{4 b h} = \frac{3 \times 540}{4 \times 6 \times 3} = \frac{1620}{72} = 22.5 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Comment on the Result (2 marks):**
An ILSS of 22.5 MPa is **below the typical 30–60 MPa marine-grade acceptance range**. The low value correlates directly with the high void content (4%).

**Step 3 — Suitability Verdict (2 marks):**
For a **boat hull**, this panel **should be rejected** under standard DNV/IRS rules, which require void content typically **$V_v < 2\%$** and ILSS in the marine-grade range. The panel is at high risk of **delamination under hull-slamming loads** and is not fit for marine service until re-consolidated or replaced.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part A):** Students often compute the formula but forget to (i) state the formula, (ii) include units, and (iii) comment on the result. A 14-mark question typically allocates ~3 marks for the *engineering interpretation*, not just the arithmetic. Always close with a verdict on the part's fitness for service.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Non-Destructive Testing Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question B, Module 2, PENST742]**

**(a)** Explain the principle of **Ultrasonic C-scan testing** for FRP boat hull panels. Describe the role of the couplant, the piezoelectric transducer, and the time-gate. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 6 mm thick FRP hull panel is inspected by a 5 MHz longitudinal ultrasonic wave in water couplant. The transducer records an echo from a suspected delamination at a two-way time-of-flight of $\Delta t = 1.6$ μs. If the ultrasonic velocity in the laminate is $c = 2.9$ km/s, calculate the depth of the delamination. Comment on whether the defect is repairable by resin injection. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Principle (2 marks):** Ultrasonic C-scan uses a focused piezoelectric transducer that emits short pulses of high-frequency (typically 1–25 MHz) longitudinal sound waves into the composite through a liquid couplant (water or gel). The waves reflect from acoustic-impedance discontinuities — such as delaminations (air-filled gaps), voids, inclusions, or back-surface echoes. The pulse-echo time and amplitude are recorded, and an X-Y scanner raster-moves the transducer across the panel to build a 2-D plan-view image of defect locations and severities.

2. **Role of the Couplant (2 marks):** Air has very low acoustic impedance; a bare transducer-to-specimen air gap would reflect ~99.99% of the energy. A liquid couplant (water, gel) replaces the air gap, providing an efficient transmission path because its impedance matches the transducer and wets into surface roughness. Water immersion is the standard marine-yard approach.

3. **Piezoelectric Transducer (1.5 marks):** A piezo-electric crystal (e.g., PZT) converts an electrical pulse into a mechanical (ultrasonic) wave on transmission, and converts the returning mechanical echo back into an electrical signal on reception. Its central frequency (e.g., 5 MHz) sets the wavelength and therefore the *minimum detectable flaw size* (typically $\lambda/2 \approx 0.3$ mm for 5 MHz in E-glass/epoxy).

4. **Time-Gate (1.5 marks):** The time-gate is an electronic window that selects echoes arriving within a specific time interval. By gating on the depth range corresponding to the laminate thickness, the system isolates defect echoes from front-surface and back-surface echoes, ensuring the C-scan image displays only the in-laminate defects.

---

**Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Two-way travel distance (2 marks):**

$$
2d = c \cdot \Delta t
$$

$$
2d = 2.9 \times 10^3 \, \text{m/s} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{s}
$$

$$
2d = 4.64 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m} = 4.64 \, \text{mm}
$$

*Explanation:* The pulse travels *down* to the defect and *back* up, so the total distance is $2d$.

**Step 2 — Depth of delamination (2 marks):**

$$
d = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2} = \frac{4.64}{2} = 2.32 \, \text{mm}
$$

*Explanation:* Dividing by 2 gives the one-way depth from the entry surface.

**Step 3 — Interpretation (1.5 marks):**
The delamination is at 2.32 mm depth in a 6 mm thick panel — about **39% of the depth** from the surface. This is well within the range accessible to vacuum-assisted resin-injection repair.

**Step 4 — Repairability Verdict (1.5 marks):**
The defect **is repairable by resin injection** provided:
- The delaminated area is small and accessible.
- The panel is dry (no water ingress — confirmed by IRT).
- The surrounding laminate is sound (confirmed by full C-scan).
- Standard resin-injection repair (e.g., per IRS guidelines) is performed and re-verified by post-repair C-scan.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** Students often write "$d = c \cdot t$" without dividing by 2 — this is the #1 source of a 2-mark deduction. Always remember: *pulse-echo* time-of-flight is **round-trip**. Always write $\Delta t = 2d/c$ or $d = c\Delta t/2$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Destructive and Non-Destructive Testing: Final 20-Point Recap**
>
> 1. **DT** = specimen is destroyed; **NDT** = specimen remains serviceable.
> 2. **ASTM D3039** → Tensile; **ASTM D7264** → Flexural; **ASTM D2344** → ILSS.
> 3. **ILSS formula:** $F^{sbs} = 3P_m / (4bh)$. Marine-grade target: **30–60 MPa**.
> 4. **Flexural stress (3-pt):** $\sigma_f = 3PL/(2bh^2)$.
> 5. **Flexural modulus:** $E_f = L^3 m / (4bh^3)$.
> 6. **Barcol hardness** is a quick *cure indicator* — values <25 indicate under-cure.
> 7. **$T_g$** is the most important thermal property — it must be ≥ 20–25 °C above the maximum service temperature.
> 8. **DMA** gives $E'$, $E''$, and $\tan\delta$; peak of $\tan\delta$ ≈ $T_g$.
> 9. **Void content** must be **< 2%** for boat-grade marine laminates.
> 10. **Ultrasonic C-scan** is the industry workhorse for hull-panel inspection.
> 11. **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho c$. A delamination (air, $Z \approx 0$) gives $\vert R \vert \approx 1$ — strong echo.
> 12. **Time-of-flight:** $d = c \Delta t / 2$ (always divide by 2 in pulse-echo).
> 13. **Beer-Lambert law** governs radiographic testing: $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$.
> 14. **Acoustic Emission** is a *passive* monitor; **Felicity Ratio < 1** indicates significant prior damage.
> 15. **Infrared Thermography** detects defects via their effect on heat conduction — fast, full-field, no couplant.
> 16. **Tap / Coin Test** is the cheapest, fastest field-NDT — used by shipyard supervisors daily.
> 17. **A-scan** = 1-D time-amplitude; **B-scan** = cross-section; **C-scan** = plan view.
> 18. **Void content formula:** $V_v = (\rho_{th} - \rho_{exp})/\rho_{th}$.
> 19. **NDT method selection logic:** match the *defect type* to the *physical principle* — surface crack → VT/DPT; delamination → UT; porosity → RT; water ingress → IRT; active damage → AE.
> 20. **Mandatory under classification rules (DNV/IRS/LR):** both DT (on coupons) and NDT (on full hull) are required; a *closed-loop* verification is required after every repair.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Micromechanical Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Micromechanical Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Micromechanical Analysis (KTU 2024 Definition):** *Micromechanics* of composites is the analytical study of the **discrete mechanical and physical behaviour** of the constituent phases (fiber, matrix, and the fiber–matrix interface) and their **synergistic interaction** at the micro-scale, used to mathematically predict the **effective macroscopic (bulk) properties** of a composite lamina from the known properties of its individual constituents.

In simpler terms — it is the science of answering one question: *"If I know the strength, stiffness, and density of the fiber and the resin separately, can I mathematically predict how the combined material (the composite) will behave when loaded?"*

The governing assumptions adopted in classical micromechanical models are:
1. The fiber is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic**.
2. The matrix is **homogeneous and isotropic**.
3. The fiber–matrix interface is **perfectly bonded** (no slip or debonding).
4. Loads are applied along **principal material directions** (0° and 90°).
5. The composite is **initially void-free** (porosity is treated as a separate correction).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The "Concrete & Steel Rebar" model:**
> Think of a fiber-reinforced composite exactly like **reinforced concrete (RCC)**:
> - The **concrete (matrix)** is weak in tension but good in compression — just like a polymer resin (epoxy/polyester).
> - The **steel rebar (fiber)** is very strong in tension along its length — just like carbon/glass fiber.
> - When you pull on RCC, the concrete alone would crack; but because the steel rebar carries the load, the combined material becomes extremely strong.
> - **Micromechanics is the calculator that tells you exactly how strong the combined RCC will be**, given the diameter of the rebar, the strength of concrete, and how much rebar is embedded inside.

For a **boat hull**, the fibers (glass/carbon) are the "skeleton," and the resin is the "muscle" that holds everything together and transfers load into the skeleton. Micromechanics helps naval architects **optimize fiber content** so the hull is light, stiff, and seaworthy.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the **universally accepted constants and notations** used throughout KTU examinations:

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| $V_f$ | Fiber volume fraction | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| $V_m$ | Matrix volume fraction | dimensionless |
| $V_v$ | Void volume fraction | dimensionless |
| $W_f$ | Fiber weight fraction | dimensionless |
| $W_m$ | Matrix weight fraction | dimensionless |
| $\rho_c$ | Composite density | $\text{g/cm}^3$ or $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| $E_f, E_m, E_c$ | Young's modulus of fiber, matrix, composite | **GPa** |
| $\sigma_f, \sigma_m$ | Strength of fiber and matrix | **MPa** |
| $d_f$ | Fiber diameter | **µm** (micrometers) |
| $l_c$ | Critical fiber length | **mm** |

---

## 1.4 Scope of Micromechanical Analysis in Boat Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the context of **PENST742 (Composite Boat Design)**, micromechanical analysis is the **first computational step** before any structural design of a hull, deck, or bulkhead. It is used to:
> 1. Select the **optimal fiber/resin combination** for a given service load.
> 2. Estimate the **weight savings** over a traditional marine-grade aluminum or steel hull.
> 3. Predict **failure initiation stresses** for the 0° and 90° plies.
> 4. Calculate the **critical fiber length**, which dictates the minimum chopped-strand length needed in a hand-laid hull.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Three Building Blocks

Micromechanical analysis rests on **three pillars**:

### Pillar 1: The Rule of Mixtures (ROM)
The classical Voigt–Reuss approach assumes that a composite property is a **linear weighted average** of the constituent properties, weighted by their respective volume fractions. This works remarkably well for **longitudinal loading** (load parallel to fibers).

### Pillar 2: The Mechanics of Materials Approach
Used when the **stress/strain distribution** between phases is not uniform. For example, in **transverse loading** (load perpendicular to fibers), a series–parallel spring analogy is used to derive the transverse modulus.

### Pillar 3: The Semi-Empirical Approach (Halpin–Tsai)
Since exact closed-form solutions are mathematically intractable for transverse loading, the **Halpin–Tsai equation** is used as an empirical interpolation between the Voigt (upper bound) and Reuss (lower bound) solutions. It is the **industry standard** for KTU and real-world naval/marine design.

---

## 2.2 Why Each Step Matters (The "Why & How")

| Step | Why It Matters | How It Is Computed |
|------|---------------|--------------------|
| Calculate $V_f, V_m$ | Determines how much load the fiber can carry vs. the matrix | From constituent densities and weights |
| Calculate $\rho_c$ | Boat designers need **lightweight** hulls to improve fuel efficiency | Rule of mixtures on densities |
| Calculate $E_{1L}$ (longitudinal modulus) | Primary stiffness direction along fibers | ROM: $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ |
| Calculate $E_{2T}$ (transverse modulus) | Stiffness perpendicular to fibers | Halpin–Tsai with shape factor $\xi = 2$ |
| Calculate $X_t$ (longitudinal strength) | Maximum load along fibers | ROM modified for strain compatibility |
| Calculate $Y_t$ (transverse strength) | Weakest direction in a lamina | Semi-empirical formulas (e.g., Puck) |
| Critical fiber length $l_c$ | Ensures fibers carry full load before matrix cracks | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d_f}{2 \tau_i}$ |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table contains EVERY formula a KTU student must memorize for Micromechanical Analysis.**

| # | Property | Formula | Valid For |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | Volume fraction constraint | $V_f + V_m + V_v = 1$ | All composites |
| 2 | Weight–volume relation | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_c}$ | All composites |
| 3 | Composite density (ROM) | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Void-free composites |
| 4 | Composite density (with voids) | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m - \rho_v V_v$ | Real laminates |
| 5 | Longitudinal Young's Modulus $E_1$ | $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Rule of mixtures (Voigt) |
| 6 | Transverse Young's Modulus $E_2$ | $\dfrac{1}{E_2} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Reuss (inverse rule) |
| 7 | Major Poisson's ratio $\nu_{12}$ | $\nu_{12} = \nu_f V_f + \nu_m V_m$ | ROM |
| 8 | Minor Poisson's ratio $\nu_{21}$ | $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12} \cdot \dfrac{E_2}{E_1}$ | Reciprocal relation |
| 9 | Shear modulus $G_{12}$ | $G_{12} = G_f V_f + G_m V_m$ | ROM (approximate) |
| 10 | Halpin–Tsai equation | $\dfrac{E_2}{E_m} = \dfrac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$, where $\eta = \dfrac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + \xi}$ | Transverse modulus (industry standard) |
| 11 | Halpin–Tsai shape factor | $\xi = 2 \dfrac{l_f}{d_f}$ (circular fibers) | General |
| 12 | Longitudinal tensile strength $X_t$ | $X_t = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m^\prime (1 - V_f)$ | Strain compatibility |
| 13 | Transverse tensile strength $Y_t$ | $Y_t = \dfrac{\sigma_{mu}}{K}$ where $K$ is a stress concentration factor | Matrix-dominated |
| 14 | Critical fiber length | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d_f}{2 \tau_i}$ | Fiber pull-out criterion |
| 15 | Fiber efficiency factor | $\eta_l = 1 - \dfrac{l_c}{2 l_f}$ (for $l_f > l_c$) | Strength correction |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where these formulas are applied in Marine / Boat Engineering:**
> - **Hull plating**: To minimize weight while maintaining stiffness, $V_f$ is typically optimized between **0.45 and 0.60** for woven roving in polyester hulls.
> - **Mast and keel reinforcement**: Unidirectional carbon prepreg at $V_f = 0.65$ yields $E_1 > 180$ GPa.
> - **Sandwich core**: Micromechanics predicts the through-thickness modulus and strength of balsa/PVC cores.
> - **Inspection criteria**: Void volume fraction $V_v > 0.02$ in a marine laminate is grounds for **rejection under classification society rules** (DNV, Lloyd's Register).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volume Fraction from Weight Fraction (Algebraic Foundation)

**Statement:** Given the weight fractions $W_f$ and $W_m$ of the fiber and matrix, derive the fiber volume fraction $V_f$.

**Starting Equation (definition of weight fraction):**

$$
W_f = \frac{m_f}{m_c} = \frac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}
$$

**Step 1:** Rearrange the equation to isolate the volume terms.

$$
W_f (\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m) = \rho_f V_f
$$

**Step 2:** Expand the left-hand side.

$$
W_f \rho_f V_f + W_f \rho_m V_m = \rho_f V_f
$$

**Step 3:** Group $V_f$ terms on one side.

$$
\rho_f V_f - W_f \rho_f V_f = W_f \rho_m V_m
$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $V_f$.

$$
\rho_f V_f (1 - W_f) = W_f \rho_m V_m
$$

**Step 5:** Use the constraint $V_m = 1 - V_f$ for a void-free composite.

$$
\rho_f V_f (1 - W_f) = W_f \rho_m (1 - V_f)
$$

**Step 6:** Expand and solve for $V_f$.

$$
\rho_f V_f - \rho_f V_f W_f = W_f \rho_m - W_f \rho_m V_f
$$

$$
V_f (\rho_f - \rho_f W_f + W_f \rho_m) = W_f \rho_m
$$

$$
V_f = \frac{W_f \rho_m}{\rho_f - W_f \rho_f + W_f \rho_m} = \frac{W_f \rho_m}{\rho_f (1 - W_f) + W_f \rho_m}
$$

**Final Boxed Result (KTU standard form):**

$$
\boxed{V_f = \frac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Longitudinal Modulus $E_1$ by Iso-Strain (Voigt) Assumption

**Statement:** Derive $E_1 = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ from first principles.

**Assumption (Iso-Strain):** When load is applied parallel to continuous, aligned fibers, both the fiber and matrix experience the **same strain**.

$$
\epsilon_c = \epsilon_f = \epsilon_m = \epsilon
$$

**Step 1:** Apply Hooke's law to each phase.

$$
\sigma_f = E_f \epsilon, \quad \sigma_m = E_m \epsilon, \quad \sigma_c = E_c \epsilon
$$

**Step 2:** Use force equilibrium across any cross-section of area $A_c$.

$$
\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m
$$

**Step 3:** Divide throughout by $A_c$.

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f \left(\frac{A_f}{A_c}\right) + \sigma_m \left(\frac{A_m}{A_c}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the area ratios as volume fractions (for fibers of uniform cross-section).

$$
\frac{A_f}{A_c} = V_f, \quad \frac{A_m}{A_c} = V_m
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the Hooke's law stresses.

$$
E_c \epsilon = E_f \epsilon \cdot V_f + E_m \epsilon \cdot V_m
$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $\epsilon$ (non-zero strain) from both sides.

$$
\boxed{E_1 = E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transverse Modulus $E_2$ by Iso-Stress (Reuss) Assumption

**Statement:** Derive $E_2$ when load is applied **perpendicular** to the fibers.

**Assumption (Iso-Stress):** The stress in the fiber and matrix phases is the same and equal to the composite stress.

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma
$$

**Step 1:** The total elongation is the sum of elongations in each phase (series model).

$$
\Delta_c = \Delta_f + \Delta_m
$$

**Step 2:** Express each elongation in terms of compliance (inverse of modulus).

$$
\epsilon_c L_c = \epsilon_f L_f + \epsilon_m L_m
$$

**Step 3:** Divide by total length $L_c$ and use the iso-stress assumption.

$$
\frac{\sigma}{E_c} = \frac{\sigma}{E_f} \cdot V_f + \frac{\sigma}{E_m} \cdot V_m
$$

**Step 4:** Cancel $\sigma$.

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{V_f}{E_f} + \frac{V_m}{E_m}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Critical Fiber Length $l_c$

**Statement:** A short fiber embedded in a matrix transfers load through **interfacial shear stress** $\tau_i$. Find the minimum length at which the fiber can carry its full tensile load before the matrix cracks.

**Step 1:** Consider force equilibrium on a fiber of length $l_f$ and diameter $d_f$.

The tensile load in the fiber at its mid-point is balanced by the shear load transferred from the matrix over its surface (from $x=0$ to $x=l_f/2$).

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi d_f \cdot \frac{l_f}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the required length to develop full fiber stress.

$$
l_f = \frac{\sigma_f d_f}{2 \tau_i}
$$

**Step 3:** Define this as the **critical length** $l_c$.

$$
\boxed{l_c = \frac{\sigma_f d_f}{2 \tau_i}}
$$

**Step 4 (Engineering interpretation):**
- If $l_f < l_c$, the fiber **pulls out** before reaching its full strength (inefficient).
- If $l_f \gg l_c$, the fiber fractures first (efficient).
- Typical rule: for marine-grade E-glass ($d_f = 10$ µm, $\sigma_f = 2.4$ GPa, $\tau_i = 30$ MPa):

$$
l_c = \frac{2400 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 30} = 0.4 \, \text{mm}
$$

Hence, marine laminates typically use fibers of **length $\geq 25$ mm** (chopped strand mat) or **continuous fibers**, both far exceeding $l_c$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A unidirectional glass-fiber/epoxy composite has $V_f = 0.55$. Given:
$E_f = 72$ GPa, $E_m = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_f = 2.55$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.20$ g/cm³, $\sigma_f = 2400$ MPa, $\tau_i = 40$ MPa, $d_f = 14$ µm.

**Compute (a) $E_1$, (b) $\rho_c$, (c) $E_2$ by Reuss, (d) $E_2$ by Halpin–Tsai ($\xi = 2$).**

**Solution:**

**(a) Longitudinal modulus:**
$$
E_1 = (72)(0.55) + (3.5)(0.45) = 39.6 + 1.575 = 41.175 \text{ GPa}
$$

**(b) Composite density:**
$$
\rho_c = (2.55)(0.55) + (1.20)(0.45) = 1.4025 + 0.54 = 1.9425 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**(c) Transverse modulus (Reuss):**
$$
\frac{1}{E_2} = \frac{0.55}{72} + \frac{0.45}{3.5} = 0.00764 + 0.12857 = 0.13621
$$
$$
E_2 = 7.342 \text{ GPa}
$$

**(d) Transverse modulus (Halpin–Tsai, $\xi = 2$):**

Compute $\eta$:
$$
\eta = \frac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + \xi} = \frac{72/3.5 - 1}{72/3.5 + 2} = \frac{19.57}{22.57} = 0.8672
$$

Compute $E_2 / E_m$:
$$
\frac{E_2}{E_m} = \frac{1 + 2(0.8672)(0.55)}{1 - (0.8672)(0.55)} = \frac{1 + 0.9539}{1 - 0.4770} = \frac{1.9539}{0.5230} = 3.7361
$$

$$
E_2 = 3.7361 \times 3.5 = 13.076 \text{ GPa}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observations from the numerical example:**
> - $E_1 \gg E_2$ — this **anisotropy** is the defining feature of unidirectional composites.
> - Halpin–Tsai gives a **higher, more realistic** $E_2$ than the simple Reuss bound (which is a lower bound and under-predicts real values by ~44% in this case).
> - Composite density is **24% lower than pure aluminum (2.7 g/cm³)** — the key reason composite boats are faster and more fuel-efficient.
