## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *State and briefly justify any **four** basic assumptions of Classical Laminate Theory as applied to a GRP boat hull.*

**Model Answer (≈ 110 words):**

1. **Thin laminate assumption** — Hull plating satisfies $L/h \gg 100$, so transverse shear deformation is negligible; valid for hand-laid GRP panels of 3–25 mm thickness.
2. **Kirchhoff–Love straight-normal hypothesis** — Normals to the mid-surface remain straight and normal after deformation; reduces 3-D problem to 2-D.
3. **Plane stress** — $\sigma_z = 0$ because plies are thin; gives the reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$.
4. **Perfect inter-ply bonding** — No slip between adjacent plies; ensures continuity of $u, v, w$ at every interface.
5. **Linearly elastic, specially orthotropic ply** — Each unidirectional ply is homogeneous and has four independent elastic constants $(E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12})$.
6. **Small deformations** — Slopes $\partial w/\partial x, \partial w/\partial y \ll 1$, allowing linearisation of the strain tensor.

*(Any four with one-line justification earn full 3 marks.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Explain the physical significance of the assumption $\sigma_z = 0$ in the analysis of a composite boat hull.*

**Model Answer (≈ 95 words):**

The assumption $\sigma_z = 0$ (plane stress) is valid because the **ply thickness is much smaller than the in-plane hull dimensions** and the **hull is loaded primarily in its own plane** by hydrostatic pressure and bending. As a result, the stress component perpendicular to the laminate surface is negligibly small compared to the in-plane stresses $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}$. Mathematically, this reduces the 3-D orthotropic Hooke's law (with 9 independent elastic constants) to the 2-D reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$ containing only 4 constants. This simplification is the **starting point of the entire CLT framework** used in ISO 12215 scantling calculations.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** State the **basic assumptions of Classical Laminate Theory** as applied to composite boat hulls. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Starting from the Kirchhoff–Love hypothesis, **derive the linear variation of in-plane strain through the laminate thickness** and obtain the $[A], [B], [D]$ stiffness matrices. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Statement of assumptions (7 marks):**

List the eleven assumptions from Section 2.1, grouped under the three headings. Award marks as:
- Geometric/kinematic assumptions: 3 marks (state at least 3)
- Material/constitutive assumptions: 2 marks (state at least 2)
- Load and boundary assumptions: 2 marks (state at least 2)

A model answer should include: (i) thin laminate; (ii) Kirchhoff–Love straight-normal; (iii) $\sigma_z = 0$ plane stress; (iv) perfect inter-ply bonding; (v) homogeneous, linearly elastic, specially orthotropic ply; (vi) small deformations; (vii) no inter-laminar slip; (viii) quasi-static hydrostatic load; (ix) simply supported boundary; (x) first-ply-failure criterion; (xi) no residual stress or known a-priori.

**(b) — Derivation of linear strain and $[A], [B], [D]$ (7 marks):**

1. **Mid-plane displacements** $u_0, v_0, w_0$ — 1 mark
2. **Extension to thickness** $u = u_0 - z\,\partial w_0/\partial x$ — 1 mark
3. **Strain computation** $\varepsilon_x = \partial u_0/\partial x - z\,\partial^2 w_0/\partial x^2$ — 2 marks
4. **Identification of mid-plane strain and curvature** — 1 mark
5. **Resultant definitions and integration yielding $[A], [B], [D]$** — 2 marks

(Full derivation is presented in Section 3.1 and 3.3 above; reproduce it step-by-step in the exam.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** With the help of neat sketches, **explain the Kirchhoff–Love straight-normal hypothesis** and state **four assumptions** required for its validity in marine FRP panel analysis. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** For a 4-ply cross-ply GRP laminate $\left[ 0^\circ/90^\circ/90^\circ/0^\circ \right]$ with each ply of thickness $0.25$ mm and $E_1 = 38$ GPa, $E_2 = 8.5$ GPa, $G_{12} = 4.5$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.30$, **compute the extensional stiffness matrix $[A]$** using the basic assumptions of CLT. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Kirchhoff–Love hypothesis (7 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** A material line originally normal to the undeformed mid-surface remains *straight, inextensible, and normal* to the deformed mid-surface.
- **Sketch (2 marks):** Show undeformed plate (horizontal mid-surface with vertical normals) and deformed plate (curved mid-surface with normals still perpendicular to it).
- **Four supporting assumptions (3 marks):**
  1. Thin plate ($L/h \gg 100$)
  2. Small deflections (linear strain)
  3. Plane stress ($\sigma_z = 0$)
  4. Perfect inter-ply bonding (no slip)

**(b) — Computation of $[A]$ for cross-ply laminate (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute the reduced stiffnesses $Q_{ij}$.**

$$
\nu_{21} = \nu_{12}\frac{E_2}{E_1} = 0.30 \times \frac{8.5}{38} = 0.0671
$$

$$
1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 1 - 0.30 \times 0.0671 = 0.9799
$$

$$
Q_{11} = \frac{38 \times 10^9}{0.9799} = 38.78 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
Q_{22} = \frac{8.5 \times 10^9}{0.9799} = 8.67 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
Q_{12} = \frac{0.30 \times 8.5 \times 10^9}{0.9799} = 2.60 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
Q_{66} = G_{12} = 4.5 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the transformed stiffnesses for each ply.**

For the **$0^\circ$ plies**, $[\bar{Q}] = [Q]$ (no transformation needed):

$$
\left[ \bar{Q}^{(0^\circ)} \right] = \begin{bmatrix} 38.78 & 2.60 & 0 \\ 2.60 & 8.67 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4.5 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa}
$$

For the **$90^\circ$ plies**, indices 1 and 2 swap ($Q_{11} \leftrightarrow Q_{22}$), so:

$$
\left[ \bar{Q}^{(90^\circ)} \right] = \begin{bmatrix} 8.67 & 2.60 & 0 \\ 2.60 & 38.78 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4.5 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate to get $[A]$ using each ply of thickness $t = 0.25$ mm.**

$$
[A] = \sum_{k=1}^{4} \left[ \bar{Q}^{(k)} \right] \, t_k = 2 \left[ \bar{Q}^{(0^\circ)} \right] (0.25 \text{ mm}) + 2 \left[ \bar{Q}^{(90^\circ)} \right] (0.25 \text{ mm})
$$

In units of GPa·mm:

$$
A_{11} = 2 \times 0.25 \times 38.78 + 2 \times 0.25 \times 8.67 = 23.73 \text{ GPa·mm}
$$

$$
A_{22} = 2 \times 0.25 \times 8.67 + 2 \times 0.25 \times 38.78 = 23.73 \text{ GPa·mm}
$$

$$
A_{12} = 4 \times 0.25 \times 2.60 = 2.60 \text{ GPa·mm}
$$

$$
A_{66} = 4 \times 0.25 \times 4.5 = 4.50 \text{ GPa·mm}
$$

$$
[A] = \begin{bmatrix} 23.73 & 2.60 & 0 \\ 2.60 & 23.73 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4.50 \end{bmatrix} \text{ GPa·mm}
$$

> *Note the symmetric cross-ply lay-up yields $A_{11} = A_{22}$ — examiners specifically look for this consistency check (1 mark).*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to state $\sigma_z = 0$ as a separate assumption** (often confused with "plane stress in each ply" — both are listed separately for 1 mark each).
> 2. **Mixing up $Q_{ij}$ and $\bar{Q}_{ij}$** — forgetting the rotation transformation for off-axis plies. Cross-ply $\left[ 0/90 \right]$ is the *only* case where $\bar{Q} = Q$ (up to index swap).
> 3. **Sign error in $u = u_0 - z\,\partial w_0/\partial x$** — examiners check this carefully; positive $w_0$ *downward* convention must be explicitly stated.
> 4. **Not justifying why assumption is valid for a boat hull** — KTU 2024 scheme demands *application-context justification*, not just a textbook list. Always add: *"valid because the GRP hull plating is thin ($L/h \gg 100$) and loaded in its own plane."*
> 5. **Forgetting to mention small-deformation linearisation** — even when the question asks for "all" assumptions, students often skip the small-strain postulate, losing 1 mark.
> 6. **Computing $[A]$ in wrong units** — must end in **GPa·mm** (or N/m × mm) — unit consistency is checked.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] The **Basic Assumptions of CLT** are 11 in number, grouped under **Geometric, Material, and Load/Boundary** headings.
- [x] The **Kirchhoff–Love straight-normal hypothesis** is the most powerful single assumption — it gives the linear strain variation $\varepsilon = \varepsilon^0 + z\kappa$.
- [x] **Plane stress** ($\sigma_z = 0$) reduces 3-D Hooke's law to the **reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$** with 4 independent entries.
- [x] The **transformed reduced stiffness** $\left[ \bar{Q} \right]$ is obtained by rotation transformation using the angle $\theta$ of each ply relative to the laminate $x$-axis.
- [x] **Resultant forces** $[N] = [A][\varepsilon^0] + [B][\kappa]$ and **moments** $[M] = [B][\varepsilon^0] + [D][\kappa]$.
- [x] **$[A]$** = extensional stiffness, **$[B]$** = coupling stiffness, **$[D]$** = bending stiffness.
- [x] For a **symmetric laminate**, $[B] = 0$, decoupling bending from stretching — highly desirable in marine panels.
- [x] For a **cross-ply laminate** $\left[ 0/90 \right]_s$, $A_{11} = A_{22}$ (a useful check).
- [x] Typical marine FRP values: $E_1 = 38$ GPa, $E_2 = 8.5$ GPa, $G_{12} = 4.5$ GPa, $\nu_{12} = 0.30$.
- [x] These assumptions are the **theoretical foundation of ISO 12215-5, DNV GL, and Lloyd's Register scantling rules for FRP boats**.
- [x] After CLT comes **ply-stress recovery** → **failure criteria** (Tsai–Wu, Hashin) — which is the next logical step in Module 3 design.
- [x] Always state **why** an assumption is valid for a boat hull, not just list it — KTU 2024 examiners reward application context.
- [x] Remember the **sign convention**: positive $w$ downward, $u = u_0 - z\,\partial w_0/\partial x$, $v = v_0 - z\,\partial w_0/\partial y$.
- [x] In a 14-mark derivation question, examiners award **1 mark for the displacement field**, **2 marks for strain derivation**, **1 mark for curvature identification**, and **2 marks for $[A], [B], [D]$ integration** — the remaining marks go to *justification* and *neat presentation*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress-Displacement and Stress-Strain Relationship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stress-Displacement and Stress-Strain Relationship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Generalized Anisotropic Hooke's Law (The Foundational Definition)

In **Composite Boat Design**, the hull skin, deck, and internal stiffeners are almost always made of fibre-reinforced polymer (FRP) laminates (e.g., glass/epoxy, carbon/vinylester). Unlike steel or aluminium, a single FRP ply is **strongly anisotropic** — its stiffness along the fibre direction is 5 to 20 times larger than across it. Therefore, the classical isotropic relation $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ is *insufficient*.

The most general linear elastic constitutive law for a 3-D anisotropic body is given by the **Generalized Hooke's Law**:

$$
\sigma_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{3} \sum_{l=1}^{3} C_{ijkl}\,\varepsilon_{kl}
$$

Equivalently in contracted (Voigt–Mises) notation:

$$
\begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \sigma_3 \\ \tau_{23} \\ \tau_{31} \\ \tau_{12} \end{Bmatrix}_{6\times 1} = [C]_{6\times 6} \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \varepsilon_3 \\ \gamma_{23} \\ \gamma_{31} \\ \gamma_{12} \end{Bmatrix}_{6\times 1}
$$

The inverse form uses the **Compliance Matrix** $[S] = [C]^{-1}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For a marine composite hull (ply thickness $t \approx 0.5\text{–}2\,\text{mm}$), the **plane stress assumption** is valid because the in-plane dimensions are hundreds of times the thickness. This reduces the $6\times 6$ matrix to a $3\times 3$ form, which is the form you must master for Module 3.

### 1.2 Engineering Constants of an Orthotropic Ply

A unidirectional (UD) ply of glass/epoxy or carbon/epoxy is **orthotropic**: it possesses two mutually perpendicular planes of material symmetry (the 1–2 plane, where 1 = fibres, 2 = transverse).

It is characterised by **five independent engineering constants**:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range (Marine Composites) |
|---|---|---|
| $E_1$ | Young's modulus along fibre | $38\text{–}45\,\text{GPa}$ (glass/epoxy), $130\text{–}140\,\text{GPa}$ (carbon/epoxy) |
| $E_2$ | Young's modulus transverse to fibre | $8\text{–}12\,\text{GPa}$ (glass/epoxy), $8\text{–}10\,\text{GPa}$ (carbon/epoxy) |
| $\nu_{12}$ | Major Poisson's ratio | $0.25\text{–}0.30$ |
| $\nu_{21}$ | Minor Poisson's ratio | $0.05\text{–}0.10$ |
| $G_{12}$ | In-plane shear modulus | $3\text{–}5\,\text{GPa}$ (glass/epoxy), $4\text{–}6\,\text{GPa}$ (carbon/epoxy) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry Condition (Reciprocal Relation):** $\dfrac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} = \dfrac{\nu_{21}}{E_2}$ — this is **not** optional; it falls out of the symmetry of the stiffness matrix $[C]$ and will cost you marks if omitted in derivations.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Timber Plank vs. Plywood"

Imagine you are building a kayak.

- An **isotropic** material (like a sheet of aluminium) behaves the same whether you push it along its length, across its width, or at $45^\circ$ — like a perfectly uniform cake.
- A **single timber plank** is *orthotropic*: along the grain (fibre) it is stiff, across the grain it bends easily, and along the depth it is intermediate. Push at $45^\circ$ and the plank twists non-trivially.
- **Marine plywood / FRP laminate** is *anisotropic*: each ply is orthotropic, but when you stack plies at different angles (e.g., $0^\circ/45^\circ/90^\circ/-45^\circ$) the resulting laminate is fully anisotropic — its response depends on the *direction* of the load.

The **stress–strain relationship** is essentially the mathematical fingerprint that lets us predict how much the hull will stretch, compress, or shear when the boat is loaded by wave pressure, hull weight, or impact.

> [!NOTE]
> **Displacement vs. Strain:** Displacement $\vec{u} = (u, v, w)$ tells us *where each point of the hull moves*. Strain $\varepsilon_{ij}$ tells us *how much it deforms locally*. They are linked by the **kinematic (strain–displacement) relation**:
> $$\varepsilon_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Three Layered Constitutive Equations in a Composite Boat

The complete mechanical response of a composite hull is governed by **three coupled sets** of equations:

**Layer 1 — Kinematics (Strain–Displacement):**
$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x},\quad \varepsilon_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial y},\quad \gamma_{xy} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}
$$

**Layer 2 — Constitutive (Stress–Strain) Law:** (the heart of this module)
$$
\begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = [\bar{Q}] \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Layer 3 — Equilibrium:**
$$
\frac{\partial \sigma_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xy}}{\partial y} + X = 0
$$

### 2.2 The Reduced Stiffness Matrix $[Q]$

Under the **plane stress assumption** ($\sigma_z = \tau_{yz} = \tau_{xz} = 0$), the $6\times 6$ Hooke's law collapses. The resulting $3\times 3$ **Reduced Stiffness Matrix** $[Q]$ in the *principal material coordinates* (1, 2) is:

$$
[Q] = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix}
$$

with elements:
$$
Q_{11} = \frac{E_1}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{22} = \frac{E_2}{1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{12} = \frac{\nu_{12}E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}, \quad Q_{66} = G_{12}
$$

### 2.3 The Compliance Matrix $[S]$ (Inverse of $[Q]$)

$$
[S] = [Q]^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{1}{E_1} & -\dfrac{\nu_{21}}{E_2} & 0 \\[2mm] -\dfrac{\nu_{12}}{E_1} & \dfrac{1}{E_2} & 0 \\[2mm] 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1}{G_{12}} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.4 The Off-Axis Problem — Why We Need $[Q]$

In a real boat hull, the fibres of a given ply are **rarely aligned** with the global loading axes (x, y). For a ply whose fibres make an angle $\theta$ with the global $x$-axis, the local stiffness is $[Q]$, but the *engineer* measures stresses in $(x, y)$. We need the **transformed reduced stiffness** $[\bar{Q}]$.

### 2.5 Stress & Strain Transformation Matrices

The rotation matrix $[T]$ (which contains $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$) maps stresses and (tensorial) strains between the two coordinate systems:

$$
[T] = \begin{bmatrix} c^{2} & s^{2} & 2sc \\ s^{2} & c^{2} & -2sc \\ -sc & sc & c^{2}-s^{2} \end{bmatrix}, \quad c = \cos\theta,\; s = \sin\theta
$$

Stress transformation: $\quad \{\sigma\}_{x,y} = [T]^{-1}\,\{\sigma\}_{1,2}$

Strain transformation (tensorial): $\quad \{\varepsilon^{(\text{ten})}\}_{x,y} = [T]^{-1}\,\{\varepsilon^{(\text{ten})}\}_{1,2}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The off-axis stiffness is **not** simply $[T]^{-1}[Q][T]$. The exact relation is:
> $$[\bar{Q}] = [T]^{-1}\,[Q]\,[T]^{-T}$$
> where $[T]^{-T} = ([T]^{-1})^{T}$. The double inverse-transpose is mandatory because strains in the contracted Voigt form include the engineering shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$, which is twice the tensorial shear.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **minimum** set of formulas KTU examiners expect you to reproduce from memory in Part B answers.

| # | Relation | Formula | Units (SI) | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reduced stiffness $Q_{11}$ | $\dfrac{E_1}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$ | $\text{GPa}$ | Constitutive matrix |
| 2 | Reduced stiffness $Q_{22}$ | $\dfrac{E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$ | $\text{GPa}$ | Constitutive matrix |
| 3 | Reduced stiffness $Q_{12}$ | $\dfrac{\nu_{12}E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}}$ | $\text{GPa}$ | Coupling term |
| 4 | Reduced stiffness $Q_{66}$ | $G_{12}$ | $\text{GPa}$ | In-plane shear |
| 5 | Reciprocal Poisson | $\nu_{21} = \nu_{12}\dfrac{E_2}{E_1}$ | dimensionless | Symmetry check |
| 6 | Stress transformation | $\{\sigma\}_{xy} = [T]^{-1}\{\sigma\}_{12}$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Off-axis load |
| 7 | Strain transformation (tensorial) | $\{\varepsilon^{\text{ten}}\}_{xy} = [T]^{-1}\{\varepsilon^{\text{ten}}\}_{12}$ | dimensionless | Off-axis deformation |
| 8 | Off-axis stiffness | $[\bar{Q}] = [T]^{-1}[Q][T]^{-T}$ | $\text{GPa}$ | **The Master Formula** |
| 9 | Strain–Displacement | $\varepsilon_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}(u_{i,j}+u_{j,i})$ | dimensionless | Kinematics |
| 10 | Reduced compliance $S_{11}$ | $1/E_1$ | $1/\text{GPa}$ | Inverse law |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Hull-skin scantling:** Predicts how much a GRP hull stretches under slamming loads.
* **Sail optimisation:** Predicts anisotropic stretch of a carbon-fibre mast.
* **Failure prediction:** Tsai–Wu and Tsai–Hill criteria depend directly on $[\bar{Q}]$.
* **Vibration & acoustic analysis:** Hull resonant frequencies depend on $[A]$, $[B]$, $[D]$ matrices, which are integrals of $[\bar{Q}]$ across ply thickness.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Reduction of the 3-D Stiffness Matrix to the Plane-Stress Reduced Stiffness $[Q]$

**Starting point:** Generalized Hooke's Law for a 3-D linearly elastic, orthotropic body:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= C_{11}\varepsilon_1 + C_{12}\varepsilon_2 + C_{13}\varepsilon_3 \\
\sigma_2 &= C_{12}\varepsilon_1 + C_{22}\varepsilon_2 + C_{23}\varepsilon_3 \\
\sigma_3 &= C_{13}\varepsilon_1 + C_{23}\varepsilon_2 + C_{33}\varepsilon_3 \\
\tau_{23} &= C_{44}\,\gamma_{23} \\
\tau_{31} &= C_{55}\,\gamma_{31} \\
\tau_{12} &= C_{66}\,\gamma_{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Apply the **plane stress assumption** (valid for thin FRP laminates where $t \ll L$):

$$
\sigma_3 = \tau_{23} = \tau_{31} = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Setting $\sigma_3 = 0$ in the third equation:

$$
0 = C_{13}\varepsilon_1 + C_{23}\varepsilon_2 + C_{33}\varepsilon_3
$$

Solve for $\varepsilon_3$:

$$
\varepsilon_3 = -\frac{C_{13}}{C_{33}}\varepsilon_1 - \frac{C_{23}}{C_{33}}\varepsilon_2
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\varepsilon_3$ back into the expressions for $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= C_{11}\varepsilon_1 + C_{12}\varepsilon_2 + C_{13}\!\left(-\frac{C_{13}}{C_{33}}\varepsilon_1 - \frac{C_{23}}{C_{33}}\varepsilon_2\right) \\
\sigma_1 &= \left(C_{11}-\frac{C_{13}^{2}}{C_{33}}\right)\varepsilon_1 + \left(C_{12}-\frac{C_{13}C_{23}}{C_{33}}\right)\varepsilon_2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_2 &= C_{12}\varepsilon_1 + C_{22}\varepsilon_2 + C_{23}\!\left(-\frac{C_{13}}{C_{33}}\varepsilon_1 - \frac{C_{23}}{C_{33}}\varepsilon_2\right) \\
\sigma_2 &= \left(C_{12}-\frac{C_{13}C_{23}}{C_{33}}\right)\varepsilon_1 + \left(C_{22}-\frac{C_{23}^{2}}{C_{33}}\right)\varepsilon_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Recognise the new effective coefficients as the elements of $[Q]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{11} &= C_{11}-\frac{C_{13}^{2}}{C_{33}} = \frac{E_1}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \\
Q_{22} &= C_{22}-\frac{C_{23}^{2}}{C_{33}} = \frac{E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \\
Q_{12} &= C_{12}-\frac{C_{13}C_{23}}{C_{33}} = \frac{\nu_{12}E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}} \\
Q_{66} &= C_{66} = G_{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** The constitutive law in plane stress is now:

$$
\boxed{\begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_1 \\ \varepsilon_2 \\ \gamma_{12} \end{Bmatrix}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Stress Transformation Equations

**Starting point:** Stress tensor transformation. For a 2-D rotation of axes by angle $\theta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \sigma_x \cos^{2}\theta + \sigma_y \sin^{2}\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta \\
\sigma_2 &= \sigma_x \sin^{2}\theta + \sigma_y \cos^{2}\theta - 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta \\
\tau_{12} &= (\sigma_y-\sigma_x)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^{2}\theta-\sin^{2}\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Collect into matrix form. Using $c=\cos\theta$, $s=\sin\theta$:

$$
\begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} c^{2} & s^{2} & 2sc \\ s^{2} & c^{2} & -2sc \\ -sc & sc & c^{2}-s^{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}
$$

Therefore $[T]$ is identified as the matrix above.

**Step 2:** Invert to obtain stresses in the global $(x,y)$ frame:

$$
\begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{Bmatrix} = [T]^{-1} \begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{Bmatrix}
$$

The inverse of $[T]$ can be shown to be:

$$
[T]^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} c^{2} & s^{2} & -2sc \\ s^{2} & c^{2} & 2sc \\ sc & -sc & c^{2}-s^{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the sign change in the $(3,1)$ and $(3,2)$ entries — many students wrongly retain $+sc$ and lose marks.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — The Master Formula $[\bar{Q}] = [T]^{-1}[Q][T]^{-T}$

**Starting point:** In the *material* (1, 2) frame, the stress–strain law is:

$$
\{\sigma\}_{12} = [Q]\{\varepsilon\}_{12}
$$

**Step 1:** Transform stress from $(x,y)$ to $(1,2)$:

$$
\{\sigma\}_{12} = [T]\{\sigma\}_{xy}
$$

**Step 2:** Transform the *tensorial* strain (engineering shear must be halved):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_1 &= \varepsilon_x c^{2}+\varepsilon_y s^{2}+\gamma_{xy} sc \\
\varepsilon_2 &= \varepsilon_x s^{2}+\varepsilon_y c^{2}-\gamma_{xy} sc \\
\frac{\gamma_{12}}{2} &= (\varepsilon_y-\varepsilon_x)sc + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}(c^{2}-s^{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives:

$$
\{\varepsilon^{\text{ten}}\}_{12} = [T]\{\varepsilon^{\text{ten}}\}_{xy}
$$

But the engineering strain vector is $\{\varepsilon\} = \{\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \gamma_{xy}\}^{T}$, so:

$$
\{\varepsilon\}_{12} = [R]\{\varepsilon\}_{xy}, \quad [R] = \begin{bmatrix} c^{2} & s^{2} & sc \\ s^{2} & c^{2} & -sc \\ -2sc & 2sc & c^{2}-s^{2} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3:** Recognise that $[R] = [T]^{-T}$ (transpose of inverse). Substitute into the constitutive law:

$$
[T]\{\sigma\}_{xy} = [Q]\,[T]^{-T}\{\varepsilon\}_{xy}
$$

**Step 4:** Pre-multiply both sides by $[T]^{-1}$:

$$
\{\sigma\}_{xy} = \underbrace{[T]^{-1}[Q][T]^{-T}}_{[\bar{Q}]}\,\{\varepsilon\}_{xy}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{[\bar{Q}] = [T]^{-1}[Q][T]^{-T}}
$$

This is the **master off-axis constitutive equation** and is the single most important formula of Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — Material Classification (Why a FRP Ply is Orthotropic, not Isotropic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Linear Elastic Material] --> B[Isotropic]
    A --> C[Anisotropic]
    C --> D[Orthotropic]
    C --> E[Monoclinic]
    C --> F[Triclinic]
    D --> G[Unidirectional FRP Ply]
    B --> H[Same E and nu in all directions]
    D --> I[Two perpendicular symmetry planes]
    G --> J[Five independent constants]
    J --> K1[E1]
    J --> K2[E2]
    J --> K3[G12]
    J --> K4[nu12]
    J --> K5[nu21]
```

### 4.2 Diagram B — Stress, Strain & Displacement Coupling in a Composite Hull Plies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Hull Load Nx Ny Nxy] --> B[Stress in Global Frame sigma x sigma y tau xy]
    B --> C[Apply Inverse T to rotate to ply local frame]
    C --> D[Stress in Material Frame sigma 1 sigma 2 tau 12]
    D --> E[Apply Q]
    E --> F[Strain in Material Frame epsilon 1 epsilon 2 gamma 12]
    F --> G[Apply Inverse T transpose]
    G --> H[Strain in Global Frame epsilon x epsilon y gamma xy]
    H --> I[Integrate via kinematic relations]
    I --> J[Displacement field u v w]
    J --> K[Hull Deformation]
```

### 4.3 Diagram C — Block-Level Architecture of the Constitutive Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InputA[Engineering Constants E1 E2 G12 nu12] --> Calc[Compute Q Matrix]
    InputB[Ply Angle theta] --> CalcT[Compute T Matrix]
    Calc --> Mult[Multiply]
    CalcT --> Mult
    Mult --> Qbar[Compute Q bar via T inverse Q T inverse T]
    Qbar --> Out[Output Off Axis Stiffness Q bar]
    Qbar --> Apply[Apply to Strain]
    Apply --> StressOut[Compute Off Axis Stress]
```

### 4.4 Diagram D — Strain-Displacement Kinematics in a Ply Element

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Original Point P] -->|displacement u| P1[Deformed Point P prime]
    P1 --> Compute1[Compute du dx as epsilon x]
    P1 --> Compute2[Compute dv dy as epsilon y]
    P1 --> Compute3[Compute du dy plus dv dx as gamma xy]
    Compute1 --> StrainVec[Strain Vector epsilon x epsilon y gamma xy]
    Compute2 --> StrainVec
    Compute3 --> StrainVec
    StrainVec --> Constit[Multiply by Q bar]
    Constit --> StressVec[Stress Vector sigma x sigma y tau xy]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Hint:** Each diagram above maps directly to one of the three mathematical layers introduced in Section 2 (kinematics, constitutive law, equilibrium). Together they form the closed-loop pipeline that converts hull loads into final displacement.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
Define the **reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$** for an orthotropic lamina under plane stress. List its four independent elements in terms of engineering constants.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Under the plane stress assumption ($\sigma_3=\tau_{23}=\tau_{31}=0$), the 3-D constitutive law reduces to a $3\times 3$ relation in the principal material axes $(1,2)$. The reduced stiffness matrix is:

$$
[Q] = \begin{bmatrix} Q_{11} & Q_{12} & 0 \\ Q_{12} & Q_{22} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & Q_{66} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where
$$
Q_{11}=\frac{E_1}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}},\; Q_{22}=\frac{E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}},\; Q_{12}=\frac{\nu_{12}E_2}{1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21}},\; Q_{66}=G_{12}.
$$

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
State and justify the **plane stress assumption** used in deriving the constitutive equations for a single ply of a marine composite hull.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
*Statement:* In a thin composite lamina, $\sigma_z=\tau_{yz}=\tau_{xz}=0$ throughout the thickness.
*Justification:* (i) Hull skin thickness $t$ is at least two orders of magnitude smaller than the in-plane dimensions; (ii) the laminate faces are traction-free (no external pressure acts perpendicular to the surface except hydrostatic, which is carried separately); (iii) shear-through-thickness stresses are negligible compared with in-plane stresses. Therefore, the stress state is essentially **biaxial in-plane**, and only the in-plane components of strain ($\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2, \gamma_{12}$) need to be considered.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — Full 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Starting from the 3-D generalised Hooke's law for an orthotropic material, derive the reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$ for plane stress. Express each element in terms of engineering constants. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A unidirectional glass/epoxy ply used in a fishing-boat hull has the following properties: $E_1=38\,\text{GPa},\; E_2=8.5\,\text{GPa},\; G_{12}=4.5\,\text{GPa},\; \nu_{12}=0.30$. Determine the **complete reduced stiffness matrix $[Q]$** and the **compliance matrix $[S]$**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *The full derivation is given in Section 3.1 above. Briefly outline the key steps:*

1. Write the 6 equations of generalised Hooke's law.
2. Apply plane stress: $\sigma_3=\tau_{23}=\tau_{31}=0$.
3. From $\sigma_3=0$, solve for $\varepsilon_3$.
4. Substitute back to eliminate $\varepsilon_3$ from $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ expressions.
5. Identify the new coefficients as $Q_{11}, Q_{22}, Q_{12}$.
6. The shear equation is unaffected, giving $Q_{66}=G_{12}$.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

*Step 1 — Reciprocal Poisson's ratio:*
$$
\nu_{21} = \nu_{12}\cdot\frac{E_2}{E_1} = 0.30 \times \frac{8.5}{38} = 0.0671
$$

*Step 2 — Denominator:*
$$
1 - \nu_{12}\nu_{21} = 1 - (0.30)(0.0671) = 1 - 0.0201 = 0.9799
$$

*Step 3 — Compute $Q$ elements:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{11} &= \frac{38}{0.9799} = 38.78\;\text{GPa} \\
Q_{22} &= \frac{8.5}{0.9799} = 8.674\;\text{GPa} \\
Q_{12} &= \frac{(0.30)(8.5)}{0.9799} = \frac{2.55}{0.9799} = 2.602\;\text{GPa} \\
Q_{66} &= G_{12} = 4.5\;\text{GPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
[Q] = \begin{bmatrix} 38.78 & 2.602 & 0 \\ 2.602 & 8.674 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4.5 \end{bmatrix}\;\text{GPa}
$$

*Step 4 — Compliance matrix $[S]$:*
$$
[S] = \begin{bmatrix} 1/E_1 & -\nu_{21}/E_2 & 0 \\ -\nu_{12}/E_1 & 1/E_2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1/G_{12} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.02632 & -0.00789 & 0 \\ -0.00789 & 0.11765 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.22222 \end{bmatrix}\;\text{(GPa)}^{-1}
$$

*Verification:* $[Q][S] \approx [I]$ — confirms consistency.

---

#### **Question B — Full 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** For a unidirectional ply whose fibres make an angle $\theta$ with the global $x$-axis, derive the **stress transformation equations** relating $(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \tau_{12})$ to $(\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy})$. Express them in matrix form using the transformation matrix $[T]$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Hence, **derive the master relation** $[\bar{Q}] = [T]^{-1}[Q][T]^{-T}$ that gives the off-axis stiffness matrix, clearly stating why the inverse-transpose (and not just the inverse) is required. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Begin from the 2-D stress tensor transformation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \sigma_x\cos^{2}\theta + \sigma_y\sin^{2}\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta \\
\sigma_2 &= \sigma_x\sin^{2}\theta + \sigma_y\cos^{2}\theta - 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta \\
\tau_{12} &= (\sigma_y-\sigma_x)\sin\theta\cos\theta + \tau_{xy}(\cos^{2}\theta-\sin^{2}\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

In matrix form:

$$
\begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_1 \\ \sigma_2 \\ \tau_{12} \end{Bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} c^{2} & s^{2} & 2sc \\ s^{2} & c^{2} & -2sc \\ -sc & sc & c^{2}-s^{2} \end{bmatrix}}_{[T]} \begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{Bmatrix}, \quad c=\cos\theta,\;s=\sin\theta
$$

*(Full derivation using Mohr's circle / stress-tensor rotation: rotate coordinate axes counter-clockwise by $\theta$. The stress tensor component in the new frame is $\sigma_{i'j'} = a_{ip}\,a_{jq}\,\sigma_{pq}$, where $a_{ip}$ are direction cosines.)*

**(b)** Steps:
1. From $(a)$: $\{\sigma\}_{12} = [T]\{\sigma\}_{xy}$.
2. Tensorial strain transforms the same way: $\{\varepsilon^{\text{ten}}\}_{12} = [T]\{\varepsilon^{\text{ten}}\}_{xy}$.
3. In Voigt contracted notation, engineering shear strain $\gamma_{xy}=2\varepsilon^{\text{ten}}_{xy}$, so a *Reuter matrix* $[R]$ is needed: $\{\varepsilon\}_{12} = [R]\{\varepsilon\}_{xy}$, where $[R] = [T]^{-T}$.
4. Substituting into $\{\sigma\}_{12} = [Q]\{\varepsilon\}_{12}$:
$$
[T]\{\sigma\}_{xy} = [Q][T]^{-T}\{\varepsilon\}_{xy}
$$
5. Pre-multiply by $[T]^{-1}$:
$$
\{\sigma\}_{xy} = [T]^{-1}[Q][T]^{-T}\{\varepsilon\}_{xy} \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{[\bar{Q}] = [T]^{-1}[Q][T]^{-T}}
$$

*Why inverse-transpose?* Because the strain vector in contracted notation uses the engineering shear $\gamma_{xy}$, which is twice the tensorial component. The transformation matrix that takes $(\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \gamma_{xy})$ to $(\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2, \gamma_{12})$ is $[R]$, and it is precisely $[T]^{-T}$.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing $[Q]$ and $[\bar{Q}]$:** $[Q]$ is in material axes $(1,2)$; $[\bar{Q}]$ is in global axes $(x,y)$. Both matrices are symmetric, but only $[Q]$ is orthotropic.
> 2. **Forgetting the reciprocal relation:** Always verify $\nu_{21}=\nu_{12}E_2/E_1$ — examiners award a half-mark if missing.
> 3. **Wrong sign in $[T]$:** The $(3,3)$ entry is $c^2-s^2=\cos 2\theta$, **not** $s^2-c^2$.
> 4. **Using $[T]$ instead of $[T]^{-T}$ for strain:** The strain vector contains the engineering shear $\gamma$, hence the inverse-transpose is mandatory. This is the single most common error in the question "Derive $[\bar{Q}]$".
> 5. **Assuming isotropy:** Do **not** write $\nu_{12}=\nu_{21}$ unless the material is isotropic (it is not, for FRP).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Generalised Hooke's Law** in 3-D: 36 constants $\rightarrow$ reduced by symmetries.
- [x] **Orthotropic ply** has only **5 independent constants**: $E_1, E_2, G_{12}, \nu_{12}, \nu_{21}$.
- [x] **Reciprocal relation:** $\nu_{21}/E_2 = \nu_{12}/E_1$ is the symmetry condition for $[C]$.
- [x] **Plane stress assumption** valid because hull skin is thin ($t \ll L, B$).
- [x] **Reduced stiffness $[Q]$** is $3\times 3$, symmetric, defined in material axes $(1,2)$.
- [x] **Transformation matrix $[T]$** uses $c=\cos\theta, s=\sin\theta$.
- [x] **Master off-axis formula:** $[\bar{Q}] = [T]^{-1}[Q][T]^{-T}$ — must use **inverse-transpose**, not just inverse, because of the engineering shear convention.
- [x] **Three coupled layers** in any composite hull: Kinematics (strain–displacement) → Constitutive (stress–strain) → Equilibrium.
- [x] **Compliance $[S] = [Q]^{-1}$** contains direct and coupling compliances.
- [x] **Carbon/epoxy** has higher $E_1$ but similar $E_2$ compared with glass/epoxy — affects hull stiffness dramatically.
- [x] **Numerical safety check:** always verify $1-\nu_{12}\nu_{21} > 0$ (otherwise the material is unstable).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to derive $[\bar{Q}]$, write out all three steps explicitly: (i) transform stress, (ii) transform strain, (iii) substitute and pre-multiply. Most students who skip step (ii) lose 2–3 marks instantly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coupling Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Coupling Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coupling Effect** in composite laminates is the phenomenon in which an applied load (force or moment) of one specific type produces a response (deformation) of a fundamentally different physical nature. In a boat hull laminate, this means that an in-plane tensile load may induce curvature (bending) or twisting, and conversely, a bending moment may produce in-plane stretching or shear.

Mathematically, the **Coupling Effect** is governed by the off-diagonal terms of the laminate stiffness matrices derived from **Classical Laminate Theory (CLT)**. The governing constitutive equation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{Bmatrix} N \\ M \end{Bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^{0} \\ \kappa \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
* $\begin{Bmatrix} N \end{Bmatrix}$ = In-plane force resultants (N/m)
* $\begin{Bmatrix} M \end{Bmatrix}$ = Moment resultants (N·m/m)
* $[A]$ = **Extensional Stiffness Matrix** (N/m)
* $[B]$ = **Coupling Stiffness Matrix** (N) — *This is the origin of the coupling effect*
* $[D]$ = **Bending Stiffness Matrix** (N·m)
* $\begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^{0} \end{Bmatrix}$ = Mid-plane strains
* $\begin{Bmatrix} \kappa \end{Bmatrix}$ = Curvatures (rad/m)

A non-zero $[B]$ matrix directly indicates the presence of the **extension-bending coupling effect**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bimetallic Strip

Imagine two flat strips of dissimilar metals (e.g., brass and Invar) bonded firmly together. If you heat the assembly uniformly:
* The **brass** wants to expand more than the **Invar**.
* Because they are physically bonded, neither can expand freely.
* The result? The strip **bends** into an arc.

This is exactly what happens inside a composite laminate. Each ply (layer) of oriented fibers behaves like a different "metal." When a load is applied:
* The **0° plies** are very stiff along the load direction but weak transversely.
* The **90° plies** behave oppositely.
* The **±45° plies** are dominated by shear response.

The laminate is forced to deform in a way that is a *compromise* between these conflicting stiffnesses. This compromise is the **coupling effect**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Boat Scenario:** A sailor applies a single-sided foot pressure on the cockpit floor of a fiberglass boat. Instead of the hull simply bending inward, the deck may simultaneously twist, the side may bulge outward, and the keel may slightly extend — *all from one load*. This is the **Coupling Effect** in action.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

* Standard ply thickness for marine composites: **0.125 mm to 0.5 mm** (typical single-ply prepreg)
* Typical hull laminate thickness: **6 mm to 25 mm**
* Hull material density: **~1850 kg/m³** for E-glass/polyester
* Coupling stiffness units: **Newtons (N)** for $[B]$ matrix
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Coupling Effects in Laminates

In a general composite laminate, three distinct types of coupling can occur. Each corresponds to a different sub-matrix in the global $[A B D]$ stiffness system.

### (a) Membrane-Bending Coupling (Extension–Bending Coupling)
* **Source:** Non-zero $[B]$ matrix.
* **Physical Effect:** In-plane forces $N_x, N_y, N_{xy}$ produce curvatures $\kappa_x, \kappa_y, \kappa_{xy}$. Conversely, moments $M_x, M_y, M_{xy}$ produce mid-plane strains.
* **Boat Design Impact:** A hull panel stretched longitudinally will bow (buckle) out-of-plane — destroying hydrodynamic shape.

### (b) Extension-Shear Coupling (Membrane Coupling)
* **Source:** Non-zero $A_{16}$ and $A_{26}$ terms in the $[A]$ matrix.
* **Physical Effect:** An axial load $N_x$ produces shear strain $\varepsilon_{xy}$, even if no shear force is applied.
* **Boat Design Impact:** Racking loads on a hull (diagonal tension) cause shearing distortion instead of pure stretching.

### (c) Bending-Twist Coupling (Flexural-Torsional Coupling)
* **Source:** Non-zero $D_{16}$ and $D_{26}$ terms in the $[D]$ matrix.
* **Physical Effect:** A pure bending moment $M_x$ causes twist curvature $\kappa_{xy}$.
* **Boat Design Impact:** A transverse wave load causes the hull bottom to *twist* — extremely dangerous for propeller shafts and rudder alignment.

## 2.2 Conditions for Elimination of Coupling

The Coupling Effect is **eliminated** (or dramatically reduced) under specific layup configurations:

| Layup Configuration | Coupling Behavior | Use in Boat Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Symmetric Laminate** (e.g., [0/90/90/0]) | $[B] = 0$ — No extension-bending coupling | Hull shells, decks |
| **Specially Orthotropic** (e.g., [0/90/90/0]) | $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$, $D_{16} = D_{26} = 0$ | Flat panels, bulkheads |
| **Cross-Ply** (e.g., [0/90/0/90]) | $[B] = 0$, all coupling-shear terms vanish | Flat bottoms, floors |
| **Unsymmetric Angle-Ply** (e.g., [30/−30]) | Strong **extension-shear** + **bending-twist** coupling | **AVOID** in primary hull structure |
| **General Unsymmetric** (e.g., [0/45/90]) | All coupling types present | **AVOID** in primary hull structure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Takeaway:** In boat hull design, **symmetric, balanced, and specially orthotropic** laminates are mandated for primary hull skins to suppress the Coupling Effect. The presence of coupling is the single most common reason for premature hull failure in improperly designed FRP (Fiber Reinforced Plastic) vessels.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Global Force-Moment Vector | $\{N, M\}$ | $\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix} \{\varepsilon^0, \kappa\}$ | N/m, N·m/m | Master CLT equation |
| Extensional Stiffness | $A_{ij}$ | $\sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_k - z_{k-1})$ | N/m | In-plane stiffness |
| **Coupling Stiffness** | $B_{ij}$ | $\sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}}{2} (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2)$ | **N** | **Origin of coupling** |
| Bending Stiffness | $D_{ij}$ | $\sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}}{3} (z_k^3 - z_{k-1}^3)$ | N·m | Flexural stiffness |
| Transformed Reduced Stiffness | $\bar{Q}_{ij}$ | Depends on ply angle $\theta$ | Pa | Single-ply stiffness |
| Mid-plane strain for given N | $\varepsilon^0$ | $[A^\ast]N + [B^\ast]M$ | dimensionless | Inverse CLT form |
| Laminate Compliance | $[A^\ast]$ | Inverse of $A - B D^{-1} B$ | m/N | Used in design iteration |

> **Note on Table:** The vertical bar notation is intentionally avoided. For instance, the absolute value of a stiffness component should be written as $\vert A_{11} \vert$ in plain text, not $\vert A_{11} \vert$ inside a table cell.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the marine industry, the Coupling Effect is **deliberately exploited** in some applications:
* **Sailboat Mast Sections:** Controlled bending-twist coupling allows the mast to "untwist" as it bends, depowering the sail and preventing capsize in gusts.
* **Helicopter Rotor Blades:** Composite blades are designed with intentional coupling to adaptively change pitch.
* **Propeller Blades:** Coupling allows pitch changes under centrifugal and hydrodynamic loads.

However, in **hull, deck, and bulkhead** structures, coupling is treated as a **defect to be suppressed** using symmetric, balanced layups.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Coupling Stiffness Matrix [B]

The $[B]$ matrix is the mathematical heart of the Coupling Effect. We derive it from the fundamental assumption of Classical Laminate Theory: the displacement field is linear through the thickness.

### Step 1: Assume a Linear Displacement Field
By Kirchhoff-Love plate theory, the displacement $u(x,y,z)$ at any thickness $z$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u(x,y,z) &= u^{0}(x,y) - z \cdot \frac{\partial w^{0}}{\partial x} \\
v(x,y,z) &= v^{0}(x,y) - z \cdot \frac{\partial w^{0}}{\partial y} \\
w(x,y,z) &= w^{0}(x,y)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $u^0, v^0, w^0$ are the mid-plane displacements.

### Step 2: Derive the Strain Field
Differentiating the displacement field with respect to $x, y, z$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{x} &= \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial u^{0}}{\partial x} - z \cdot \frac{\partial^{2} w^{0}}{\partial x^{2}} = \varepsilon_{x}^{0} - z \cdot \kappa_{x} \\
\varepsilon_{y} &= \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial v^{0}}{\partial y} - z \cdot \frac{\partial^{2} w^{0}}{\partial y^{2}} = \varepsilon_{y}^{0} - z \cdot \kappa_{y} \\
\gamma_{xy} &= \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \gamma_{xy}^{0} - 2z \cdot \frac{\partial^{2} w^{0}}{\partial x \partial y} = \gamma_{xy}^{0} - z \cdot \kappa_{xy}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Each strain is the sum of a mid-plane strain ($\varepsilon^0$) and a curvature term ($z \cdot \kappa$). This linear $z$-dependence is what couples membrane and bending.

### Step 3: Apply the Constitutive Law for Each Ply
For the $k$-th ply with transformed reduced stiffness $\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{x}^{(k)} &= \bar{Q}_{11}^{(k)} \varepsilon_{x} + \bar{Q}_{12}^{(k)} \varepsilon_{y} + \bar{Q}_{16}^{(k)} \gamma_{xy}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Integrate Stress Through the Thickness to Obtain Resultant Forces
The in-plane force resultant $N_x$ is the integral of $\sigma_x$ from $z = -h/2$ to $z = h/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{x} &= \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \sigma_{x}^{(k)} \, dz = \int_{-h/2}^{h/2} \left[ \bar{Q}_{11}^{(k)} (\varepsilon_{x}^{0} - z\kappa_{x}) + \bar{Q}_{12}^{(k)} (\varepsilon_{y}^{0} - z\kappa_{y}) + \bar{Q}_{16}^{(k)} (\gamma_{xy}^{0} - z\kappa_{xy}) \right] dz
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Separate the Integral into Membrane and Bending Parts
Because the layup consists of discrete plies, the integral becomes a sum over $N$ plies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{x} &= \underbrace{\left( \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{11}^{(k)} \Delta z_{k} \right)}_{A_{11}} \varepsilon_{x}^{0} + \underbrace{\left( \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{12}^{(k)} \Delta z_{k} \right)}_{A_{12}} \varepsilon_{y}^{0} + \underbrace{\left( \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{16}^{(k)} \Delta z_{k} \right)}_{A_{16}} \gamma_{xy}^{0} \\
&\quad - \underbrace{\left( \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{\bar{Q}_{11}^{(k)}}{2} (z_{k}^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2}) \right)}_{B_{11}} \kappa_{x} - \underbrace{\left( \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{\bar{Q}_{12}^{(k)}}{2} (z_{k}^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2}) \right)}_{B_{12}} \kappa_{y} - \underbrace{\left( \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{\bar{Q}_{16}^{(k)}}{2} (z_{k}^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2}) \right)}_{B_{16}} \kappa_{xy}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The terms with $A_{ij}$ multiply mid-plane strains (membrane behavior), while the terms with $B_{ij}$ multiply curvatures (bending behavior). The fact that $N_x$ contains both $A$ and $B$ terms **is the Coupling Effect**.

### Step 6: Generalize the Coupling Stiffness Formula
Repeating the procedure for $N_y, N_{xy}, M_x, M_y, M_{xy}$, we obtain the general element of the $[B]$ matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{ij} &= \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{1}{2} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} \left( z_{k}^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **fundamental Coupling Effect formula** for KTU 2024 board examinations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** The $[B]$ matrix is **zero** for any laminate that is **symmetric about its mid-plane** (i.e., $z_k = -z_{N+1-k}$). In such a case, the quadratic term $z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2$ becomes self-cancelling across symmetric plies. This is the **mathematical proof** of why symmetric laminates eliminate coupling.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A boat hull laminate has the following stacking sequence: [30°/−30°/0°/90°]ₛ with each ply of thickness $t = 0.25$ mm. The material is E-Glass/Epoxy with the following transformed reduced stiffness values (in GPa):

| Ply | $\bar{Q}_{11}$ | $\bar{Q}_{12}$ | $\bar{Q}_{16}$ | $\bar{Q}_{22}$ | $\bar{Q}_{26}$ | $\bar{Q}_{66}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 30° | 28.5 | 7.2 | 9.8 | 14.6 | 6.5 | 9.2 |
| −30° | 28.5 | 7.2 | −9.8 | 14.6 | −6.5 | 9.2 |
| 0° | 38.0 | 3.5 | 0 | 8.5 | 0 | 4.0 |
| 90° | 8.5 | 3.5 | 0 | 38.0 | 0 | 4.0 |

**Calculate the Coupling Stiffness Matrix [B].**

### Step 1: Identify Layer Positions
The subscript "s" means the stack is mirrored about the mid-plane. Total thickness $h = 8 \times 0.25 = 2.0$ mm. Using $z = 0$ at the geometric mid-plane:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_{0} &= -1.00 \text{ mm}, \quad z_{1} = -0.75, \quad z_{2} = -0.50, \quad z_{3} = -0.25 \\
z_{4} &= 0.00, \quad z_{5} = +0.25, \quad z_{6} = +0.50, \quad z_{7} = +0.75, \quad z_{8} = +1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

Ply assignments (bottom to top): 30°, −30°, 0°, 90°, 90°, 0°, −30°, 30°.

### Step 2: Compute $B_{11}$
Using the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{11} &= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{8} \bar{Q}_{11}^{(k)} (z_{k}^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2$ for each ply (in mm²):
* Ply 1: $0.75^2 - 1.00^2 = -0.4375$
* Ply 2: $0.50^2 - 0.75^2 = -0.3125$
* Ply 3: $0.25^2 - 0.50^2 = -0.1875$
* Ply 4: $0.00^2 - 0.25^2 = -0.0625$
* Ply 5: $0.25^2 - 0.00^2 = +0.0625$
* Ply 6: $0.50^2 - 0.25^2 = +0.1875$
* Ply 7: $0.75^2 - 0.50^2 = +0.3125$
* Ply 8: $1.00^2 - 0.75^2 = +0.4375$

Now sum the contribution of each ply:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{11} &= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ 28.5(-0.4375) + 28.5(-0.3125) + 38.0(-0.1875) + 8.5(-0.0625) \\
&\quad + 8.5(+0.0625) + 38.0(+0.1875) + 28.5(+0.3125) + 28.5(+0.4375) \Big] \times 10^{9} \times 10^{-6} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 \Big] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Because the layup is symmetric (30° and −30° plies are at symmetric positions with identical $\bar{Q}_{11}$), the negative and positive contributions cancel pairwise. $B_{11} = 0$.

### Step 3: Compute $B_{16}$ (The Critical Coupling Term)

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{16} &= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{8} \bar{Q}_{16}^{(k)} (z_{k}^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2})
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the $\bar{Q}_{16}$ values and the same $z^2$ differences:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{16} &= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ 9.8(-0.4375) + (-9.8)(-0.3125) + 0 + 0 \\
&\quad + 0 + 0 + (-9.8)(+0.3125) + 9.8(+0.4375) \Big] \times 10^{3} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ -4.2875 + 3.0625 + 0 + 0 - 3.0625 + 4.2875 \Big] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $[B] = [0]$ — **No coupling effect** because the laminate is **symmetric**. This is why boat hull designers use symmetric layups.

### Step 4: Counter-Example — Uns symmetric Layup [30°/−30°/0°/90°]
If the layup were **not** symmetric (no subscript s), with the same $z$ positions from $z_0 = -1.00$ to $z_4 = +1.00$ mm, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{16}^{unsym} &= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ 9.8(-0.4375) + (-9.8)(-0.3125) + 0(-0.1875) + 0(-0.0625) \Big] \times 10^{3} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ -4.2875 + 3.0625 \Big] = -0.6125 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Physical Meaning:** A non-zero $B_{16}$ of $-612.5$ N means that applying a pure axial force $N_x$ will induce a **twist curvature** $\kappa_{xy}$ — a classic example of the **Extension-Twist Coupling Effect** in composite boat hulls.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Coupling Effect — Functional Block Diagram

This Mermaid block diagram shows how a single input load produces multiple deformation outputs through the Coupling Effect.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Input Force N_x] --> matA[A Matrix Extension Block]
    inputB[Input Moment M_x] --> matB[D Matrix Bending Block]
    matA --> coupB[Coupling B Matrix Cross Block]
    matB --> coupB
    coupA[A16 Shear Coupling] --> matA
    coupD[D16 Twist Coupling] --> matB
    matA --> outE[Output Mid Plane Strain epsilon]
    matA --> outK1[Output Curvature kappa x Bending]
    coupB --> outK2[Output Curvature kappa xy Twist]
    coupB --> outE
    matB --> outK1
    matB --> outK2
    style inputA fill:#e1f5ff
    style inputB fill:#fff4e1
    style coupB fill:#ffe1e1
    style outE fill:#e8f5e9
    style outK1 fill:#e8f5e9
    style outK2 fill:#e8f5e9
```

## 4.2 Coupling Taxonomy — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root[Coupling Effect in Composite Laminates] --> mem[Membrane Coupling]
    root --> bend[Bending Coupling]
    root --> memBend[Membrane Bending Coupling]
    mem --> a16[Extension Shear A16]
    mem --> a26[Extension Shear A26]
    bend --> d16[Bending Twist D16]
    bend --> d26[Bending Twist D26]
    memBend --> b11[B11 Axial Bending]
    memBend --> b12[B12 Cross Bending]
    memBend --> b16[B16 Membrane Twist]
    memBend --> b26[B26 Cross Twist]
    a16 --> boatImpact1[Boat Hull Racking Distortion]
    d16 --> boatImpact2[Boat Hull Twist Under Wave Load]
    b16 --> boatImpact3[Boat Hull Bowing Under Axial Load]
    style root fill:#bbdefb
    style mem fill:#c8e6c9
    style bend fill:#fff9c4
    style memBend fill:#ffccbc
    style boatImpact1 fill:#ffcdd2
    style boatImpact2 fill:#ffcdd2
    style boatImpact3 fill:#ffcdd2
```

## 4.3 Layup Decision Flow — Suppressing Coupling in Boat Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Hull Panel Design Requirement] --> symm{Q1 Is the laminate symmetric about mid plane?}
    symm --> yes1[Yes: B matrix equals 0]
    symm --> no1[No: Coupling Effect Present]
    yes1 --> bal{Q2 Is the laminate balanced plus theta and minus theta pairs?}
    bal --> yes2[Yes: A16 equals A26 equals 0]
    bal --> no2[No: Shear coupling present]
    yes2 --> orth{Q3 Are all plies at 0 or 90 degrees?}
    orth --> yes3[Yes: D16 equals D26 equals 0]
    orth --> no3[No: Bending twist coupling present]
    yes3 --> final[Ideal Layup: Zero Coupling Approved for Hull]
    no1 --> warn1[Warning: Unsymmetric Layup Add Doubler Plates]
    no2 --> warn2[Warning: Unbalanced Angle Ply Causes Racking]
    no3 --> warn3[Warning: Angle Plies Cause Twist Under Bending]
    warn1 --> final2[Use Stiffeners to Compensate]
    warn2 --> final2
    warn3 --> final2
    style start fill:#e3f2fd
    style final fill:#c8e6c9
    style final2 fill:#fff9c4
    style warn1 fill:#ffcdd2
    style warn2 fill:#ffcdd2
    style warn3 fill:#ffcdd2
```

## 4.4 Coupling Mechanism — Physical Schematic (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ply0[Ply 1: 0 Degree Fibers] --> bond[Resin Bond Layer]
    ply45[Ply 2: 45 Degree Fibers] --> bond
    bond --> plyN[Ply N: 90 Degree Fibers]
    ply0 --> forceX[Load N_x along 0 deg]
    forceX --> response1[Pure Stretch in 0 deg]
    forceX --> response2[Shear in 45 deg]
    forceX --> response3[Constraint Reaction in 90 deg]
    response1 --> coupling[Coupling Effect Emerges]
    response2 --> coupling
    response3 --> coupling
    coupling --> deformation[Combined Output: Bending Plus Twist Plus Stretch]
    style ply0 fill:#b3e5fc
    style ply45 fill:#b3e5fc
    style plyN fill:#b3e5fc
    style bond fill:#fff59d
    style coupling fill:#ffab91
    style deformation fill:#ef9a9a
```
