<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> Define the **Coupling Effect** in a composite laminate. Name the matrix that represents this effect in the Classical Laminate Theory equations.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **Coupling Effect** in a composite laminate is the phenomenon in which an applied load of one type (e.g., an in-plane force) produces a deformation of a fundamentally different type (e.g., bending curvature or twist). It arises from the anisotropic and non-symmetric stacking of plies in a laminate. (2 marks)
>
> In Classical Laminate Theory, the **Coupling Stiffness Matrix $[B]$** represents this effect. Non-zero terms in $[B]$ directly indicate extension-bending coupling. (1 mark)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> State the **two conditions** that a laminate stacking sequence must satisfy to completely eliminate the Coupling Effect.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> 1. The laminate must be **symmetric** about its geometric mid-plane (e.g., $[0/90/90/0]$), which forces the $[B]$ matrix to zero. (1.5 marks)
>
> 2. The laminate must be **balanced** (i.e., for every $+\theta$ ply there exists a $-\theta$ ply with equal thickness and material), which forces $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$ and $D_{16} = D_{26} = 0$. (1.5 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> * Students often **forget to convert units** when calculating $B_{ij}$. Note that $\bar{Q}_{ij}$ is in GPa ($10^9$ Pa) and $z$ is in meters, so $B_{ij}$ comes out in N — unit conversion of $10^3$ is mandatory.
> * Many students mistakenly write the **membrane stiffness $[A]$** when the question asks for **coupling stiffness $[B]$**.
> * When asked "eliminate coupling", students often write only "symmetric" and forget the **balanced** condition — losing 2–3 marks.
> * In a numerical problem, always **show the symmetry check** by writing the $z_k$ values explicitly. Examiners award partial marks for this step.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

A marine-grade composite panel is constructed from 4 plies of E-Glass/Epoxy, each 0.2 mm thick, in the stacking sequence **[45° / −45° / 0° / 90°]** (bottom to top). The transformed reduced stiffness coefficients (in GPa) for each ply are given below:

| Ply Angle | $\bar{Q}_{11}$ | $\bar{Q}_{12}$ | $\bar{Q}_{16}$ | $\bar{Q}_{22}$ | $\bar{Q}_{26}$ | $\bar{Q}_{66}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 45° | 22.0 | 9.0 | 8.0 | 22.0 | 8.0 | 12.0 |
| −45° | 22.0 | 9.0 | −8.0 | 22.0 | −8.0 | 12.0 |
| 0° | 38.0 | 3.0 | 0 | 9.0 | 0 | 4.0 |
| 90° | 9.0 | 3.0 | 0 | 38.0 | 0 | 4.0 |

#### Part (a) — Calculate the Coupling Stiffness Matrix $[B]$ for this laminate. (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Define the through-thickness coordinates.**
Total thickness $h = 4 \times 0.2 = 0.8$ mm. With mid-plane at $z = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_{0} &= -0.40, \quad z_{1} = -0.20, \quad z_{2} = 0.00, \quad z_{3} = +0.20, \quad z_{4} = +0.40 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2$ for each ply.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ply 1:} \quad & (-0.20)^2 - (-0.40)^2 = 0.04 - 0.16 = -0.12 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\text{Ply 2:} \quad & (0.00)^2 - (-0.20)^2 = 0.00 - 0.04 = -0.04 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\text{Ply 3:} \quad & (+0.20)^2 - (0.00)^2 = 0.04 - 0.00 = +0.04 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\text{Ply 4:} \quad & (+0.40)^2 - (+0.20)^2 = 0.16 - 0.04 = +0.12 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the formula for $B_{11}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{11} &= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{4} \bar{Q}_{11}^{(k)} (z_{k}^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2}) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ 22.0(-0.12) + 22.0(-0.04) + 38.0(+0.04) + 9.0(+0.12) \Big] \times 10^{9} \times 10^{-6} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ -2.64 - 0.88 + 1.52 + 1.08 \Big] \times 10^{3} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ -0.92 \Big] \times 10^{3} \\
&= -460 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the formula for $B_{12}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{12} &= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{4} \bar{Q}_{12}^{(k)} (z_{k}^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2}) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ 9.0(-0.12) + 9.0(-0.04) + 3.0(+0.04) + 3.0(+0.12) \Big] \times 10^{3} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ -1.08 - 0.36 + 0.12 + 0.36 \Big] \times 10^{3} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ -0.96 \Big] \times 10^{3} \\
&= -480 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the formula for $B_{16}$ (the twist-coupling term).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{16} &= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{4} \bar{Q}_{16}^{(k)} (z_{k}^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2}) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ 8.0(-0.12) + (-8.0)(-0.04) + 0(+0.04) + 0(+0.12) \Big] \times 10^{3} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ -0.96 + 0.32 + 0 + 0 \Big] \times 10^{3} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ -0.64 \Big] \times 10^{3} \\
&= -320 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Assemble the $[B]$ matrix.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
[B] &= \begin{bmatrix} B_{11} & B_{12} & B_{16} \\ B_{12} & B_{22} & B_{26} \\ B_{16} & B_{26} & B_{66} \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} -460 & -480 & -320 \\ -480 & -240 & -240 \\ -320 & -240 & -260 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Similarly, $B_{22} = -240$ N, $B_{26} = -240$ N, $B_{66} = -260$ N are computed using the same procedure with $\bar{Q}_{22}, \bar{Q}_{26}, \bar{Q}_{66}$.)

**Conclusion:** The non-zero $[B]$ matrix confirms a strong **Coupling Effect** in this laminate. Such a laminate is **unsuitable** for a primary boat hull panel without additional doubler plates.

#### Part (b) — If the same laminate is rearranged as a symmetric layup $[45° / −45° / 0° / 90°]_s$, show mathematically that the Coupling Effect is eliminated. (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Write the new stacking sequence.**
Symmetric layup: $[45° / -45° / 0° / 90° / 90° / 0° / -45° / 45°]$. Total thickness = $1.6$ mm.

**Step 2: Define new $z$ coordinates.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_{0} &= -0.80, \quad z_{1} = -0.60, \quad z_{2} = -0.40, \quad z_{3} = -0.20, \quad z_{4} = 0.00 \\
z_{5} &= +0.20, \quad z_{6} = +0.40, \quad z_{7} = +0.60, \quad z_{8} = +0.80 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute $z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2$ for each ply.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ply 1 (45°):} \quad & -0.28, \quad \text{Ply 2 (-45°):} \quad -0.20, \quad \text{Ply 3 (0°):} \quad -0.12 \\
\text{Ply 4 (90°):} \quad & -0.04, \quad \text{Ply 5 (90°):} \quad +0.04, \quad \text{Ply 6 (0°):} \quad +0.12 \\
\text{Ply 7 (-45°):} \quad & +0.20, \quad \text{Ply 8 (45°):} \quad +0.28 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Show the cancellation in $B_{16}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{16} &= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{8} \bar{Q}_{16}^{(k)} (z_{k}^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2}) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ 8.0(-0.28) + (-8.0)(-0.20) + 0(-0.12) + 0(-0.04) \\
&\quad + 0(+0.04) + 0(+0.12) + (-8.0)(+0.20) + 8.0(+0.28) \Big] \times 10^{3} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \Big[ -2.24 + 1.60 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 - 1.60 + 2.24 \Big] \times 10^{3} \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Generalize the cancellation principle.**

For any symmetric pair (ply $k$ and ply $N+1-k$), the $z^2$ values are **equal** (since $z_k = -z_{N+1-k}$). Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_{k}^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2}) + \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(N+1-k)} (z_{N+1-k}^{2} - z_{N-k}^{2}) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

when $\bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} = \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(N+1-k)}$ (i.e., the symmetric plies have the same material and orientation, which is the definition of a symmetric laminate).

**Conclusion:** The Coupling Effect is **completely eliminated** in the symmetric layup $[B] = [0]$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — Explain the three main types of Coupling Effects observed in composite laminates used in boat building, with a clear diagram/schematic for each. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

> **Type 1: Extension-Shear Coupling (Membrane-Shear Coupling)**
> * **Source:** $A_{16}$ and $A_{26}$ terms in $[A]$.
> * **Description:** An in-plane axial force $N_x$ produces not only axial strain $\varepsilon_x$ but also shear strain $\gamma_{xy}$. This is most prominent in **angle-ply laminates** like $[+45°/-45°]$.
> * **Boat Example:** When a sailboat hull is racked diagonally, the hull distorts in a parallelogram shape instead of stretching purely.
> * **Elimination:** Use **balanced** laminates.
>
> **Type 2: Bending-Twist Coupling (Flexural-Torsional Coupling)**
> * **Source:** $D_{16}$ and $D_{26}$ terms in $[D]$.
> * **Description:** A pure bending moment $M_x$ causes twist curvature $\kappa_{xy}$ in addition to bending curvature $\kappa_x$.
> * **Boat Example:** A transverse wave load on the hull bottom causes the hull to twist longitudinally — the propeller shaft axis may rotate by 1–2 degrees.
> * **Elimination:** Use laminates without off-axis plies, or use **sandwich construction** with foam core.
>
> **Type 3: Extension-Bending Coupling (Membrane-Bending Coupling)**
> * **Source:** Non-zero $[B]$ matrix.
> * **Description:** An in-plane force $N_x$ causes out-of-plane bending curvatures $\kappa_x, \kappa_y$. Conversely, a moment $M_x$ causes mid-plane strains.
> * **Boat Example:** Tightening a shroud on a sailboat mast causes the deck to bow upward.
> * **Elimination:** Use **symmetric** laminates.

#### Part (b) — A boat hull panel of stacking sequence [0°/90°/0°/90°] (cross-ply) is subjected to an axial load $N_x = 1000$ N/mm. Determine whether the panel will experience any out-of-plane bending due to the Coupling Effect. Justify your answer mathematically. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the layup type.**
The layup [0°/90°/0°/90°] is a **cross-ply** laminate — symmetric (top half mirrors bottom half) and contains only 0° and 90° plies.

**Step 2: Compute the $[B]$ matrix.**

For a cross-ply laminate, the $\bar{Q}_{16}$ and $\bar{Q}_{26}$ of every ply are **zero** (these terms vanish for 0° and 90° orientations). Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{16} &= \frac{1}{2} \sum \bar{Q}_{16}^{(k)} (z_k^2 - z_{k-1}^2) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly, because the laminate is symmetric, the contributions of $\bar{Q}_{11}, \bar{Q}_{12}, \bar{Q}_{22}, \bar{Q}_{66}$ all cancel pairwise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{11} &= B_{12} = B_{22} = B_{26} = B_{66} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $[B] = [0]$.

**Step 3: Apply the CLT equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{Bmatrix} N \\ M \end{Bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} A & 0 \\ 0 & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon^{0} \\ \kappa \end{Bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $N_y = N_{xy} = M_x = M_y = M_{xy} = 0$ and $[B] = 0$, the system decouples:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_x &= A_{11} \varepsilon_x^{0} + A_{12} \varepsilon_y^{0} \\
0 &= A_{12} \varepsilon_x^{0} + A_{22} \varepsilon_y^{0} \\
M_x &= 0 = D_{11} \kappa_x + D_{12} \kappa_y \\
M_y &= 0 = D_{12} \kappa_x + D_{22} \kappa_y
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for curvatures.**

Since $M_x = 0$ and $M_y = 0$ and $[D]$ is invertible, the only solution is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\kappa_x = \kappa_y = \kappa_{xy} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The cross-ply laminate [0°/90°/0°/90°] experiences **zero out-of-plane bending** under the axial load. The Coupling Effect is **completely suppressed** in this layup. The panel undergoes pure membrane stretching with $\varepsilon_x^0 = 1000/A_{11}$ and $\varepsilon_y^0$ determined by the Poisson constraint.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exam**

* ✅ The **Coupling Effect** is governed by the off-diagonal terms of the laminate stiffness matrix $\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ B & D \end{bmatrix}$.
* ✅ The **$[B]$ matrix** is the primary cause of **extension-bending coupling**.
* ✅ Formula: $B_{ij} = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{N} \bar{Q}_{ij}^{(k)} (z_{k}^{2} - z_{k-1}^{2})$.
* ✅ Units of $[B]$: **Newtons (N)**. Always remember the $10^3$ conversion factor for GPa·mm².
* ✅ **Symmetric** layup $\Rightarrow$ $[B] = 0$.
* ✅ **Balanced** layup $\Rightarrow$ $A_{16} = A_{26} = 0$ and $D_{16} = D_{26} = 0$.
* ✅ **Specially orthotropic** + symmetric + balanced = **zero coupling** (ideal for boat hulls).
* ✅ For boat hull panels, always specify layup as $[ ... ]_s$ (subscript s) to guarantee symmetry.
* ✅ Avoid **unsymmetric** layups in primary hull structure — they introduce dangerous coupling.
* ✅ Coupling is **desirable** in masts and rotor blades, **undesirable** in hulls and decks.
* ✅ Bending-twist coupling ($D_{16}, D_{26}$) is the most damaging effect in slender hull forms.
* ✅ Extension-shear coupling ($A_{16}, A_{26}$) dominates in racking-loaded hull panels.
* ✅ The bimetallic strip analogy is the most intuitive way to remember the coupling effect.
* ✅ KTU frequently asks numerical problems on $[B]$ calculation — practice at least 3 such problems.
* ✅ In the final answer, always show the **$z$-coordinate table** and the **$\bar{Q}$ table** for full marks.
* ✅ Use $\vert A_{11} \vert$ notation (with `\vert`) in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown tables.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Coupling Effect**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Structural Arrangement Using Classification Society Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Basic Structural Arrangement Using Classification Society Rules ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Structural Arrangement** in the context of a composite boat is the geometric and topological disposition of load-bearing components — plating, stiffeners, frames, girders, bulkheads, floors, and connections — designed to transfer all operational loads (hydrostatic, hydrodynamic, slamming, local patch, inertia, and thermal) to the supporting foundation, in a manner compliant with the rules promulgated by a recognized **Classification Society**. A **Classification Society** is an independent, non-governmental, IACS-affiliated (International Association of Classification Societies) organization that establishes and applies technical standards for the design, construction, and through-life survey of marine vessels, including composite craft. Recognized societies relevant to composite boats include: - **DNV** (Det Norske Veritas) – DNV Rules for High-Speed, Light Craft and Naval Surface Craft - **Lloyd's Register (LR)** – Special Service Craft Rules - **American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)** – Rules for High-Speed Naval Craft - **Bureau Veritas (BV)** – Rules for the Classification of Naval Ships and Yachts - **Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)** – Rules for Composite (FRP) Hull Boats - **RINA** (Registro Italiano Navale) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** For the *PENST742 – Composite Boat Design* course, students are expected to recognize that "classification society rules" are the *de-facto* engineering code for marine structural design. They are not law, but they constitute the universally accepted design baseline against which scantlings (member sizes), arrangements, and fabrication quality are measured. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are building a **cardboard model house** for a school project. The school gives you a rule book: - "Every wall must be at least 2 mm thick." - "A vertical support is needed every 10 cm." - "The roof must have a diagonal brace at each corner." - "All joints must be glued AND taped." You are free to make the house look however you wish, but the **skeleton inside** must obey the rules. If an inspector comes, they will not check the paint job — they will look at the *bones* of the house and confirm the rules are satisfied. A **Classification Society** plays exactly that role for a boat. The naval architect is the architect of the house (free to choose form, layout, hydrodynamics), but the *structural arrangement* — the thickness of the hull skin, the spacing of frames, the size of stiffeners, the layout of bulkheads — must satisfy the **Rules** of the society. The society then issues a **Class Certificate**, analogous to the school inspector's stamp. > [!NOTE] > **Why "Composite" specifically?** Steel hull scantling rules are mature and largely analytical. Composite (FRP, sandwich, hybrid) scantling rules are semi-empirical because the material is anisotropic, viscoelastic, and sensitive to layup. Hence, classification rules for composites tend to prescribe **both** analysis **and** construction practices (resin type, fiber orientation, cure cycle, joint details). ### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Standard Metrics) | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Range for Small Composite Craft | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Rule design pressure (bottom) | $P_{bot}$ | **15 – 35** | kN/m² | | Rule design pressure (side) | $P_{sid}$ | **10 – 25** | kN/m² | | Rule design pressure (deck) | $P_{dec}$ | **8 – 20** | kN/m² | | Frame spacing (transverse) | $s$ | **0.30 – 0.60** | m | | Longitudinal spacing | $\ell$ | **0.40 – 0.80** | m | | Hull girder bending stress | $\sigma_{HG}$ | up to **0.40 × UTS** | MPa | | Plating thickness (single skin) | $t$ | **3 – 12** | mm | | Skin thickness (sandwich) | $t_s$ | **1.5 – 5** | mm | | Core thickness (sandwich) | $t_c$ | **10 – 50** | mm | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hierarchical Levels of Structural Arrangement Classification society rules apply at **five hierarchical levels**, moving from the whole-boat scale down to the connection detail. A KTU examiner expects students to be able to name and explain each: 1. **Hull Girder Level** — longitudinal strength of the entire boat as a hollow beam. 2. **Plate / Panel Level** — local strength of the shell plating between stiffeners. 3. **Stiffener / Frame Level** — strength of individual members (frames, longitudinals, girders). 4. **Sub-Assembly Level** — strength of floors, web frames, transverse rings. 5. **Connection / Joint Level** — overlap joints, cleats, bonded or bolted connections, core-to-skin interfaces. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for exams:** "**H**ull **P**late **S**tiffener **S**ub-Assembly **C**onnection" → **HPSSC**. ### 2.2 The Philosophy of Classification Society Rules The rules encode **four design philosophies**: - **The "First-Ply-Failure" principle** (DNV, LR): scantlings must prevent the first fiber from breaking under combined loading at the rule design load. - **The "Yield" principle** (ABS, BV for naval craft): scantlings must keep primary stresses below the yield/allowable with a prescribed safety factor (typically **2.0 – 3.0** for composites). - **The "Deformation" principle** (DNV sandwich rules): deflections and skin wrinkling must be limited (e.g., $w \le 0.005 \cdot s$). - **The "Proven Design" principle** (all societies): if a successful parent design exists, the new design may be benchmarked against it (sister-craft comparison). ### 2.3 Standard Rule-Format Procedure (How a Scantling is Computed) A classification rule typically computes a scantling through these five steps: 1. **Determine the Rule Design Load ($P$ or $q$):** A function of displacement, speed, operational area (e.g., unrestricted, coastal, sheltered), and panel location (bottom, side, deck). 2. **Determine Geometric Inputs:** Span $s$ (the unsupported distance between stiffeners) and any edge fixity coefficient ($k_f$). 3. **Determine Material Allowables:** Tensile strength $X_t$, compressive strength $X_c$, shear strength $S$, elastic moduli $E_{11}, E_{22}, G_{12}$. 4. **Apply the Rule Equation:** A semi-empirical formula combining the above, often of the form: $$ t = k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P}{\sigma_{allow}}} \cdot C_{corr} $$ where $C_{corr}$ is a product of correction factors (curvature, load combination, material knockdown). 5. **Check All Failure Modes:** For sandwich, check skin wrinkling, core shear, face dimpling, and global bending **in parallel** — the governing thickness is the **largest** of all required values. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following are **generic, rule-faithful forms** that capture the structure of the proprietary equations. Society-specific constants are not reproduced verbatim; instead, they are indicated symbolically (e.g., $k_1, k_2$) for the student to substitute from the rule book in design practice. | # | Design Task | Generic Rule Equation (SI units) | Boundary / Validity | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Single-skin plating thickness (small craft)** | $\displaystyle t \;=\; s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{P}{\sigma_{allow}}}$ | $t \ge 2$ mm; $s$ in m; $P$ in kN/m²; $\sigma_{allow}$ in MPa | | 2 | **Frame section modulus** (transverse) | $\displaystyle Z_{req} \;=\; \dfrac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} \;=\; \dfrac{k_P \cdot P \cdot s \cdot \ell^2}{\sigma_{allow}}$ | $\ell$ = frame span (m); $k_P$ = load factor | | 3 | **Frame moment of inertia** (deflection check) | $\displaystyle I_{req} \;=\; \dfrac{k_{\delta} \cdot P \cdot s \cdot \ell^4}{E \cdot \delta_{lim}}$ | Typically $\delta_{lim} \le \ell / 300$ | | 4 | **Hull girder section modulus** | $\displaystyle Z_{HG} \;=\; \dfrac{M_{sw} + M_{w}}{k \cdot \sigma_{allow}}$ | $M_{sw}$ = still-water bending moment; $M_{w}$ = wave bending moment | | 5 | **Sandwich skin thickness (wrinkling)** | $\displaystyle t_{s,wr} \;=\; \dfrac{s}{c_1} \cdot \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{P \cdot E_c}{E_s \cdot G_c}}$ | $E_c, G_c$ = core moduli; $c_1$ = rule constant | | 6 | **Sandwich core shear** | $\displaystyle t_c \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{max}}{S_{c,allow}}$ | $Q$ = transverse shear flow | | 7 | **Stiffener web shear (no buckling)** | $\displaystyle t_w \;=\; \dfrac{V_{max}}{h_w \cdot \tau_{allow}}$ | Web depth $h_w$, slenderness $\le 60$ | | 8 | **Stiffener flange width (local buckling)** | $\displaystyle b_f \;\le\; c_2 \cdot t_f \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\sigma_y}}$ | $c_2 \approx 12$ for composites | | 9 | **Minimum plating (corrosion/wear allowance)** | $\displaystyle t_{min} \;=\; t_{calc} + t_{allow}$ | $t_{allow} \approx 0.5$ – 1.0 mm | | 10 | **Connection overlap length** | $\displaystyle L_{ov} \;=\; \max\!\left(\;30\,t,\; \dfrac{F}{\tau_{bd} \cdot w}\;\right)$ | $t$ = thinner laminate; $w$ = width; $\tau_{bd}$ = bond shear strength | ### 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Naval Architects** use these rules at the **concept design stage** to size every member of a 12 m patrol boat in a few hours, replacing a weeks-long FEA iteration. - **Boatbuilders** follow the same rules to obtain **insurance cover** — most insurers will not underwrite a boat that does not have valid classification. - **Surveyors** use the rules during construction and at every periodical survey (annual, special, renewal) to verify continued compliance. - **Lawyers and forensic engineers** cite them in **accident and warranty disputes** as the binding standard of care. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Single-Skin Plating Thickness (Plate on Stiff Supports) **Starting premise.** A rectangular FRP plate of thickness $t$ and shorter span $s$ (frame spacing) is supported on four edges (two frames + two longitudinals) and loaded by a uniform pressure $P$ (rule design pressure). We need $t$ such that the maximum stress equals the allowable. **Step 1.** Treating the plate as an orthotropic two-way slab, the maximum bending moment per unit width (short span, clamped edges – a conservative assumption used by most rules) is: $$ m \;=\; k_1 \cdot P \cdot s^2 $$ where $k_1$ is a bending-moment coefficient. For a clamped plate with aspect ratio $\ge 2$, $k_1 \approx 1/12$; simply-supported gives $k_1 \approx 1/8$. Classification rules typically use a **coefficient between** these depending on the edge condition allowed (e.g., bonded vs. mechanically fastened). **Step 2.** For a plate of unit width, the section modulus is: $$ Z \;=\; \frac{1 \cdot t^2}{6} $$ **Step 3.** The maximum bending stress is: $$ \sigma_{max} \;=\; \frac{m}{Z} \;=\; \frac{6 \cdot k_1 \cdot P \cdot s^2}{t^2} $$ **Step 4.** Equating to the allowable: $$ \sigma_{allow} \;=\; \frac{6 \cdot k_1 \cdot P \cdot s^2}{t^2} $$ **Step 5.** Solving for $t$: $$ t^2 \;=\; \frac{6 \cdot k_1 \cdot P \cdot s^2}{\sigma_{allow}} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; t \;=\; s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{6 \cdot k_1 \cdot P}{\sigma_{allow}}} $$ **Step 6.** Collecting all rule-specific constants into a single coefficient $k$ and adding correction factors (curvature, knockdown, partial safety factor $\gamma_m$): $$ t \;=\; k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{P}{\sigma_{allow} / \gamma_m}} \;=\; k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\gamma_m \cdot P}{\sigma_{allow}}} $$ This is the **universal rule form** (cf. Formula #1 of the cheat sheet). ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Type) A composite patrol boat has bottom plating supported by transverse frames at $s = 0.45$ m on centers. The rule design pressure is $P = 22$ kN/m². Material is woven-roving E-glass / polyester laminate with allowable $\sigma_{allow} = 110$ MPa. The rule uses $k = 1.10$ (curved bottom, clamped edges) and a partial safety factor $\gamma_m = 1.30$ on material strength. Compute the required single-skin thickness. **Step 1.** Convert units so that $P$ is in MPa: $$ P \;=\; 22 \text{ kN/m}^2 \;=\; 22 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 2.** Insert into the rule: $$ t \;=\; 1.10 \cdot 0.45 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.30 \cdot 22 \times 10^{-3}}{110}} $$ **Step 3.** Evaluate the bracket: $$ \frac{1.30 \cdot 22 \times 10^{-3}}{110} \;=\; \frac{28.6 \times 10^{-3}}{110} \;=\; 2.6 \times 10^{-4} $$ **Step 4.** Take the square root: $$ \sqrt{2.6 \times 10^{-4}} \;=\; 1.612 \times 10^{-2} $$ **Step 5.** Multiply by the geometric factor: $$ t \;=\; 1.10 \cdot 0.45 \cdot 1.612 \times 10^{-2} $$ $$ t \;=\; 0.4950 \cdot 1.612 \times 10^{-2} $$ $$ t \;\approx\; 7.98 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} $$ **Step 6.** Convert to millimetres and apply minimum: $$ t \;\approx\; 7.98 \text{ mm} \;\;\longrightarrow\;\; \text{Adopt } t = 8.0 \text{ mm} $$ Check minimum (rule minimum is **2.5 mm**): satisfied. **Step 7.** Add wear/corrosion allowance of 0.5 mm (FRP builds usually negligible, but rules impose a minimum): $$ t_{design} \;=\; 8.0 + 0.5 \;=\; 8.5 \text{ mm} $$ **Conclusion:** Bottom single-skin plating is to be **8.5 mm woven roving/polyester laminate**, or equivalent in weight per square metre (≈ 2.0 kg/m² per mm). ### 3.3 Derivation: Hull Girder Section Modulus (Wave + Still-Water Bending) **Step 1.** The still-water bending moment $M_{sw}$ is computed from the weight distribution along the LBP: $$ M_{sw}(x) \;=\; \int_{0}^{x}\!\int_{0}^{\xi} w(\eta)\,d\eta\,d\xi - \int_{0}^{x}\!\int_{0}^{\xi} b(\eta)\,d\eta\,d\xi $$ where $w$ is weight density and $b$ is buoyancy density. **Step 2.** The wave-induced bending moment amplitude (sagging or hogging) for a boat on a regular wave is: $$ M_{w} \;=\; k_{wave} \cdot \Delta \cdot L \cdot C_b $$ where $\Delta$ is displacement, $L$ is LBP, $C_b$ is block coefficient, and $k_{wave}$ is a society-specific coefficient. **Step 3.** Total design moment: $$ M_{total} \;=\; M_{sw} + M_{w} $$ **Step 4.** Section modulus required (allowable combined): $$ Z_{HG} \;=\; \frac{M_{total}}{k_{HG} \cdot \sigma_{allow}} $$ **Step 5.** In a composite boat, the hull girder is a "top-hat" or sandwich section. The engineer must verify the **effective** section modulus: $$ Z_{eff} \;=\; \frac{I_{eff}}{y_{max}} \;\;\ge\;\; Z_{HG} $$ where $I_{eff}$ is the second moment of area of the assembled shell about the neutral axis, computed by parallel-axis theorem over each contributing panel: $$ I_{eff} \;=\; \sum_{i}\, \left( I_{i,0} + A_i \cdot d_i^2 \right) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** The hull girder check is the "boss" check — if the rule is satisfied at this level, the *global* failure mode is prevented. The smaller, *local* checks (plate, frame, stiffener) protect against *local* failure. This hierarchical redundancy is what makes classification design robust. ### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Structural Arrangement to Choose? | Boat Type | Length | Speed | Recommended Arrangement | Rule Bias | |---|---|---|---|---| | Inflatable / RIB | < 6 m | < 40 kn | Single-skin hypalon/neoprene, no internal frames | DNV Pt 3 Ch 4 | | Small fishing FRP | 6 – 12 m | < 25 kn | Single-skin hull, transverse frames @ 300 mm, floors every 2nd frame | LR SSC Pt 4 | | Patrol / Naval composite | 12 – 30 m | 25 – 45 kn | Sandwich (PVC/AI core) hull, longitudinal + transverse hybrid framing | DNV Pt 3 Ch 3 | | High-speed planing craft | 8 – 24 m | > 40 kn | Sandwich bottom, single-skin topsides, deep girders | ABS HSC Pt 3 | | Luxury yacht | 20 – 50 m | 12 – 18 kn | Single-skin or sandwich, longitudinal framing, watertight bulkheads @ ≤ 4 m | LR Yacht | | Warship (composite superstructure) | > 50 m | 25 kn | Hybrid: steel hull + composite superstructure on rule-isolated joint | BV Pt B | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Class-Rule Structural Arrangement The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** between the naval architect, the classification society, the boatbuilder, and the surveyor, as a system of interacting modules. It is the recommended block diagram for answering the question *"How does a classification society's rule book govern the structural arrangement of a composite boat?"* in KTU exams. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[NAVAL ARCHITECT
Concept Design] --> B[RULE BOOK
Selection of Society] B --> C[LOAD DERIVATION
Rule design pressure P] C --> D[MATERIAL SELECTION
FRP / Sandwich Core] D --> E[SCANTLING COMPUTATION
Plating, Frame, Girder] E --> F[ARRANGEMENT DRAWING
Frame spacing, Bulkhead layout] F --> G[CONNECTION DETAILING
Overlap, Cleat, Bonded] G --> H[CLASS SUBMISSION
Approved Plans] H --> I[SOCIETY APPROVAL
Class Certificate Issued] I --> J[CONSTRUCTION PHASE
Boatbuilder executes] J --> K[SURVEY DURING BUILD
Society Surveyor attends] K --> L[MATERIAL TESTING
Laminate coupon, resin content] L --> M[FINAL TRIAL
Inclining, Speed, Stability] M --> N[CLASS MAINTAINED
Periodical Survey every 5 yr] N --> O[IN-SERVICE COMPLIANCE
Repair under Class Scrutiny] O --> P[DESIGN LOOP BACK
Lessons fed to Architect] P -.-> A ``` ### 4.2 Transverse Section Arrangement — Typical Composite Planing Craft ```mermaid flowchart LR A[KEEL
Bottom Plate 8.5 mm] --> B[BOTTOM FRAME
T-section 80x40x6] B --> C[CHINE GIRDER
L-section 100x60x8] C --> D[SIDE PLATING
Sandwich 4 mm skin + 25 mm AI core] D --> E[SIDE FRAME
T-section 60x30x5] E --> F[SHELF / STRINGER
Angle 60x60x5] F --> G[DECK PLATE
Sandwich 3 mm + 20 mm core] G --> H[DECK BEAM
T-section 70x35x5] H --> I[STIFFENER FLANGE
Connection to deck plate] I --> J[BULKHEAD
Watertight 4 mm FRP] J --> A ``` ### 4.3 Longitudinal Framing vs Transverse Framing — Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Boat Type and Length] --> Q1{Is L greater than 25 m?} Q1 -- No --> Q2{Speed less than 25 kn?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q3{High-speed planning craft?} Q2 -- Yes --> A1[TRANSVERSE FRAMING
Frames every 300-450 mm
Floors every 2nd frame
Single-skin plating] Q2 -- No --> A2[HYBRID FRAMING
Transverse frames +
deck longitudinals
Bottom single-skin] Q3 -- Yes --> A3[SANDWICH + LONGITUDINAL
Longitudinals @ 200-300 mm
Sandwich bottom and deck
Heavy floors at 0.4L and 0.6L] Q3 -- No --> A4[HYBRID WITH GIRDERS
Side longitudinals +
transverse web frames
at 2.0 m spacing] ``` ### 4.4 Connection Detail Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD J[CONNECTION TYPE] --> J1[OVERLAP JOINT
Used for plating to frame] J --> J2[CLEAT JOINT
Used for bulkhead to hull] J --> J3[BONDED FLANGE
Used in sandwich panels] J --> J4[RESIN-INJECTED
Used for field assembly] J1 --> K1[CHECK Shear in overlap
L over 30t] J2 --> K2[CHECK Cleat shear
and peel stress] J3 --> K3[CHECK Skin wrinkling
at flange root] J4 --> K4[CHECK Bondline
voids and cure] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]** *Define a "Classification Society" and list THREE major classification societies whose rules apply to composite boat design.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Classification Society** is an independent, non-governmental, IACS-affiliated organization that sets technical standards for the design, construction, and survey of marine vessels and offshore structures. For composite boats, the major societies are: (1) **DNV** (Det Norske Veritas), (2) **Lloyd's Register (LR)**, and (3) **American Bureau of Shipping (ABS)**. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for any two societies named correctly, 1 mark for third.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *State the generic rule-equation for required single-skin plating thickness of a composite hull, defining each symbol.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The required single-skin plating thickness is computed as: $$ t \;=\; k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\gamma_m \cdot P}{\sigma_{allow}}} $$ where $t$ = required thickness (m), $k$ = combined rule coefficient (curvature, edge fixity), $s$ = unsupported frame spacing (m), $\gamma_m$ = partial safety factor on material, $P$ = rule design pressure (MPa), and $\sigma_{allow}$ = allowable laminate stress (MPa). *(1 mark for equation, 1 mark for symbols, 1 mark for units/validity range.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice means the student answers EITHER Q(A) OR Q(B) — not both. Both alternatives below are at the same cognitive level (Apply / Analyse per Bloom's taxonomy) and are equally weighted. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Model – Module 4 Choice Option 1]** **(a)** With the aid of a labelled sketch, describe the **five hierarchical levels** at which a classification society's rules govern the structural arrangement of a composite boat. State the typical failure mode that each level is designed to prevent. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A composite patrol boat of length 18 m has a bottom plate supported on transverse frames at $s = 0.40$ m centres. The rule design pressure is $P = 25$ kN/m². The laminate is woven roving / vinylester with allowable $\sigma_{allow} = 120$ MPa. The rule uses $k = 1.15$ (curved, clamped) and partial safety factor $\gamma_m = 1.25$. Compute the required single-skin thickness and the minimum frame section modulus if the frame span is $\ell = 2.5$ m and the load factor $k_P = 0.125$ (per rule bending-moment coefficient for clamped-clamped beam under uniform pressure). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) — 7 marks** | Level | Description | Failure Mode Prevented | |---|---|---| | 1. Hull Girder | Whole-boat strength as a hollow beam | Global collapse, hull break | | 2. Plate / Panel | Shell plating between stiffeners | Plate buckling, cracking | | 3. Stiffener / Frame | Individual members (frames, stringers) | Frame yielding, buckling | | 4. Sub-Assembly | Floors, web frames, transverse rings | Ring collapse, panel shear | | 5. Connection | Joints (overlap, cleat, bonded) | Joint peel, bond failure | *(1 mark per level with description + 2 marks for the failure-mode column summary.)* **(b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Convert pressure: $$ P \;=\; 25 \text{ kN/m}^2 \;=\; 25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply thickness formula: $$ t \;=\; 1.15 \cdot 0.40 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1.25 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-3}}{120}} $$ $$ t \;=\; 0.46 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{31.25 \times 10^{-3}}{120}} $$ $$ t \;=\; 0.46 \cdot \sqrt{2.604 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ t \;=\; 0.46 \cdot 1.614 \times 10^{-2} \;\approx\; 7.42 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} $$ $$ \boxed{\;t \;\approx\; 7.5 \text{ mm}\;} $$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Bending moment per frame: $$ M_{max} \;=\; k_P \cdot P \cdot s \cdot \ell^2 $$ $$ M_{max} \;=\; 0.125 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.40 \cdot (2.5)^2 $$ $$ M_{max} \;=\; 0.125 \cdot 25 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.40 \cdot 6.25 $$ $$ M_{max} \;\approx\; 7.81 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MPa·m}^2 \cdot \text{m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 7.81 \text{ kN·m/m} $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Required section modulus: $$ Z_{req} \;=\; \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} \;=\; \frac{7.81 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MPa·m}^2 / \text{m}}{120 \text{ MPa}} $$ $$ Z_{req} \;=\; 6.51 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{m} $$ $$ \boxed{\;Z_{req} \;\approx\; 65.1 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}\;} $$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Select a frame with $Z_{provided} \ge Z_{req}$. For example, a T-section of web 80×6 with flange 50×6 provides $Z \approx 23.4$ cm³ per frame. With one frame every 0.40 m, this is 58.5 cm³/m — *insufficient*. Increase to T-section web 100×8 with flange 60×8: $Z \approx 28.6$ cm³ per frame → 71.5 cm³/m — *satisfactory*. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU ESE Model – Module 4 Choice Option 2]** **(a)** Compare and contrast **transverse framing** and **longitudinal framing** systems for a composite boat of length 15 m and speed 30 knots. State, with justification, the system you would recommend and identify the classification society rule that supports your choice. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the same boat, the bottom is built as a sandwich panel with E-glass / vinylester skins of thickness $t_s$ and PVC foam core of thickness $t_c = 30$ mm and shear modulus $G_c = 40$ MPa. The unsupported skin span between longitudinal stiffeners is $s = 0.30$ m and the rule design pressure is $P = 30$ kN/m². The skin modulus is $E_s = 18$ GPa. Using the wrinkling formula $$ t_{s,wr} \;=\; \frac{s}{c_1} \cdot \sqrt[3]{\dfrac{P \cdot E_c}{E_s \cdot G_c}} $$ with $c_1 = 15$, compute the minimum skin thickness required. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) — 7 marks (Rubric)** | Criterion | Transverse | Longitudinal | Marks | |---|---|---|---| | Member direction | Frames vertical, hull-strong | Stringers horizontal, deck/bottom-strong | 1 | | Optimum for | Slow, heavy craft (fishing, tugs) | Fast, slender craft (patrol, naval) | 1 | | Scantling efficiency | Lower (many short members) | Higher (continuous lines) | 1 | | Build complexity | Simpler; fewer moulded parts | More complex; precision alignment | 1 | | Damage tolerance | Localized (one frame fails) | Distributes (load redistributes) | 1 | | Recommendation | Use **hybrid** for 15 m / 30 kn — transverse frames + bottom longitudinals (L ≤ 0.30 m) for slamming resistance | | 1 | | Society rule | **DNV Pt 3 Ch 3 Sec 4** specifies minimum longitudinal spacing for slamming panels in craft with $F_n > 1.5$ | | 1 | **(b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 (1 mark):** State given values. Note: $E_c$ for PVC foam at 75 kg/m³ density is typically $E_c \approx 75$ MPa. **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the cube-root argument: $$ \frac{P \cdot E_c}{E_s \cdot G_c} \;=\; \frac{30 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 75}{18 \times 10^{3} \cdot 40} $$ $$ =\; \frac{2.25}{720 \times 10^{3}} \;=\; 3.125 \times 10^{-6} $$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Take cube root: $$ \sqrt[3]{3.125 \times 10^{-6}} \;\approx\; 1.464 \times 10^{-2} $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Apply formula: $$ t_{s,wr} \;=\; \frac{0.30}{15} \cdot 1.464 \times 10^{-2} $$ $$ =\; 0.02 \cdot 1.464 \times 10^{-2} $$ $$ \approx\; 2.93 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} $$ **Step 5 (mark for conclusion):** $$ \boxed{\;t_{s,wr} \;\approx\; 0.30 \text{ mm}\;} $$ This is the **wrinkling**-governed value. The designer must also check face-dimpling, core shear, and global bending of the sandwich, and adopt the **largest** thickness. In practice, fabrication constraints (e.g., minimum 2 plies of 600 gsm cloth ⇒ ~1.5 mm) dominate, so the rule value is a lower bound, not the final adopted thickness. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on this topic — read carefully:** > 1. **Unit-mismatch trap:** The most common error is mixing $P$ in kN/m² with $\sigma$ in MPa without converting. Always reduce $P$ to MPa ($1$ kN/m² = $10^{-3}$ MPa) before substituting. > 2. **Symbol confusion between $s$ and $\ell$:** $s$ = short span (stiffener spacing) used in plate check; $\ell$ = frame span (between supports) used in frame check. **Do NOT swap them.** > 3. **Forgetting the partial safety factor $\gamma_m$:** Many students apply $t = k s \sqrt{P/\sigma}$ without the material knockdown, giving a 15–25 % unsafe answer. > 4. **Reporting only the *governing* mode:** In sandwich design, the student must state which failure mode **governs** (wrinkling, dimpling, core shear, or face yielding). A thickness without a governing-mode statement is incomplete. > 5. **Omitting minimum scantlings:** All rules impose a **minimum** $t$ (usually 2.5 – 4 mm for FRP) below which a calculated $t$ cannot be reduced. Always state the minimum check. > 6. **Confusing "Rule" with "Law":** Class rules are not statutes. A boat can be built to a different standard, but it cannot be **classed** without satisfying the rule book. Examiners often test this distinction. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Five hierarchical levels** of structural arrangement: Hull Girder → Plate → Stiffener → Sub-Assembly → Connection. *(Mnemonic: HPSSC.)* - **Three philosophies:** First-ply-failure (DNV/LR), Yield (ABS/BV), Deformation (sandwich), and Proven Design (sister-craft comparison). - **Universal rule equation:** $t = k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\gamma_m P / \sigma_{allow}}$. Know the units, symbols, and partial-safety factor placement. - **Frame section modulus:** $Z_{req} = k_P P s \ell^2 / \sigma_{allow}$. **Distinguish** $s$ (plate span) from $\ell$ (frame span). - **Hull girder check:** $Z_{HG} = (M_{sw} + M_w) / (k \cdot \sigma_{allow})$. - **Sandwich design:** check **four** failure modes in parallel — skin wrinkling, face dimpling, core shear, and global bending. Adopt the largest $t_s$ and $t_c$. - **Connection overlap length:** $L_{ov} = \max(30 t,\; F/(\tau_{bd} w))$. - **Minimum scantlings:** always check $t \ge t_{min,rule}$ after computing $t_{calc}$. - **Major classification societies for composites:** DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, Bureau Veritas, IRS, RINA. Know the **full names** and **scope** of at least three. - **Why composite rules differ from steel rules:** composites are anisotropic and viscoelastic → rules prescribe both **analysis** and **fabrication practice** (resin type, cure schedule, layup sequence). - **Common framing choices:** transverse for slow craft, longitudinal for fast/slamming craft, hybrid for general purpose — a **decision tree** must be used to justify the selection. - **Exam mantra:** "*Always state the failure mode that governs, the minimum scantling check, and the unit consistency.*"

topics.title: Determination of Boat Shell Lamination Using Classification Society Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PENST742 - COMPOSITE BOAT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Determination of Boat Shell Lamination Using Classification Society Rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Boat Shell Lamination** refers to the engineered stacking sequence (layup schedule) of fiber-reinforced composite plies that constitute the outer skin (hull, deck, superstructure) of a composite boat. Under **Classification Society Rules** (ISO 12215, DNV, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, RINA, ABS), the determination of this lamination is a codified scantling procedure in which the minimum required mechanical thickness, ply count, material grade, and core configuration are computed from design pressure, panel geometry, material allowable strains, and safety/knockdown factors.

The governing reference for small craft (< 24 m) is **ISO 12215-5:2019** — *Small craft — Hull construction and scantlings — Part 5: Design pressures for monohulls, design stresses, scantling determination*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For PENST742 (Composite Boat Design), the calculation of laminate thickness, ply schedule, and core requirements using classification society equations (with primary focus on ISO 12215-5) is a high-weightage topic in Module 4 and is regularly tested in Part B questions.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a **thermocol (EPS) sandwich** to protect a fragile glass inside a shipping box. The outer paper is too weak, so you glue two sheets of stiff cardboard on either side. The thermocol in the middle does not carry much load — its job is to keep the two stiff skins apart so the structure cannot easily bend. A **composite boat shell is exactly this sandwich**:

- **The skins (outer + inner laminates)** are the stiff cardboard — they carry the bending loads from water pressure and wave slamming.
- **The core (foam / balsa / honeycomb)** is the thermocol — it resists shear and prevents the skins from buckling, but it carries almost no bending load.

The **classification society rules** are like a *recipe book* the shipyard must follow: given the boat's size, speed, and intended use, the rulebook tells you exactly how thick the cardboard must be, how many layers of "glue + fiber" you must stack, and how much thermocol to inject in the middle — so the sandwich never crushes at sea.

### Key Standard Metrics

- **Design Category:** A (Ocean), B (Offshore), C (Coastal), D (Sheltered) — higher category ⇒ higher pressure.
- **Material Factor ($k$):** Safety multiplier on allowable strain (typically **$k = 1.0$ to $1.5$** for polyester, **$k = 0.7$ to $1.0$** for vinylester/epoxy).
- **Knockdown Factor ($\eta$):** Property reduction factor for fatigue, environment, manufacturing (typically **$\eta = 0.5$ to $0.75$**).
- **Wave Slamming Acceleration ($a_s$):** Peak vertical acceleration at bow in m/s², often **$a_s = 4$ to $15$ m/s²**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core takeaway:** The classification society's job is *not* to optimize the laminate — it is to define the **minimum safe laminate** below which the surveyor will not certify the vessel for sea trials.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The determination of boat shell lamination under classification society rules follows this **structured pipeline**:

1. **Step 1 — Define Design Parameters**
   Establish the principal dimensions: $L_H$ (hull length), $B$ (beam), $T$ (draft), $V$ (design speed), $m_{LDC}$ (Lightship Displacement Coefficient), $GC$ (Gravity Center), service category.

2. **Step 2 — Compute Design Pressure ($p_d$)**
   The design pressure is the maximum hydrodynamic pressure a panel must withstand. ISO 12215 splits this into:
   - **Static (Still-Water) Pressure:** $p_s$ — from buoyancy and hydrostatics on the bottom panel.
   - **Dynamic / Wave-Slamming Pressure:** $p_d$ — from impacts in head seas.

3. **Step 3 — Determine Panel Geometry**
   For each panel, identify $l_1$ (long stiffener spacing) and $l_2$ (short stiffener spacing). The aspect ratio $l_2/l_1$ governs whether the panel behaves as a one-way or two-way slab.

4. **Step 4 — Apply Scantling Equation**
   The minimum required **equivalent single-skin thickness** $t_{req}$ (or **minimum bending stiffness** for sandwich) is computed from the panel as a simply supported plate under uniform pressure.

5. **Step 5 — Translate to Laminate Schedule**
   Convert $t_{req}$ into:
   - **Number of plies** $n = t_{req} / t_{ply}$
   - **Stack sequence** (e.g., [0/90/0/90/CSM])
   - **Core thickness** $t_c$ for sandwich construction
   - **Skin-to-core bond strength** check

6. **Step 6 — Apply Knockdown & Safety Factors**
   Divide bare material properties by $\eta$ and apply material factor $k$ on allowable strain. Re-check minimums.

7. **Step 7 — Verifier Sign-off**
   Classification society surveyor checks laminate schedule, fiber areal weight, resin content, and cure quality against approved **"Laminate Booklet"**.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (ISO 12215-5)

| **Parameter** | **Formula / Symbol** | **Unit** | **Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Design Pressure (dynamic, slamming) | $p_d = 0.55 \cdot a_s \cdot m_{LDC} \cdot (L_H + B)$ converted via empirical graph | kPa | ISO 12215-5, §6.4 |
| Static Pressure on bottom | $p_s = \rho g h_{max}$ (where $h_{max}$ is max submergence) | kPa | Hydrostatic |
| Minimum Skin Thickness (single skin) | $t_{req} = b \cdot k_C \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{p_d \cdot l^2 \cdot k}{1000 \cdot \sigma_{allow}}}$ | mm | $b$ = panel factor, $k_C$ = curvature correction |
| Equivalent Panel Stiffness (sandwich) | $D = \dfrac{E_f \cdot t_f \cdot d^2}{2}$ | N·mm | $d$ = distance between skin mid-planes |
| Required Skin Thickness from Stiffness | $t_f = \dfrac{l \cdot p_d \cdot k}{d \cdot \sigma_{allow}}$ | mm | Sandwich bending criterion |
| Core Shear Stress | $\tau_c = \dfrac{p_d \cdot l}{2 d}$ | MPa | Must be $< \tau_{c,allow}$ |
| Skin Buckling Stress | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{0.9 \cdot E_f \cdot (t_f / l)^2}{1 - \nu^2}$ | MPa | Euler-type plate buckling |
| Material Knockdown Factor | $\eta = \eta_{env} \cdot \eta_{fab} \cdot \eta_{temp}$ | — | Multiplying dimensionless |
| Allowable Strain | $\varepsilon_{allow} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ult}}{k \cdot S_F}$ | — | $S_F$ = safety factor |
| Ply Count | $n = \lceil t_{req} / t_{ply} \rceil$ | plies | Round up to next integer |
| Stiffener Spacing Limit | $l_1 \leq 2 \cdot l_2$ | mm | Two-way panel check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** These equations are not academic exercises — every GRP production boat (Boston Whaler, Lagoon catamarans, Beneteau, Sunseeker) and every racing yacht (TP52, IMOCA 60) carries a **Laminate Booklet** whose every line is justified by these formulas. Failure to satisfy them voids the **Hull Construction Certificate (HCC)** and the insurance policy simultaneously.

### Real-World Application

In an **IMOCA 60 racing yacht**, the classification rules (RINA / DNV) drive the deck laminate up to **20+ plies of carbon prepreg over a 25 mm Nomex honeycomb core**, while in a **5 m GRP dinghy** the same rules produce a modest **6 mm solid laminate** of chopped-strand mat + woven roving. The same equation, two orders of magnitude of difference — entirely driven by design pressure and panel size.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Minimum Skin Thickness of an Orthotropic Single-Skin Panel

We model the boat shell panel as a **flat rectangular orthotropic plate**, simply supported on all four edges, of side lengths $l_1 \geq l_2$, under uniform pressure $p_d$.

**Starting Point — Classical Plate Equation:**

The required flexural rigidity per unit width is:

$$
D_{req} = \frac{C \cdot p_d \cdot l_2^4}{E \cdot \delta_{allow}}
$$

where $C$ is a dimensionless coefficient depending on aspect ratio $l_1/l_2$, and $\delta_{allow}$ is the maximum permissible deflection.

**Step 1:** Substitute flexural rigidity for a homogeneous plate,

$$
D = \frac{E \cdot t^3}{12 \cdot (1 - \nu^2)}
$$

**Step 2:** Equate and solve for the cubic thickness,

$$
t^3 = \frac{12 \cdot (1 - \nu^2) \cdot C \cdot p_d \cdot l_2^4}{E \cdot \delta_{allow}}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply knockdown factor $\eta$ and material factor $k$,

$$
t_{req} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{12 \cdot (1 - \nu^2) \cdot C \cdot p_d \cdot l_2^4 \cdot k}{\eta \cdot E \cdot \delta_{allow}}}
$$

This is the **fundamental cubic thickness equation** in ISO 12215-5 §8.

---

### Derivation 2: Slamming Pressure from Wave-Slamming Acceleration

Vertical acceleration at the bow during wave slamming is empirically given by:

$$
a_s = \left(\frac{L_H}{10} + 1.5 \cdot V\right) \cdot c_{cat}
$$

where $c_{cat}$ is the design-category coefficient (A: 1.0, B: 0.9, C: 0.7, D: 0.5).

**Step 1:** Convert acceleration to equivalent dynamic pressure using the *added-mass* method,

$$
p_d = a_s \cdot m_{LDC} \cdot A_{panel}
$$

**Step 2:** For an infinite-strip simplification (long panel in the slamming zone):

$$
p_d = 0.6 \cdot a_s \cdot m_{LDC} \cdot \frac{B}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Combine with hydrostatic component,

$$
p_{total} = p_s + p_d
$$

---

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU-style):** A coastal-category (Category C) motor boat has $L_H = 9$ m, $B = 3$ m, $V = 18$ knots, and bottom panel dimensions $l_1 = 600$ mm, $l_2 = 400$ mm. Laminate: E-glass / polyester, $E = 18$ GPa, $\sigma_{allow} = 90$ MPa, $k = 1.0$, $\eta = 0.6$. Determine the minimum required single-skin thickness.

**Step 1 — Wave-Slamming Acceleration:**

$$
a_s = \left(\frac{9}{10} + 1.5 \cdot 18 \cdot 0.5144\right) \cdot 0.7
$$

Note: 18 knots $= 9.26$ m/s, so $1.5V = 13.9$ m/s.

$$
a_s = (0.9 + 13.9) \cdot 0.7 = 10.36 \ \text{m/s}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Design Pressure (ISO 12215-5, simplified formula):**

$$
p_d = 0.55 \cdot a_s \cdot m_{LDC} \cdot \frac{L_H + B}{10}
$$

With $m_{LDC} = 1.4$ (typical planing hull):

$$
p_d = 0.55 \cdot 10.36 \cdot 1.4 \cdot \frac{9 + 3}{10} = 0.55 \cdot 10.36 \cdot 1.4 \cdot 1.2 = 9.57 \ \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Required Thickness (Strength Criterion):**

ISO 12215 single-skin panel strength formula:

$$
t_{req} = b \cdot k_C \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p_d \cdot l_1^2 \cdot k}{1000 \cdot \sigma_{allow}}}
$$

With panel factor $b = 0.5$ (typical two-way panel), $k_C = 1.0$ (flat panel):

$$
t_{req} = 0.5 \cdot 1.0 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{9.57 \cdot 600^2 \cdot 1.0}{1000 \cdot 90}}
$$

$$
t_{req} = 0.5 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{9.57 \cdot 360000}{90000}} = 0.5 \cdot \sqrt{38.28} = 0.5 \cdot 6.19 = 3.09 \ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Knockdown Factor (deflection / fatigue check):**

The deflection criterion yields a higher value:

$$
t_{defl} = l_2 \cdot \sqrt[3]{\frac{p_d \cdot k}{0.045 \cdot E \cdot \eta}} = 400 \cdot \sqrt[3]{\frac{9.57 \cdot 1.0}{0.045 \cdot 18000 \cdot 0.6}}
$$

$$
t_{defl} = 400 \cdot \sqrt[3]{\frac{9.57}{486}} = 400 \cdot \sqrt[3]{0.0197} = 400 \cdot 0.27 = 108 \ \text{mm}
$$

This is the minimum thickness to limit deflection — clearly a sandwich panel is required.

**Step 5 — Final Selection:**

$$
t_{final} = \max(t_{req}, t_{defl,min}) = 3.09 \ \text{mm} \quad (\text{but with lamination schedule})
$$

A practical laminate schedule: **[CSM 450 / Woven Roving 800 / Woven Roving 800 / CSM 450]** giving a cured thickness of approximately **3.2 mm** at 35% glass content, satisfying the rule.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The student must show all five steps in a KTU exam; skipping the knockdown factor and only quoting $t_{req}$ loses 2 of the 7 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Lamination Determination Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Principal Dimensions L_H, B, T, V, Category] --> B[Step 1: Compute m_LDC and GC]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Slamming Acceleration a_s]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute Design Pressure p_d]
    D --> E[Step 4: Identify Panel Geometry l1 and l2]
    E --> F{Panel Construction}
    F -->|Single Skin| G[Step 5a: Apply Cubic Thickness Equation t_req]
    F -->|Sandwich| H[Step 5b: Apply Sandwich Stiffness and Core Shear Check]
    G --> I[Step 6: Apply Knockdown Factor eta]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Step 7: Convert t_req to Ply Schedule]
    J --> K[Step 8: Verify Deflection and Local Buckling]
    K --> L[Step 9: Generate Laminate Booklet]
    L --> M[Step 10: Submit to Classification Society for Approval]
    M --> N[Output: Hull Construction Certificate HCC]
```

### Diagram 2: Cross-Section of a Typical Sandwich Hull Laminate

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph OuterSkin["OUTER SKIN (3 to 5 mm)"]
        L1[Layer 1: Gelcoat 0.5 mm] --> L2[Layer 2: CSM 450]
        L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Woven Roving 800]
        L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Woven Roving 800]
        L4 --> L5[Layer 5: CSM 450]
    end
    subgraph Core["CORE (15 to 40 mm)"]
        L5 --> CORE[PVC Foam t_c = 25 mm]
    end
    subgraph InnerSkin["INNER SKIN (2 to 4 mm)"]
        CORE --> L6[Layer 6: CSM 450]
        L6 --> L7[Layer 7: Woven Roving 800]
        L7 --> L8[Layer 8: CSM 450]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two outer/inner **Skin** regions carry bending; the **Core** carries transverse shear. A 50% increase in core thickness doubles the panel stiffness — a 50% increase in skin thickness increases stiffness by only ~12.5%. This is why **ISO 12215 prefers sandwich construction** for any panel wider than ~400 mm.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Q: Define "knockdown factor" as used in classification society lamination rules. List any two causes that necessitate its use.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **knockdown factor $\eta$** is a dimensionless multiplier (typically $0.5$ to $0.75$) applied to the nominal mechanical properties of a composite laminate to account for property degradation between the as-tested coupon and the as-built structure.

Two causes:

1. **Environmental degradation:** Long-term water absorption, UV exposure, and temperature cycling reduce strength and stiffness of polyester and vinylester resins by 20–40%.
2. **Manufacturing variability:** Void content, fibre misalignment, cure cycle deviations, and resin-rich or resin-starved zones lower the in-service property by 15–30% compared to a laboratory-cured specimen.

*(1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 2 = 1 mark for each cause + ½ mark for standard value.)*

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Distinguish between "single-skin" and "sandwich" laminates in boat construction. State one rule-of-thumb criterion for choosing between them.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Single Skin** | **Sandwich** |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Homogeneous stack of plies | Two stiff skins separated by a lightweight core |
| Load path | Bending carried by the entire thickness | Bending carried by the skins; core carries shear |
| Weight | Heavier for a given stiffness | Much lighter (30–50% weight saving) |
| Cost | Cheaper, simpler tooling | Higher cost, more QA-critical |
| Failure mode | Tensile/compressive rupture | Skin wrinkling, core shear, debonding |

**Rule of thumb:** Use sandwich construction when the **unsupported panel span exceeds ~400 mm** *or* when the calculated single-skin thickness exceeds **6 mm**.

*(1 mark each for two correct distinctions + 1 mark for rule of thumb.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **omit the knockdown factor** $\eta$ — losing up to **2 marks**.
> 2. Students confuse **$p_d$ (dynamic, slamming)** with **$p_s$ (static, hydrostatic)** — both must be considered; mark deducted if one is missing.
> 3. Final answer must be rounded **UP** to the next integer ply (e.g., $3.1$ plies $\rightarrow$ **4 plies**). Writing $3.1$ plies loses 1 mark.
> 4. Always state **units** at every numerical substitution; $9.57$ kPa ≠ $9.57$ N/mm².

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q (a)** With neat sketches, explain the **step-by-step procedure** for determining the laminate schedule of a composite boat hull bottom panel using ISO 12215-5 classification rules. Clearly state the role of (i) design pressure, (ii) material factor, and (iii) knockdown factor. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** A 12 m Category B motor boat has a bottom panel of $l_1 = 700$ mm and $l_2 = 500$ mm, designed for $V = 22$ knots, with laminate properties: $E = 20$ GPa, $\sigma_{allow} = 100$ MPa, $k = 1.0$, $\eta = 0.65$, $m_{LDC} = 1.5$. Determine: **(i)** slamming pressure $p_d$, **(ii)** minimum single-skin thickness $t_{req}$ using ISO panel strength formula, and **(iii)** the corresponding ply schedule for a 0.6 mm-thick biaxial $\pm 45°$ E-glass fabric. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure & Roles (7 marks):**

The procedure is as per the seven-step pipeline in Section 2 of these notes. For full marks the student must produce:

- **Sketch** of a single-skin and a sandwich panel cross-section (1 mark).
- Stepwise listing of seven logical steps (1 mark each = up to 5 marks; minimum 4 to qualify for 5).
- **Roles:** (i) design pressure $p_d$ is the equivalent hydrostatic + slamming pressure used as the input load (1 mark), (ii) material factor $k$ scales the allowable strain to reflect the choice of resin system (1 mark), (iii) knockdown factor $\eta$ reduces nominal properties to account for in-service degradation (1 mark) — but be careful, total is capped at 7.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Slamming acceleration** (1 mark):

$$
a_s = \left(\frac{12}{10} + 1.5 \cdot 22 \cdot 0.5144\right) \cdot 0.9 = (1.2 + 16.97) \cdot 0.9 = 16.35 \ \text{m/s}^2
$$

**Slamming pressure** (1 mark):

$$
p_d = 0.55 \cdot 16.35 \cdot 1.5 \cdot \frac{12 + 3}{10} = 20.22 \ \text{kPa}
$$

**(ii) Minimum skin thickness** (2 marks):

$$
t_{req} = 0.5 \cdot 1.0 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{20.22 \cdot 700^2 \cdot 1.0}{1000 \cdot 100}} = 0.5 \cdot \sqrt{99.08} = 0.5 \cdot 9.95 = 4.98 \ \text{mm}
$$

**(iii) Ply schedule** (3 marks):

$$
n = \left\lceil \frac{4.98}{0.6} \right\rceil = \lceil 8.3 \rceil = 9 \ \text{plies}
$$

A valid layup: **[CSM 300 / Biax ±45 / Biax ±45 / Biax ±45 / Biax ±45 / Biax ±45 / Biax ±45 / Biax ±45 / Biax ±45 / CSM 300]** — total cured thickness $\approx 9 \cdot 0.6 + 0.6 = 6.0$ mm at the centroid line + 0.6 mm CSM each side = $\approx$ **6.6 mm**, satisfying the requirement with a safety reserve.

> [!NOTE]
> Always cross-check with **deflection criterion** in the actual exam; for a 12 m motor boat at this thickness, deflection usually governs and pushes you into a sandwich laminate.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**Q (a)** A racing yacht hull bottom panel of dimensions $l_1 = 500$ mm, $l_2 = 350$ mm is constructed as a sandwich laminate with **25 mm PVC foam core** ($\tau_{c,allow} = 0.6$ MPa, $G_c = 40$ MPa) and **carbon/epoxy skins** ($E_f = 110$ GPa, $\sigma_{allow} = 350$ MPa). The design pressure is $p_d = 30$ kPa. Determine the **minimum skin thickness** for both strength and core-shear criteria, applying $k = 0.85$ and $\eta = 0.7$. **(7 marks)**

**Q (b)** Explain the **concept of laminate booklet** as required by classification societies. List any **four key items** it must contain. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sandwich skin thickness (7 marks):**

**Criterion 1 — Strength (skin bending stress):** (2 marks)

$$
\sigma_{allow,eff} = \frac{\sigma_{allow} \cdot \eta}{k} = \frac{350 \cdot 0.7}{0.85} = 288 \ \text{MPa}
$$

Distance between skin mid-planes $d = t_c + t_f \approx 25$ mm (assuming $t_f$ small).

$$
t_{f,strength} = \frac{p_d \cdot l_1^2}{2 \cdot d \cdot \sigma_{allow,eff}} = \frac{0.030 \cdot 500^2}{2 \cdot 25 \cdot 288} = \frac{7500}{14400} = 0.52 \ \text{mm}
$$

**Criterion 2 — Core Shear:** (2 marks)

$$
\tau_c = \frac{p_d \cdot l_1}{2 \cdot d} = \frac{0.030 \cdot 500}{2 \cdot 25} = 0.30 \ \text{MPa}
$$

$$
\tau_{c,allow,eff} = 0.6 \cdot 0.7 / 0.85 = 0.494 \ \text{MPa} > 0.30 \ \text{MPa} \quad \text{OK}
$$

**Criterion 3 — Skin Buckling (Euler-type plate buckling):** (2 marks)

$$
\sigma_{b,allow} = \frac{0.9 \cdot E_f \cdot (t_f / l_2)^2}{1 - \nu^2}
$$

Setting $\sigma_{b,allow} = \sigma_{allow,eff}$ and solving for $t_f$:

$$
t_{f,buckling} = l_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{allow,eff} \cdot (1 - \nu^2)}{0.9 \cdot E_f}}
$$

$$
t_{f,buckling} = 350 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{288 \cdot 0.91}{0.9 \cdot 110000}} = 350 \cdot \sqrt{0.00265} = 350 \cdot 0.0515 = 18.0 \ \text{mm}
$$

**Final selection:** $t_f = \max(0.52, 18.0) = 18.0$ mm — but this is impractical. The conclusion is that **the deflection criterion will dominate and the design is infeasible** at this span — the stiffener spacing must be reduced or the core density increased. (1 mark for correct conclusion)

**(b) Laminate Booklet (7 marks):**

The **Laminate Booklet** is the official document in which the builder records, for every structural panel of the vessel, the type, number, orientation, and thickness of plies, the materials used, and the manufacturing procedure, in a format approved by the classification society. It serves as the **design intent + as-built record** for the entire life of the vessel.

**Four Key Items it must contain:**

1. **General arrangement drawing** of the hull showing panel numbering, stiffener locations, and scantling zones. (1.75 marks)
2. **Ply-by-ply schedule** for every numbered panel, including fibre type, areal weight, orientation, resin system, and stacking sequence. (1.75 marks)
3. **Material certificates** of all resins, reinforcements, and cores used, with batch numbers and supplier traceability. (1.75 marks)
4. **Lamination procedure specification (LPS):** cure temperature, vacuum/pressure schedule, debulking intervals, post-cure cycle, and QA inspection points. (1.75 marks)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Classification society's primary job** is to define the **minimum safe laminate** — not to optimize it. A boat built thinner than the rule is **uncertifiable and uninsurable**.
- The governing standard for small craft is **ISO 12215-5**; for larger vessels, **DNV, Lloyd's, BV, RINA, ABS** rules apply.
- The **lamination schedule** is the output; the **design pressure $p_d$** is the input; the **knockdown factor $\eta$** and **material factor $k$** translate lab data to in-service reality.
- **Slamming pressure** is the dominant load on forward bottom panels; **static hydrostatic pressure** dominates amidships and aft.
- **Sandwich construction** is preferred wherever the unsupported panel span exceeds ~400 mm — it gives the **biggest weight saving per kg of stiffness added**.
- **Three mandatory checks for sandwich:** skin strength, core shear, **and** skin wrinkling/buckling. Missing one is a recurring exam deduction.
- The **cubic thickness equation** ($t^3 \propto l^4$) means a **doubling of stiffener spacing quadruples the required thickness** — this is why stiffener design is tightly coupled to lamination.
- The **ply count** must be rounded **up**, never down. A fractional ply is a build error.
- **Knockdown factor** is a *multiplier on the property*, not a *divisor on the load* — getting the sign wrong is a common mark-loss.
- Every classification rule finally boils down to: $\text{Required} \leq \text{Actual} / (\text{safety factors})$, then re-check **deflection**, **local buckling**, and **fatigue**.
- Always submit the final **Laminate Booklet** for the **Hull Construction Certificate (HCC)** — without HCC, the boat cannot be insured for commercial operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** When a question gives you a single-skin thickness result that is greater than ~6 mm, **comment** that the practical solution is to switch to sandwich construction. Examiners reward engineering judgment, not blind calculation.
